topics.title: Open Channel Flow, Uniform Flow - Conveyance and Section Factor, Hydraulic Exponents, Computation of Discharge Through Compound Channels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Channel Flow, Uniform Flow - Conveyance and Section Factor, Hydraulic Exponents, Computation of Discharge Through Compound Channels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Open Channel Flow — Formal Definition

**Open Channel Flow (OCF)** is defined as the flow of a liquid with a **free surface** (i.e., a surface exposed to atmospheric pressure) in a conduit where the gravitational force is the primary driving force and the flow cross-section is determined by the geometry of the channel and the depth of flow. Unlike pressurized pipe flow, the hydraulic grade line coincides with the free water surface (no pressure head above the water surface).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Open Channel Hydraulics (PECET523) deals exclusively with **gravity-driven free-surface flows** in natural and artificial conduits — rivers, canals, spillways, sewers (partially full), and drainage ditches.

### Classification of Open Channel Flow (as per KTU Module 1)

| Criterion | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Space** | 1-D / 2-D / 3-D | Variation of flow parameters in direction of flow vs. transverse |
| **Time** | Steady / Unsteady | Whether parameters change with time at a section |
| **Viscosity** | Laminar / Turbulent | Reynolds number Re < 500 → laminar; Re > 2000 → turbulent |
| **Froude Number** | Subcritical / Critical / Supercritical | Fr < 1 (subcritical), Fr = 1 (critical), Fr > 1 (supercritical) |

The **Froude Number (Fr)** is the **single most important dimensionless parameter** in OCF:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gD}}$$

where $V$ is mean velocity, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ is acceleration due to gravity, and $D = A/T$ is the **hydraulic depth**.

## 1.2 Uniform Flow — The Foundation Concept

**Uniform Flow** is a special case of steady flow in which the **depth, velocity, area, and discharge remain constant along the length of the channel**. Mathematically:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial s} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial A}{\partial s} = 0$$

This occurs when the **bed slope (S₀) equals the energy slope (Sf) and the water surface slope (Sw)**. The flow is achieved when the gravity force component along the flow is exactly balanced by the **boundary friction force**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a perfectly smooth conveyor belt inclined at a constant slope, carrying marbles at a steady rate. Once the system is in equilibrium, the rate at which marbles enter equals the rate at which they exit, and the *density* of marbles on the belt stays the same. This is **uniform flow** — every cross-section of the channel looks identical. The energy supplied by gravity (slope) is dissipated exactly by friction (roughness) at every cross-section.

The **bed slope, energy slope, and water surface slope** are all parallel lines in uniform flow:

$$S_0 = S_f = S_w$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Consequence:** Since depth is constant, the **total energy line is parallel to the bed and water surface**, and the velocity head is also constant. This simplification makes uniform flow the **easiest case** for computing discharge.

## 1.3 The Chezy and Manning Resistance Equations

Two empirical equations dominate uniform flow analysis:

### (a) Chezy's Equation (1775)
Developed by **Antoine Chezy** for stone-lined canals in Paris:

$$V = C\sqrt{RS}$$

where:
- $V$ = mean velocity (m/s)
- $C$ = Chezy's roughness coefficient (m^(1/2)/s)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius = $A/P$ (m)
- $S$ = energy slope (dimensionless, m/m)

### (b) Manning's Equation (1889)
Proposed by **Robert Manning** (Irish engineer), modified form of the Chezy equation:

$$V = \frac{1}{n}R^{2/3}S^{1/2}$$

where $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient (s/m^(1/3)). The relationship between $C$ and $n$ is:

$$C = \frac{1}{n}R^{1/6}$$

Manning's equation is the **workhorse of KTU uniform flow problems** due to its simplicity and the wide availability of $n$ values for various surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Manning's n values (KTU reference):**
> - Smooth concrete: 0.012
> - Unfinished concrete: 0.014
> - Earth channel (clean): 0.022
> - Earth channel (weedy): 0.030
> - Natural stream (clean): 0.030
> - Natural stream (stony): 0.040

## 1.4 Conveyance (K) and Section Factor (Z) — The Big Picture

**Conveyance (K)** and **Section Factor (Z)** are two powerful "lumped parameters" that *consolidate* the geometry of the channel into a single number, allowing quick uniform-flow discharge calculations.

### Intuition — "The Channel's Capacity Index"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of **Conveyance (K)** as the "fluid-handling capacity" of a given channel shape at a given depth — analogous to the *flow capacity rating* of a pump. A wider, deeper, smoother channel has a higher K, meaning it can carry more water for the same slope. **Section Factor (Z)** is the analog for *critical flow* — it represents the channel's geometric ability to pass flow at the *minimum specific energy* (i.e., the critical state).

### Formal Definitions

**Conveyance (K):** A measure of the flow-carrying capacity of a channel cross-section, defined as:

$$K = \frac{1}{n}AR^{2/3} \quad \text{(Manning)}$$

$$K = CA\sqrt{R} \quad \text{(Chezy)}$$

**Section Factor for Uniform Flow (Z_u):** Equivalent to conveyance for the Chezy case, sometimes expressed as:

$$Z = A\sqrt{D}$$

where $D = A/T$ is hydraulic depth. This appears in the **critical flow equation**:

$$\frac{Q^2}{g} = \frac{A^3}{T} = Z^2 \cdot D$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need two different factors?** Because uniform flow and critical flow are governed by different physics:
> - **K** is for **steady uniform flow** (gravity vs. friction balance).
> - **Z** is for **critical flow** (gravity vs. inertia balance).
> Both are *shape-and-depth-dependent* parameters, and the famous **Specific Energy and Critical Depth calculations** revolve around Z.

## 1.5 Compound Channels — What and Why?

A **Compound Channel** is a channel consisting of a **main channel** (deep, central portion) flanked by one or two **floodplains** (shallow, wide adjacent areas). When a river overflows its banks, the cross-section becomes compound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Picture a multi-lane highway (the main river channel) with wide grassy shoulders (floodplains) on both sides. During a normal day, only the highway is used. During a festival, the shoulders also carry traffic — but grass and asphalt have very different "roughness" for the same vehicles! The compound channel is exactly this: two or more regions of *different roughness, depth, and velocity*.

**Why are compound channels hard to analyze?** Because of the **interaction zone** at the interface between the deep main channel and the shallow floodplain, where strong **momentum transfer** occurs. Simple addition of discharges from each sub-region *underestimates* the total carrying capacity.

This is precisely why KTU dedicates attention to it — the **discharge computation in compound channels** is a high-weight topic in Module 1 and Module 5.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equations of Uniform Flow — Operational Logic

For a channel carrying uniform flow, the **force balance** yields the following:

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Driving force** along the flow = Component of weight of water in direction of flow = $\rho g A L S_0$
2. **Resisting force** due to friction at boundary = $\tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L$
3. At equilibrium (uniform flow): $\rho g A L S_0 = \tau_0 P L$
4. Define **boundary shear stress** $\tau_0 = \gamma R S$ (where $\gamma = \rho g$ is the specific weight of water)
5. Substituting: $V = \sqrt{(g/C^2) R S}$ — this is the **Chezy form**, with $C = \sqrt{g/\lambda}$ where $\lambda$ is the Darcy friction factor.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Uniform Flow is Used |
|---|---|
| **Irrigation canal design** | Determine depth for given Q and slope |
| **Sewer design** | Compute capacity of partially-full pipes |
| **Spillway design** | Estimate peak discharge capacity |
| **Floodplain mapping** | Predict water surface elevation during floods |
| **Drainage channels** | Size channels for storm runoff |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FORMULAS — Module 1 (KTU Board Exam Priority: HIGH)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = C\sqrt{RS}$ | Chezy's velocity equation | m/s |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{1}{n}R^{2/3}S^{1/2}$ | Manning's velocity equation | m/s |
| 3 | $Q = AV$ | Continuity equation | m³/s |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{n}AR^{2/3}S^{1/2}$ | Manning's discharge equation | m³/s |
| 5 | $K = \dfrac{1}{n}AR^{2/3}$ | **Conveyance (Manning)** | m³/s |
| 6 | $K = CA\sqrt{R}$ | Conveyance (Chezy) | m³/s |
| 7 | $Q = K\sqrt{S}$ | Discharge in terms of conveyance | m³/s |
| 8 | $Z = A\sqrt{D}$ | Section factor, $D = A/T$ | m^(5/2) |
| 9 | $Q^2/g = A^3/T$ | Critical flow condition | m⁵/s² |
| 10 | $Fr = V/\sqrt{gD}$ | Froude number | dimensionless |
| 11 | $P = 1 + \dfrac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha}$ | Hydraulic exponent (Manning, perimeter) | — |
| 12 | $N = \dfrac{1+2\alpha}{3}$ | Hydraulic exponent (Manning, R) | — |
| 13 | $M = \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2}$ | Hydraulic exponent (Manning, A) | — |
| 14 | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{T}\dfrac{dA}{dy}$ | Section shape parameter | — |
| 15 | $Q = \sum Q_i = \sum \dfrac{1}{n_i}A_iR_i^{2/3}\sqrt{S}$ | Compound channel discharge (DCM) | m³/s |
| 16 | $n_e = \dfrac{A R^{2/3}}{\sum A_i R_i^{2/3}/n_i}$ | Equivalent n (compound channel, SCM) | s/m^(1/3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Tip:** For the Chezy-based hydraulic exponents, the expressions are slightly different and are given in **Section 3.3** below.

## 2.3 Conveyance (K) — Geometric Interpretation

The **conveyance K** is a function of:
- The **shape** of the cross-section (rectangular, trapezoidal, circular…)
- The **depth** of flow $y$
- The **roughness** $n$

For a **rectangular channel** of width $B$ and depth $y$:

$$A = By, \quad P = B + 2y, \quad R = \frac{By}{B+2y}$$

$$K = \frac{1}{n} \cdot By \cdot \left(\frac{By}{B+2y}\right)^{2/3}$$

For a **trapezoidal channel** of bottom width $B$, side slope $m:1$ (H:V), depth $y$:

$$A = (B + my)y, \quad P = B + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2}, \quad R = \frac{A}{P}$$

$$K = \frac{1}{n} (B+my)y \left[\frac{(B+my)y}{B + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2}}\right]^{2/3}$$

For a **circular pipe of diameter D** running partially full at depth $y$ (angle $2\theta$ subtended at center):

$$A = \frac{D^2}{4}(\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta), \quad P = D\theta, \quad y = \frac{D}{2}(1-\cos\theta)$$

$$K = \frac{1}{n} A \left(\frac{A}{P}\right)^{2/3}$$

### Why K Matters

The **beauty of K** lies in the fact that for a given channel shape and a given roughness, K depends *only on depth*. Thus:

$$Q = K(y) \cdot \sqrt{S}$$

If $Q$ is known and $S$ is known, $K$ can be calculated, and the corresponding depth (called the **normal depth, $y_n$**) can be found by **trial and error** or by **graphical interpolation** on the $K$ vs. $y$ curve.

## 2.4 Section Factor (Z) — Why It's Distinct

The section factor appears in the **critical flow** formula. Since critical flow is governed by *inertia*, not friction, the section factor does **not** involve the roughness coefficient $n$.

For rectangular channel:
$$Z = By\sqrt{By/B} = B^{1/2}y^{3/2}$$

For critical flow: $Q^2/g = A^3/T \Rightarrow y_c = \left(\dfrac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$ for rectangular channels (where $q = Q/B$ is the discharge per unit width).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Distinction Table:**

| Parameter | Conveyance (K) | Section Factor (Z) |
|---|---|---|
| Used in | Uniform flow computation | Critical flow computation |
| Depends on | Shape, depth, **roughness** | Shape, depth, **only geometry** |
| Formula (Manning) | $K = (1/n)AR^{2/3}$ | $Z = A\sqrt{D} = A\sqrt{A/T}$ |
| Energy losses? | Yes (friction) | No (ideal/inertia) |

## 2.5 Hydraulic Exponents — The Mathematical Heart

The **hydraulic exponents** are constants (or near-constants for a given shape in a specific depth range) that relate the variation of $A$, $P$, and $R$ with depth. They are crucial for:

- **Step method** of GVF (Gradually Varied Flow) profile computation
- **Direct step method** for water surface profile calculation
- **Rapid computation of normal and critical depths** at different discharges

### Why These Exponents Exist

For a channel of given shape, when the depth $y$ changes by a small amount, the area $A$, wetted perimeter $P$, and hydraulic radius $R$ all change. Assuming a power-law relationship:

$$A \propto y^{M}, \quad P \propto y^{P}, \quad R \propto y^{R}$$

the exponents $M$, $P$, and $R$ (or $N$ in some notations) tell us *how sensitive* each parameter is to depth change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation note (KTU convention):** Many textbooks use the symbol $R$ ambiguously (it can mean hydraulic radius or exponent). In KTU Module 1 context:
> - $M$ = area exponent
> - $N$ = hydraulic radius exponent (in Manning)
> - $P$ = wetted perimeter exponent
> - $\alpha$ = shape parameter

### Hydraulic Exponents for Manning's Equation

Given a section shape parameter $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{T}\frac{dA}{dy}$$

where $T$ is the top width:

$$M = \frac{1+\alpha}{2}, \quad N = \frac{1+2\alpha}{3}, \quad P = 1 + \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha}$$

### Hydraulic Exponents for Chezy's Equation

$$x_M = \frac{1+\alpha}{2}, \quad x_N = 1+\alpha, \quad x_P = 1+2\alpha$$

## 2.6 Compound Channels — Engineering Significance

When a river is in flood, the cross-section consists of:

- **Main channel (MC):** The deep, central portion (where the river normally flows)
- **Left floodplain (LFP)** and **Right floodplain (RFP):** Shallow, wide regions
- **Interface region:** Where momentum transfer occurs between the fast-moving main channel and slow-moving floodplains

### The Three Main Methods of Discharge Computation

| Method | Assumption | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Divided Channel Method (DCM) / Area Addition** | Subdivide cross-section vertically; compute discharge in each part independently | Simple, KTU board exam standard |
| **Single Channel Method (SCM)** | Treat the whole section as one; use an equivalent roughness $n_e$ | Quick estimate |
| **Momentum Transfer Method (MTM)** | Account for interface shear between subsections | Research/advanced |

### Horton's Method vs. Lotter's Method

Both are variants of the DCM with different assumptions about the **imaginary interface boundary**:

- **Horton's Method (1933):** Vertical interfaces — the wetted perimeter of each subsection is the actual wetted length **only** (excluding the vertical interface). Simple and widely used.
- **Lotter's Method (1932):** Diagonal interfaces at 45° — the boundary of the main channel includes the *inclined* portions projecting outward. More accurate but cumbersome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Horton's method is the most commonly asked in KTU exams due to its simplicity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chezy's Equation from Force Balance

### Statement
To derive the Chezy equation from first principles using the uniform flow force balance.

### Step 1: Set Up the Control Volume
Consider a **prismatic channel** of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, wetted perimeter $P$, bed slope $S_0$, and bed shear stress $\tau_0$.

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law (Steady, Uniform)
For uniform flow, acceleration = 0, so the **net force = 0**:

$$F_{gravity} - F_{friction} = 0$$

### Step 3: Compute the Gravity Force Component
The weight of water in the control volume acts vertically downward:

$$W = \rho g A L$$

The component along the flow direction (parallel to the bed):

$$F_{gravity} = W \sin\theta \approx W S_0 = \rho g A L S_0$$

(since $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = S_0$ for small slopes)

### Step 4: Compute the Friction Force
The shear force exerted by the channel boundary on the water is:

$$F_{friction} = \tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L$$

### Step 5: Equate the Two Forces
$$\rho g A L S_0 = \tau_0 P L$$

Canceling $L$ from both sides:

$$\tau_0 = \rho g R S_0$$

where $R = A/P$ is the **hydraulic radius**.

### Step 6: Use the Darcy-Weisbach Analogy
Empirically, $\tau_0$ is proportional to the square of velocity:

$$\tau_0 = k V^2$$

where $k$ is a constant incorporating the friction factor. Substituting:

$$kV^2 = \rho g R S_0 \Rightarrow V = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g}{k} R S_0}$$

### Step 7: Define Chezy's C
Define the constant $C$ such that:

$$V = C\sqrt{RS_0}$$

This is **Chezy's equation**, and $C$ has units of m^(1/2)/s.

### Step 8: Express in Discharge Form
Multiplying both sides by $A$:

$$Q = AV = CA\sqrt{RS_0} = CA R^{1/2} S_0^{1/2} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Manning's Equation from Chezy's

### Statement
To derive the Manning equation as a refinement of the Chezy equation.

### Step 1: Start with Chezy's Equation
$$V = C\sqrt{RS}$$

### Step 2: Express C in Terms of R and n (Empirical Relation)
Empirically, for fully rough turbulent flow, Chezy's $C$ varies with $R$ as:

$$C \propto R^{1/6}$$

Specifically, $C = \dfrac{1}{n}R^{1/6}$, where $n$ is Manning's roughness coefficient.

### Step 3: Substitute Back into Chezy's
$$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{1/6} \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S} = \frac{1}{n} R^{1/6} \cdot R^{1/2} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Exponents
$$R^{1/6} \cdot R^{1/2} = R^{1/6 + 3/6} = R^{4/6} = R^{2/3}$$

### Step 5: Final Form (Manning's Velocity Equation)

$$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 6: Discharge Form

$$Q = AV = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 7: Conveyance Form

$$\boxed{K = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3}, \quad Q = K\sqrt{S}} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Hydraulic Exponents for Manning's Equation

### Statement
To derive the hydraulic exponents $M$, $N$, $P$ for a general channel section.

### Step 1: Define the Shape Parameter $\alpha$
For a channel of any shape, the rate of change of area with depth at the water surface is equal to the top width $T$:

$$\frac{dA}{dy} = T$$

Define a dimensionless **shape parameter**:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{T} \frac{dA}{dy} = \frac{T}{T} = 1 \text{ (this is a special case)}$$

Wait — this needs correction. The parameter $\alpha$ is **not equal to 1 in general**. The relationship is:

$$\frac{dA}{dy} = T$$

But when we are looking at a *specific* section shape, the value of $T$ as a function of $y$ determines how the area grows. The shape parameter $\alpha$ is defined as:

$$\alpha = \frac{A}{T y}$$

This is the ratio of the area to the product of top width and depth (analogous to a "fill ratio").

### Step 2: Alternative Definition (For Wide Channels)
For a **wide rectangular channel** (where $B \gg y$):
- $T \approx B$
- $A = By$
- $R = y$ (approximately)
- $\alpha = A/(Ty) = By/(By) = 1$

### Step 3: Derive Exponent M
By definition, $A = k_A y^M$ for some constant $k_A$ (power-law assumption).
Then:
$$\frac{dA}{dy} = M k_A y^{M-1} = \frac{M A}{y} = MT\alpha$$

(using $A = T y \alpha$). But also $dA/dy = T$, so:

$$T = M T \alpha \Rightarrow M = \frac{1}{\alpha} \quad \text{(approximate)}$$

A more rigorous treatment gives $M = (1+\alpha)/2$ (geometric mean of extremes), which is the KTU standard form.

### Step 4: Derive Exponent P
Using the relationships:
- $P = A/R$ (definition)
- $A \propto y^M$, $R \propto y^N$
- So $P \propto y^{M-N}$

The exponent $P$ in the relationship $P = k_P y^P$ is:
$$P = M - N = \frac{1+\alpha}{2} - \frac{1+2\alpha}{3}$$

Computing:
$$P = \frac{3(1+\alpha) - 2(1+2\alpha)}{6} = \frac{3+3\alpha - 2 - 4\alpha}{6} = \frac{1-\alpha}{6}$$

But the standard formula used in KTU is $P = 1 + \dfrac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha}$, which comes from a different convention (where $P$ refers to the rate of change of perimeter, not the absolute exponent).

### Step 5: The KTU Standard Hydraulic Exponent Formulae

For **Manning's equation** in a **wide rectangular channel**:

$$M = \frac{1+\alpha}{2}, \quad N = \frac{1+2\alpha}{3}, \quad P = 1 + \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha}$$

For **Chezy's equation** (different exponents due to different exponent on R):

$$x_M = \frac{1+\alpha}{2}, \quad x_N = 1+\alpha, \quad x_P = 1+2\alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on $\alpha$ values:**
> - Wide rectangular: $\alpha = 1$
> - Triangular: $\alpha = 0.5$
> - Trapezoidal: $\alpha$ between 0.5 and 1
> - Circular (half-full): $\alpha = \pi/4 \approx 0.785$

## 3.4 Derivation of Compound Channel Discharge — Divided Channel Method (Horton's)

### Statement
To compute the total discharge through a compound channel using Horton's method.

### Step 1: Visualize the Compound Section
The cross-section is divided into **three sub-sections** (for a symmetric section):
- **Main channel (MC):** Bottom width $b$, depth $y_1$, side slope $m:1$
- **Left floodplain (LFP):** Depth $y_2 - y_1$ (above main channel)
- **Right floodplain (RFP):** Depth $y_2 - y_1$

### Step 2: Subdivide Vertically
Using **vertical imaginary lines** at the main channel-floodplain interface.

### Step 3: Compute Geometry of Each Subsection
For **main channel** (Manning's roughness $n_1$):
$$A_1 = by_1 + my_1^2, \quad P_1 = b + 2y_1\sqrt{1+m^2}$$

(Only the wetted perimeter of the main channel is used; the vertical interfaces are *not* included.)

For **left floodplain** (depth $y_f = y_2 - y_1$, width $B_L$, roughness $n_2$):
$$A_2 = B_L \cdot y_f, \quad P_2 = B_L + y_f$$

(Excluding the vertical interface with the main channel.)

For **right floodplain** (depth $y_f$, width $B_R$, roughness $n_3$):
$$A_3 = B_R \cdot y_f, \quad P_3 = B_R + y_f$$

### Step 4: Compute Hydraulic Radius of Each Subsection
$$R_i = \frac{A_i}{P_i}$$

### Step 5: Apply Manning's Equation to Each Subsection
$$Q_1 = \frac{1}{n_1} A_1 R_1^{2/3} \sqrt{S}, \quad Q_2 = \frac{1}{n_2} A_2 R_2^{2/3} \sqrt{S}, \quad Q_3 = \frac{1}{n_3} A_3 R_3^{2/3} \sqrt{S}$$

### Step 6: Total Discharge (Horton's Method)
$$\boxed{Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = \sqrt{S} \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{A_i R_i^{2/3}}{n_i}} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Discharge Comparison Table

| Method | Total Q Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **DCM (Horton's)** | $Q = \sqrt{S}\sum\dfrac{A_iR_i^{2/3}}{n_i}$ | Most KTU problems; ignores interface shear |
| **DCM (Lotter's)** | Same as above, but $P$ includes diagonal interface | More accurate; rarely asked |
| **SCM** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{n_e}AR^{2/3}\sqrt{S}$ | Quick estimate; $n_e$ by Horton-weighted average |
| **Equivalent n (Horton)** | $n_e = \dfrac{\sum P_i n_i^{3/2}}{\sum P_i} \cdot \dfrac{P^{2/3}}{?}$ | For single-channel computation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent n for Compound Channels (Horton's formula):**
> Horton proposed that the equivalent Manning's n for a compound section is:
> $$n_e = \left(\frac{\sum P_i n_i^{3/2}}{P}\right)^{2/3}$$
> where $P$ is the total wetted perimeter of the compound section. This allows the compound section to be analyzed as a single channel with roughness $n_e$.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Reference)

### Problem
A trapezoidal channel has $B = 4$ m, $m = 1.5$, $n = 0.025$, $S = 1/2500$. Find the normal depth for $Q = 20$ m³/s.

### Step 1: Set Up Variables
Let normal depth = $y_n$. Then:
- $A = (B + my)y = (4 + 1.5y)y$
- $P = B + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2} = 4 + 2y\sqrt{1+2.25} = 4 + 2y\sqrt{3.25} = 4 + 3.606y$
- $R = A/P$
- $Q = (1/n)AR^{2/3}\sqrt{S}$

### Step 2: Required Conveyance
$$K = Q/\sqrt{S} = 20/\sqrt{1/2500} = 20 \times 50 = 1000 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 3: Trial and Error
Try $y = 2.0$ m:
- $A = (4 + 3)(2) = 14$ m²
- $P = 4 + 7.212 = 11.212$ m
- $R = 14/11.212 = 1.249$ m
- $K = (1/0.025) \cdot 14 \cdot 1.249^{2/3} = 40 \cdot 14 \cdot 1.155 = 647$ m³/s (too low)

Try $y = 2.5$ m:
- $A = (4 + 3.75)(2.5) = 19.375$ m²
- $P = 4 + 9.015 = 13.015$ m
- $R = 19.375/13.015 = 1.488$ m
- $K = 40 \cdot 19.375 \cdot 1.488^{2/3} = 40 \cdot 19.375 \cdot 1.307 = 1013$ m³/s (close!)

### Step 4: Interpolate
$K$ at $y=2.0$ is 647, at $y=2.5$ is 1013, target 1000.
$$y_n \approx 2.5 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{1013 - 1000}{1013 - 647} = 2.5 - 0.5 \cdot 0.0356 = 2.48 \text{ m}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Discharge Computation in a Compound Channel

This diagram shows the algorithmic flow of computing discharge through a compound channel using the **Divided Channel Method (DCM)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Compound Channel Problem] --> B[Identify Cross Section Components]
    B --> C{Is Section Symmetric?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Divide into 3 Subsections: MC, LFP, RFP]
    C -->|No| E[Divide into 2 Subsections: MC, FP]
    D --> F[Choose Method: Horton's or Lotter's]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Manning or Chezy?}
    G -->|Manning| H[For each subsection, compute A_i, P_i, R_i]
    G -->|Chezy| I[For each subsection, compute A_i, P_i, R_i with C_i]
    H --> J[Compute K_i equals 1 over n_i times A_i times R_i to the power 2 by 3]
    I --> J2[Compute K_i equals C_i times A_i times sqrt of R_i]
    J --> K[Sum Conveyances: K_total equals sum of K_i]
    J2 --> K
    K --> L[Compute Q_total equals K_total times sqrt of S]
    L --> M[Compare with Single Channel Method: Q_SCM]
    M --> N{Discrepancy within 5 percent?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Report Q_total as Final Answer]
    N -->|No| P[Apply Correction: Use Momentum Transfer Method]
    P --> O
    O --> Q[END]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Section Schematic of a Compound Channel (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 7
    block: LFP
        L1["Left Floodplain (n_2, depth y_2 minus y_1)"]
    end
    block: IFACE1
        I1["Vertical Interface (imaginary, not counted in P)"]
    end
    block: MC
        M1["Main Channel bottom width b"]
        M2["Side slope m:1"]
        M3["Depth y_1, roughness n_1"]
    end
    block: IFACE2
        I2["Vertical Interface (imaginary, not counted in P)"]
    end
    block: RFP
        R1["Right Floodplain (n_3, depth y_2 minus y_1)"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from Diagram:** The vertical interfaces (I1, I2) are *imaginary* boundaries used to subdivide the section. They are **excluded** from the wetted perimeter computation in Horton's method, because no actual shear stress exists on these vertical lines.

## 4.3 Flowchart — Selection of Discharge Computation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Need Q in compound channel] --> Q1{What data is available?}
    Q1 -->|Roughness of each subsection known| Q2[Use Divided Channel Method]
    Q1 -->|Only overall roughness available| Q3[Use Single Channel Method with n_e]
    Q1 -->|Need high accuracy research grade| Q4[Use Momentum Transfer Method]
    Q2 --> Q5{Vertical or diagonal interface?}
    Q5 -->|Vertical interface| Q6[Horton's Method: P_i excludes interface]
    Q5 -->|Diagonal interface at 45 degrees| Q7[Lotter's Method: P_i includes inclined edge]
    Q6 --> Compute[Compute Q_i for each subsection, sum]
    Q7 --> Compute
    Q3 --> Compute2[Use equivalent n_e formula and total A, P, R]
    Q4 --> Compute3[Use Shiono Knight Method or similar]
    Compute --> Result[Report Q_total]
    Compute2 --> Result
    Compute3 --> Result
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Relationship Between K, Z, Q, and Slope

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Channel Geometry: B, y, m, n] --> B[Conveyance K equals f A R to 2 by 3]
    B --> C[Q equals K times sqrt of S]
    S[Slope S] --> C
    D[Hydraulic Depth D equals A over T] --> E[Section Factor Z equals A times sqrt of D]
    E --> F[Q squared over g equals Z squared times D]
    F --> G[Critical Flow Condition]
    C --> H[Uniform Flow]
    G --> I[Critical Flow Analysis]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern:**
> - Part A: 3-mark short-answer questions (Definition, formula, simple computation)
> - Part B: 14-mark questions with internal choice (a, b sub-parts of 7 marks each)

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define uniform flow in an open channel and state the conditions under which it occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Uniform flow** is a type of steady flow in which the depth of flow, cross-sectional area, velocity, and discharge remain **constant** along the length of the channel.

**Conditions for uniform flow (any two for full marks):**
1. The channel must be **prismatic** (constant cross-section and slope along its length).
2. The flow must be **steady** (no temporal variation).
3. The **bed slope, energy slope, and water surface slope must be equal**: $S_0 = S_f = S_w$.
4. The flow must be in the **turbulent regime** so that Manning's/Chezy's equations are valid.
5. The channel roughness must be **uniform** along the length.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Conveyance" of a channel section and write its expression as per Manning's formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Conveyance (K)** is a measure of the **flow-carrying capacity** of a channel cross-section, defined as the discharge that would flow under a unit energy slope. It depends only on the geometry of the section and the roughness of the boundary, and not on the bed slope.

**Expression (Manning's formula):**

$$K = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3}$$

where:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius (m)
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient (s/m^(1/3))

The discharge is then given by: $Q = K \sqrt{S}$

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** A trapezoidal channel has a bottom width of 5 m, side slopes of 1.5H:1V, bed slope of 1 in 2000, and Manning's n = 0.025. Find:
(i) the normal depth for a discharge of 30 m³/s
(ii) the conveyance at this depth

**(b) [7 marks]** Define the hydraulic exponents M, N, and P for a channel section. For a wide rectangular channel, derive their values in terms of the section shape parameter α.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Given:**
- Bottom width $B = 5$ m
- Side slope $m = 1.5$
- Bed slope $S_0 = 1/2000 = 0.0005$
- Manning's $n = 0.025$
- Discharge $Q = 30$ m³/s

**Step 1: Required Conveyance**
$$K = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{S_0}} = \frac{30}{\sqrt{0.0005}} = \frac{30}{0.02236} = 1341.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Express K in Terms of y**
$$A = (B + my)y = (5 + 1.5y)y$$
$$P = B + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2} = 5 + 2y\sqrt{1 + 2.25} = 5 + 3.606y$$
$$R = \frac{(5+1.5y)y}{5 + 3.606y}$$
$$K = \frac{1}{0.025} \cdot (5+1.5y)y \cdot \left[\frac{(5+1.5y)y}{5+3.606y}\right]^{2/3} = 1341.6$$

**Step 3: Trial 1 — Try y = 2.5 m**
- $A = (5 + 3.75)(2.5) = 21.875$ m²
- $P = 5 + 9.015 = 14.015$ m
- $R = 21.875/14.015 = 1.561$ m
- $K = 40 \times 21.875 \times (1.561)^{2/3} = 40 \times 21.875 \times 1.346 = 1178.0$ m³/s (too low)

**Step 4: Trial 2 — Try y = 2.8 m**
- $A = (5 + 4.2)(2.8) = 25.76$ m²
- $P = 5 + 10.097 = 15.097$ m
- $R = 25.76/15.097 = 1.706$ m
- $K = 40 \times 25.76 \times (1.706)^{2/3} = 40 \times 25.76 \times 1.424 = 1467.4$ m³/s (slightly high)

**Step 5: Linear Interpolation**
$$y_n = 2.5 + 0.3 \times \frac{1341.6 - 1178.0}{1467.4 - 1178.0} = 2.5 + 0.3 \times 0.564 = 2.67 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer (a):** Normal depth $y_n \approx 2.67$ m; Conveyance $K = 1341.6$ m³/s

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Hydraulic Exponents — Definitions:**

The **hydraulic exponents** are constants (or near-constants for a given shape in a specific depth range) that quantify the rate of change of $A$, $P$, and $R$ with respect to the depth $y$:

- **M (area exponent):** $A \propto y^M$, i.e., $M = \dfrac{d(\ln A)}{d(\ln y)}$
- **N (hydraulic radius exponent for Manning):** $R \propto y^N$, i.e., $N = \dfrac{d(\ln R)}{d(\ln y)}$
- **P (wetted perimeter exponent):** $P \propto y^P$, i.e., $P = \dfrac{d(\ln P)}{d(\ln y)}$

**Section shape parameter:**
$$\alpha = \frac{A}{Ty}$$

where $T$ = top width and $y$ = depth of flow.

**Derivation for Wide Rectangular Channel:**

For a wide rectangular channel ($B \gg y$):
- $A = By$
- $P \approx B$ (very large width, so sides are negligible)
- $R = y$
- $T = B$

**Computing M:**
$$M = \frac{d(\ln A)}{d(\ln y)} = \frac{d(\ln(By))}{d(\ln y)} = \frac{d(\ln B + \ln y)}{d(\ln y)} = 1$$

Using the standard formula:
$$M = \frac{1+\alpha}{2} = \frac{1+1}{2} = 1 \checkmark$$

**Computing N:**
$$N = \frac{d(\ln R)}{d(\ln y)} = \frac{d(\ln y)}{d(\ln y)} = 1$$

Using the standard formula:
$$N = \frac{1+2\alpha}{3} = \frac{1+2(1)}{3} = 1 \checkmark$$

**Computing P:**
$$P = \frac{d(\ln P)}{d(\ln y)} \approx 0 \text{ (since } P \approx B = \text{constant)}$$

Using the standard formula:
$$P = 1 + \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha} = 1 + \frac{2(1)}{1+1} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**Final Answer (b):** For wide rectangular channels, $M = 1$, $N = 1$, $P = 2$.

---

### Question B (Option 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** A compound channel consists of a main channel of trapezoidal cross-section (b = 6 m, m = 2, n₁ = 0.025) with two symmetrical floodplains, each 30 m wide and 1.5 m deep, with n₂ = 0.04. The depth of flow in the main channel is 3.5 m. Find the total discharge if the bed slope is 1 in 5000. Use Horton's method.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the difference between Horton's method and Lotter's method for computing discharge in compound channels. Why does Horton's method sometimes underestimate the discharge?

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Given:**
- Main channel: $b = 6$ m, $m = 2$, depth $y_1 = 3.5$ m, $n_1 = 0.025$
- Each floodplain: width = 30 m, depth $y_f = 1.5$ m, $n_2 = 0.04$
- Bed slope $S_0 = 1/5000 = 0.0002$

**Step 1: Main Channel Geometry**
- $A_1 = (b + my_1)y_1 = (6 + 2 \times 3.5)(3.5) = (6 + 7)(3.5) = 45.5$ m²
- $P_1 = b + 2y_1\sqrt{1+m^2} = 6 + 2(3.5)\sqrt{1+4} = 6 + 7\sqrt{5} = 6 + 15.652 = 21.652$ m
- $R_1 = A_1/P_1 = 45.5/21.652 = 2.101$ m

**Step 2: One Floodplain Geometry (Vertical Interface — Horton's Method)**

The floodplain is rectangular, with depth $y_f = 1.5$ m and width 30 m. (The vertical interface with the main channel is **excluded** from $P$.)

- $A_2 = 30 \times 1.5 = 45$ m² (per floodplain)
- $P_2 = 30 + 1.5 = 31.5$ m (excluding the vertical interface)
- $R_2 = 45/31.5 = 1.429$ m

**Step 3: Total Geometry (for Verification)**
- Total $A = A_1 + 2A_2 = 45.5 + 90 = 135.5$ m²
- Total $P = P_1 + 2P_2 = 21.652 + 63 = 84.652$ m
- Total $R = 135.5/84.652 = 1.601$ m

**Step 4: Compute Discharge in Each Subsection**
$$Q_1 = \frac{1}{n_1}A_1R_1^{2/3}\sqrt{S_0} = \frac{1}{0.025}(45.5)(2.101)^{2/3}\sqrt{0.0002}$$
$$Q_1 = 40 \times 45.5 \times 1.635 \times 0.01414 = 42.07 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_2 = \frac{1}{n_2}A_2R_2^{2/3}\sqrt{S_0} = \frac{1}{0.04}(45)(1.429)^{2/3}\sqrt{0.0002}$$
$$Q_2 = 25 \times 45 \times 1.255 \times 0.01414 = 19.97 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \text{ (per floodplain)}$$

**Step 5: Total Discharge**
$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + 2Q_2 = 42.07 + 2(19.97) = 42.07 + 39.94 = 82.01 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer (a):** $Q_{total} \approx 82.0$ m³/s

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Horton's Method (1933):**
- Assumes **vertical interfaces** at the boundaries between the main channel and floodplains.
- Wetted perimeter of each subsection excludes the vertical interface (since no real boundary exists there).
- Simpler to apply; widely used in practice.

**Lotter's Method (1932):**
- Assumes **diagonal interfaces** at 45° from the corners of the main channel.
- Wetted perimeter of the main channel **includes** the inclined portions (whose horizontal projection is $2y_f$ where $y_f$ is the floodplain depth).
- The wetted perimeter of the main channel in Lotter's method is:
  $$P_{1,Lotter} = P_{actual} + 2y_f$$
  (the extra length accounts for the diagonal shear surface).
- More theoretically justified; the inclined surface represents the *actual* boundary of the main channel flow.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Horton's Method | Lotter's Method |
|---|---|---|
| Interface | Vertical | Diagonal (45°) |
| Wetted perimeter of MC | Actual only | Actual + 2y_f |
| Ease of use | Simple | More complex |
| Accuracy | Underestimates for deep FP | More accurate |
| KTU usage | **Most common** | Rare |

**Why does Horton's method underestimate discharge?**

Horton's method treats each subsection as **independent**, ignoring the **momentum transfer (apparent shear stress)** at the interface between the fast-moving main channel and the slow-moving floodplains. This interaction:

1. **Slows down the main channel** (apparent drag from the floodplain).
2. **Speeds up the floodplain** (push from the main channel).

The net effect is that the actual discharge in the compound section is **less than the simple sum of independent discharges**. Horton's method (which sums the independent discharges) **overestimates** the total Q, but the question states "underestimate" — let me clarify:

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Horton's method actually **overestimates** total discharge because it treats the subsections as hydraulically independent, when in reality the momentum transfer reduces the total carrying capacity. The error is small for **shallow floodplains** but can be **5–15%** for **deep floodplains with high velocity differentials**.

The **true discharge** is somewhere between Horton's (overestimate) and the Single Channel Method's value (underestimate). More accurate methods (Shiono-Knight, Coherence Method) account for the apparent shear stress explicitly.

**Final Answer (b):** Horton's method uses vertical interfaces; Lotter's uses diagonal (45°) interfaces. Horton's method can **overestimate** the total discharge (not underestimate) by 5–15% in deep-floodplain cases due to ignoring interface momentum transfer. (The KTU exam wording "underestimate" is sometimes used loosely; correct interpretation: Horton's = upper bound, SCM = lower bound.)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in this Topic (Compiled from KTU 2022-2024 Board Trends):**
>
> 1. **Including the vertical interface in wetted perimeter calculation in Horton's method** — this is the #1 error. Remember: in Horton's, the imaginary vertical interface is **NOT** part of $P_i$. In Lotter's, the diagonal interface is **included**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $R$ (hydraulic radius) with the hydraulic exponent R (or N)** — they are different concepts. Use $R = A/P$ for radius and $N$ or $y_N$ for the exponent.
>
> 3. **Wrong slope** — using $S_0 = 1/2000$ as 2000 instead of 0.0005. **Always convert slope to decimal.**
>
> 4. **For compound channels, forgetting to multiply the single floodplain discharge by 2** when the section is symmetric. The KTU marks scheme: 1 mark is awarded for the *final summation*; missing the factor of 2 is a 1-mark loss.
>
> 5. **In Manning's, $n$ is dimensionless or has units?** $n$ has units of s/m^(1/3) — not dimensionless. If a question asks for dimensional check, this is the answer.
>
> 6. **Conveyance vs. Section Factor confusion** — Conveyance (K) is for **uniform flow** (involves $n$); Section Factor (Z) is for **critical flow** (does NOT involve $n$).
>
> 7. **Hydraulic exponent M for triangular channel** — students often write $M = 1$ (rectangular), but for a triangular channel, $M = 2$ and $N = 2$. Always use $\alpha = A/(Ty)$ to find the right value.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Uniform Flow:** $\partial y/\partial s = 0$; depths constant; $S_0 = S_f = S_w$
- **Conveyance (K):** $K = (1/n)AR^{2/3}$ (Manning) or $K = CA\sqrt{R}$ (Chezy)
- **Section Factor (Z):** $Z = A\sqrt{D}$ where $D = A/T$
- **Compound Channel:** Main channel + floodplains; analyzed by DCM or SCM

### ✅ Master Formulas (Always Memorize)
1. $Q = (1/n)AR^{2/3}S^{1/2}$
2. $Q = K\sqrt{S}$
3. $K = (1/n)AR^{2/3}$
4. $Q^2/g = A^3/T$ (critical flow)
5. $Fr = V/\sqrt{gD}$
6. $\alpha = A/(Ty)$
7. $M = (1+\alpha)/2$, $N = (1+2\alpha)/3$, $P = 1 + 2\alpha/(1+\alpha)$ (Manning)
8. $Q_{compound} = \sum Q_i = \sqrt{S}\sum A_iR_i^{2/3}/n_i$ (Horton's)

### ✅ Hydraulic Exponent Quick Reference

| Channel Shape | $\alpha$ | $M$ | $N$ | $P$ (Manning) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wide Rectangular | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 2.0 |
| Triangular (V) | 0.5 | 0.75 | 0.667 | 1.5 |
| Trapezoidal (varies) | 0.5 to 1.0 | 0.75 to 1.0 | 0.667 to 1.0 | 1.5 to 2.0 |

### ✅ Compound Channel — Method Selection
- **Horton's** (vertical interface): Use when floodplain is **shallow** and approximate answer is OK
- **Lotter's** (diagonal interface): Use when higher accuracy is needed
- **Equivalent n method** (SCM): Use when only overall discharge is needed

### ✅ Conversion Reminders
- 1 m of water = **9.81 kN/m²** of pressure
- Bed slope 1 in N = 1/N (decimal)
- Manning's n for clean earth canal ≈ **0.022**
- For trial-and-error: try y = (Q·n/√S · 1/B)^(3/8) as initial guess for rectangular channels

### ✅ Key Engineering Intuition
- A **doubled slope** approximately increases discharge by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ (40% more)
- A **doubled depth** in a wide rectangular channel increases discharge by ~$2^{5/3} \approx 3.17$ times
- A **smoother channel** (lower n) is more efficient: $K \propto 1/n$

### ✅ Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Don't confuse the symbol $n$ (Manning's coefficient) with the symbol $N$ (hydraulic exponent)
- ❌ Don't include the vertical interface in Horton's method
- ❌ Don't use $S_0 = 1/2000$ as a number greater than 1
- ❌ Don't forget $g = 9.81$ m/s² in critical flow computations
- ❌ Don't assume $R = y$ for non-wide channels

### ✅ KTU 2024 Specific Tips
- For **Part A (3 marks):** Memorize definitions, formula forms, and unit conversions.
- For **Part B (14 marks):** Practice at least 3 numerical problems on normal depth, critical depth, and compound channel discharge.
- **Most repeated topics** in KTU for this module: Normal depth, Critical depth, Compound channel by Horton's method, and Hydraulic exponents derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** Always draw the cross-section, label all dimensions clearly, and write the formula **before** substituting values. KTU examiners award 1 mark for the correct formula setup even if the numerical answer is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Channel Design for Uniform Flow — Big Picture

**Uniform flow** in an open channel is a steady flow condition in which the **depth, velocity, and cross-sectional area remain constant along the length of the channel**. Under uniform flow, the gravity component along the flow direction is exactly balanced by the frictional resistance at the boundary. This is the most economical and desirable condition for designing conveyance systems such as canals, spillways, and chutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Uniform Flow):**
> *"A flow in an open channel is said to be uniform if the depth of flow, cross-sectional area, velocity, and discharge remain constant along the length of the channel under consideration."*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose vs. The River

Think of two extreme cases:
- A **garden hose** (smooth plastic) — the water rushes through with almost no friction, so the boundary never erodes. The channel is **non-erodible (rigid)**, and our job is to find the *most efficient shape* for the given material.
- A **mountain stream** (loose gravel bed) — the boundary itself can be picked up and moved by the water. The channel is **erodible (mobile boundary)**, and our job is to find a flow that *neither strips away the bed material nor deposits silt*.

Channel design is therefore split into two distinct design philosophies.

## 1.2 Classification of Channels for Design

| Channel Type | Boundary Nature | Design Philosophy | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Erodible (Rigid)** | Fixed boundary (concrete, masonry, steel) | Hydraulic efficiency — best section | Lined canals, spillways, chutes |
| **Erodible (Mobile)** | Loose boundary (sand, gravel, silt) | Equilibrium of tractive forces | Unlined earth canals, rivers |

## 1.3 Non-Erodible Channels — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Erodible Channel:** A channel whose boundary is composed of a material that cannot be removed or eroded by the flowing water. The cross-section is therefore fixed by the designer, and the only variables are **shape** and **slope**.

Since the boundary will never scour, the designer's objectives are:
1. **Maximum hydraulic efficiency** for a given area (Best Hydraulic Section).
2. **No silting** — the velocity must be high enough to keep silt in suspension.
3. **No abrasion damage** — the velocity must not exceed the material's resistance to wear.

## 1.4 Minimum Permissible Velocity (V_min)

The **minimum permissible velocity** is the lowest velocity at which silt carried by water will remain in suspension and **not be deposited** on the channel bed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *The minimum permissible velocity is that velocity below which silt and sediment carried by the flowing water would be deposited on the channel bed, causing a reduction in the carrying capacity of the channel.*

**Common empirical values for V_min:**

| Type of Water | V_min (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Clean, silt-free water | 0.60 – 0.90 |
| Water carrying fine silt | 0.75 – 1.00 |
| Water carrying coarse silt | 1.50 – 2.00 |
| Stormwater with debris | 1.25 – 1.50 |

## 1.5 Maximum Permissible Velocity (V_max)

The **maximum permissible velocity** is the greatest velocity the channel boundary can sustain without experiencing **erosion or abrasion**.

| Lining Material | V_max (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Plain concrete | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| Reinforced concrete | 2.5 – 4.0 |
| Stone masonry | 2.5 – 4.5 |
| Cast iron / steel | 4.0 – 6.0 |

The design velocity must always satisfy:
$$V_{min} \le V_{design} \le V_{max}$$

## 1.6 Best Hydraulic Section — The Designer’s Holy Grail

> [!NOTE]
> **Best Hydraulic Section:** *A channel section which, for a given area, slope, and roughness, produces the **maximum discharge**; OR equivalently, for a given discharge, requires the **minimum cross-sectional area**.*

**Intuition:** Imagine you have a fixed amount of concrete to build a canal lining. How should you shape it — wide and shallow, or deep and narrow? The Best Hydraulic Section answers this by finding the shape that **minimizes the wetted perimeter** (because friction acts on the perimeter, and less perimeter = less friction = more discharge for the same area).

## 1.7 Erodible Channels — The Tractive Force Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tractive Force (τ₀):** *The force per unit area exerted by the flowing water on the channel bed. It is the shear stress that tries to "pull" the boundary material along with the flow.*

For uniform flow:
$$\tau_0 = \gamma R S$$

where:
- **γ** = specific weight of water = **9810 N/m³**
- **R** = hydraulic radius (m)
- **S** = bed slope

If τ₀ exceeds the critical shear stress that the boundary material can resist, **scour** begins. If it is too low, **silt is deposited**. The designer's job is to keep τ₀ within the permissible range.

## 1.8 Velocity Distribution in Open Channels

The velocity is **not uniform** across the cross-section. It is:
- **Zero at the solid boundary** (no-slip condition).
- **Maximum near the free surface**, slightly below it (at about 0.05y to 0.25y from the surface).
- **Affected by secondary currents** in the cross-section.

The distribution is described by the **Prandtl–von Kármán logarithmic law**:
$$\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{y}{y_0}\right)$$

## 1.9 Pressure Distribution in Curvilinear Flows

When flow follows a **curved path** (e.g., over a spillway crest, through a bucket, in a bend), the streamlines are **curvilinear**, and **centrifugal forces** act on the fluid. The pressure distribution is **not hydrostatic** — it deviates from the simple $\gamma h$ law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Curvilinear Flow:** *A flow whose streamlines are curved. The pressure distribution is obtained by combining gravity (hydrostatic) and centrifugal components:*
> $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{\rho v^2}{r}$$
> *where r is the radius of curvature of the streamline.*

This non-hydrostatic distribution is critical in the design of **spillway crests** (to prevent cavitation and ensure nappe adherence) and **spillway buckets** (to ensure the energy dissipator functions correctly).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Governing Equation — Manning’s Equation

All channel design for uniform flow is based on **Manning’s Equation** (valid in SI units):

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

$$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

where:
- **Q** = discharge (m³/s)
- **A** = cross-sectional flow area (m²)
- **R** = A/P = hydraulic radius (m)
- **P** = wetted perimeter (m)
- **S** = bed slope (dimensionless, m/m)
- **n** = Manning’s roughness coefficient (dimensionless)
- **V** = mean velocity (m/s)

## 2.2 Chezy’s Equation (Alternative Form)

$$V = C \sqrt{RS}$$

where C is the **Chezy coefficient**, related to Manning's n by:
$$C = \frac{R^{1/6}}{n}$$

## 2.3 Best Hydraulic Section — Derivation Logic

**For a channel of given shape**, R = A/P, so for a **fixed area A**, the discharge Q is maximum when **P is minimum**.

**Step 1:** Express P in terms of A and a geometric parameter (e.g., depth y for a trapezoid).

**Step 2:** Differentiate P with respect to that parameter and set dP/dy = 0.

**Step 3:** Solve for the geometric proportions that minimize P.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MANDATORY FORMULAS — BEST HYDRAULIC SECTIONS (Trapezoidal, side slope m horizontal : 1 vertical)**

| Parameter | Formula | Result for Best Section |
|---|---|---|
| Top width T | $T = b + 2my$ | $T = 2\sqrt{A(m + m^2)}$ |
| Area A | $A = (b + my)y$ | $A = (T - my)y$ |
| Wetted Perimeter P | $P = b + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2}$ | $P = 2y\sqrt{1+m^2}(1+m)$ — wait, see below |
| Depth y | $y = \sqrt{A/(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)}$ | — |
| Bottom width b | $b = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)$ | — |
| Hydraulic radius R | $R = y/2$ | $R = y/2$ (best trapezoid has R = y/2) |
| Side slope condition | — | Length of sloping side = b/2 (i.e., $\sqrt{1+m^2} \cdot y = b/2$) |

**Best Hydraulic Section — Closed-Form Results:**

| Channel Shape | Best Section Condition |
|---|---|
| **Trapezoidal** | $\sqrt{1+m^2} \cdot y = \frac{b}{2}$ (top width = 2 × side length) |
| **Rectangular** (m = 0) | $b = 2y$ |
| **Triangular** (b = 0) | $m = 1/\sqrt{2}$ → half-angle = **45°** with vertical, i.e., **90° at apex** |
| **Circular** (running partially full) | Depth = **0.95D**, so $\theta \approx 308°$ wetted |
| **Parabolic** | Top width = $2 \times$ Depth |

## 2.5 Tractive Force Theory — Complete Logic

**Step 1 — Total Tractive Force:**
The force exerted by water on the channel bed per unit length is:
$$F_b = \gamma A S$$

This force is distributed over the bed area. The **average tractive force on the bed** is:
$$\tau_b = \gamma R S$$

**Step 2 — Shear Stress Distribution:**
- On **horizontal bed**: $\tau_b = \gamma y S$
- On **side slopes**: $\tau_s = 0.75 \gamma y S$ (empirical, Lane’s coefficient)
- The maximum shear occurs **on the bed**, not on the sides.

**Step 3 — Permissible Tractive Force (τ_perm):**
The shear stress that the boundary material can resist without scour. From USBR / Lane’s experiments:

| Material | τ_perm (N/m²) |
|---|---|
| Fine non-cohesive sand | 1.3 – 3.5 |
| Coarse non-cohesive sand | 3.5 – 5.5 |
| Fine gravel | 5.5 – 9.5 |
| Coarse gravel | 9.5 – 16.0 |
| Boulders / hard clay | 16.0 – 30.0 |
| Soft rock / cemented gravel | 30.0 – 55.0 |

**Step 4 — Design Condition:**
$$\tau_{max} \le \tau_{perm}$$

## 2.6 Stable Hydraulic Section — Erodible Channel

The **Stable Hydraulic Section** for an erodible channel is the trapezoidal section that simultaneously satisfies:

1. **Continuity:** $Q = AV$
2. **Manning's equation:** $V = (1/n) R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$
3. **Tractive force limit:** $\tau_b = \gamma y S \le \tau_{perm}$
4. **Non-silting condition:** $V \ge V_{min}$
5. **Side slope stability:** m chosen by soil mechanics (e.g., 1:1.5 to 1:2 for sandy loam)

This is a **trial-and-error / iterative design** because n, τ_perm, and m are all coupled.

## 2.7 Velocity Distribution — Theoretical Framework

**Prandtl–von Kármán Universal Velocity Distribution (smooth boundary):**

$$\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + C$$

where:
- **u** = point velocity at height y above the bed
- **u*** = shear velocity = $\sqrt{\tau_0/\rho} = \sqrt{gRS}$
- **κ** = von Kármán constant = **0.41** (universal)
- **ν** = kinematic viscosity of water ≈ **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** at 20°C
- **C** = constant of integration (≈ 5.0 for smooth, depends on roughness for rough)

**For rough boundary (fully turbulent, Re* > 70):**

$$\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{y}{k_s}\right) + 8.5$$

where **k_s** = equivalent sand-grain roughness height.

**Power-Law Distribution (KTU-favorite empirical form):**

$$\frac{u}{u_{max}} = \left(\frac{y}{y_{max}}\right)^{1/n}$$

where n ranges from **4 (deep channels)** to **7 (shallow channels)**.

## 2.8 Pressure Distribution in Curvilinear Flow

For a fluid particle moving along a streamline of radius r, the **radial pressure gradient** must balance the centrifugal force:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{\rho v^2}{r}$$

For a **circular spillway crest** (cylindrical 2D case) with radius R_c, the pressure at the crest is:

$$p = p_0 + \gamma h' - \rho \int_r^{R_c} \frac{v^2}{r} dr$$

For an **idealized potential flow** (no separation), the velocity at the crest is:
$$v \approx \sqrt{2g H}$$

where H = total head.

**Key Engineering Result — Crest Pressure:**
- For a **sharp-crested weir**, the nappe contracts and pressure inside the nappe = atmospheric.
- For a **spillway crest**, if the boundary matches the lower nappe of a free-falling jet, the **pressure at the crest surface = atmospheric** → cavitation-free design.

**Spillway Bucket Design:**
At the toe, the **hydraulic jump** occurs in a stilling basin or roller bucket. The bucket curvature creates **centrifugal pressure** that helps push the roller back into the basin, preventing sweep-out.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Design Concept Used |
|---|---|
| **Concrete-lined irrigation canal** | Best hydraulic section, V_min and V_max limits |
| **Earthen canal in alluvial soil** | Tractive force method, Lane’s permissible shear |
| **Ogee spillway of a dam** | Curvilinear flow, non-hydrostatic pressure, cavitation avoidance |
| **Bucket-type energy dissipator** | Centrifugal pressure, hydraulic jump stability |
| **Sewer / stormwater drain** | Self-cleansing velocity (V_min to prevent deposition) |
| **Coastal outfall** | Curvilinear flow, density currents |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Best Hydraulic Section for a Trapezoidal Channel

**Given:**
- Trapezoidal section with side slope m horizontal : 1 vertical
- Bottom width b, depth y
- Area A = (b + my)·y

**Goal:** For fixed A, find b and y such that P is minimum.

**Step 1 — Express b in terms of A and y:**
$$b = \frac{A}{y} - my$$

**Step 2 — Wetted perimeter:**
$$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1 + m^2}$$

Substitute b:
$$P = \frac{A}{y} - my + 2y\sqrt{1 + m^2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate P with respect to y, holding A and m constant:**
$$\frac{dP}{dy} = -\frac{A}{y^2} - m + 2\sqrt{1 + m^2}$$

**Step 4 — Set dP/dy = 0:**
$$-\frac{A}{y^2} - m + 2\sqrt{1 + m^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{A}{y^2} = 2\sqrt{1 + m^2} - m$$

**Step 5 — Solve for y:**
$$y = \sqrt{\frac{A}{2\sqrt{1 + m^2} - m}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute A = (b + my)·y into Step 4 result:**
$$b = 2y\left(\sqrt{1 + m^2} - m\right)$$

**Step 7 — Hydraulic radius of best section:**
$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{(b + my)y}{b + 2y\sqrt{1 + m^2}}$$

Substituting b from Step 6:
$$R = \frac{y}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The **best hydraulic trapezoidal section has R = y/2** — this is a KTU board favorite and a universal check.

**Step 8 — Geometric Interpretation:**
The length of the **sloping side** is:
$$L = y\sqrt{1 + m^2}$$

From Step 6, $b = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)$. Note that:
$$L + \frac{b}{2} = y\sqrt{1+m^2} + y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m) = y(2\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)$$

And the **top width** is:
$$T = b + 2my = 2y\sqrt{1 + m^2}$$

So:
$$T = 2L$$

**Top width = 2 × length of one sloping side.** This is the famous KTU result.

## 3.2 Derivation — Best Triangular Section

**Setting b = 0 in trapezoidal results:**

From Step 6: $0 = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)$, so $\sqrt{1+m^2} = m$. Squaring:
$$1 + m^2 = m^2 \implies 1 = 0 \text{ (impossible)}$$

This is a contradiction because the formula assumed b > 0. So we re-derive for triangular:

**P = 2y√(1+m²)** (no bottom width)
**A = my²**

For fixed A, min P:
$$\frac{dP}{dy} = 0, \quad \text{with } b=0, \text{ so } m = A/y^2$$

Differentiating P directly:
$$P^2 = 4y^2(1+m^2) = 4y^2\left(1 + \frac{A^2}{y^4}\right) = 4y^2 + \frac{4A^2}{y^2}$$

$$\frac{d(P^2)}{dy} = 8y - \frac{8A^2}{y^3} = 0$$

$$y^4 = A^2 \implies y = \sqrt{A}$$

Then $m = A/y^2 = 1$. Wait — let’s redo:

$$m^2 = \frac{1}{2} \implies m = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Therefore the **best triangular section** has side slope $m = 1/\sqrt{2}$, i.e., the **half-angle with the vertical is 45°**, or the **full apex angle is 90°**.

## 3.3 Derivation — Tractive Force Approach (Lane’s Method)

**Step 1 — Shear stress on the bed (horizontal surface):**
Force balance on a control volume of length L in uniform flow:
$$F_{gravity} = F_{friction}$$
$$\gamma A L S = \tau_b (b L)$$
$$\tau_b = \gamma \frac{A}{b} S = \gamma y S$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress on the side slope (inclined surface):**
Lane’s empirical result from extensive experiments:
$$\tau_s = 0.75 \, \gamma y S$$

(About 75% of the bed shear.)

**Step 3 — Critical / Permissible Tractive Force:**
For non-cohesive material, Shields (1936) showed that motion begins when:
$$\tau_c = K (\gamma_s - \gamma) d$$

where:
- $\gamma_s$ = specific weight of sediment
- d = median grain diameter
- K = Shields parameter (function of boundary Reynolds number)

For coarse material (fully rough regime), K ≈ **0.06**.

**Step 4 — Design Condition:**
$$\tau_b = \gamma y S \le \tau_{perm}$$

$$y \le \frac{\tau_{perm}}{\gamma S}$$

## 3.4 Derivation — Velocity Distribution (Power Law)

**Assumption:** Velocity varies as a power of the distance from the bed:
$$u = u_{max} \left(\frac{y}{y_{max}}\right)^n$$

**Step 1 — Mean velocity from the distribution:**
$$V = \frac{1}{y_{max}} \int_0^{y_{max}} u \, dy = \frac{u_{max}}{y_{max}} \int_0^{y_{max}} \left(\frac{y}{y_{max}}\right)^n dy$$

$$V = \frac{u_{max}}{y_{max}^{n+1}} \cdot \frac{y_{max}^{n+1}}{n+1} = \frac{u_{max}}{n+1}$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of mean to maximum velocity:**
$$\frac{V}{u_{max}} = \frac{1}{n+1}$$

For a typical open channel, $n = 1/6$ to $1/7$ (using the $1/7^{th}$ power law):
$$V \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 0.92 \; u_{max}$$

## 3.5 Derivation — Pressure at Spillway Crest (Curvilinear Flow)

**Step 1 — Streamline coordinates:**
For flow over an ogee spillway crest, the crest region has a radius of curvature R_c. Consider a point on the crest surface.

**Step 2 — Equation of motion in the radial direction:**
$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{\rho v^2}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from the crest surface (r = R_c) to the free surface (r = R_c + h):**
At the free surface, $p = p_{atm} = 0$ (gauge). Integrate inward:
$$p(r) = \int_r^{R_c} \frac{\rho v^2}{r} dr$$

If we approximate $v \approx v_0$ (constant = surface velocity):
$$p(r) = \rho v_0^2 \ln\left(\frac{R_c}{r}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Pressure on the crest surface (r = R_c):**

For a well-designed ogee that matches the **lower nappe of a sharp-crested weir**, the velocity distribution is such that the centripetal pressure exactly balances gravity, making $p(R_c) = p_{atm}$ (zero gauge pressure). This prevents **cavitation** damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule for Ogee Crest (KTU board favorite):**
> *If the spillway profile follows the equation $y = -K x^{1.85}$ (Creager profile), the pressure on the crest is atmospheric at the design head, ensuring safe, cavitation-free operation.*

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Best Hydraulic Section (Trapezoidal)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
Design the best hydraulic trapezoidal section for a discharge of 10 m³/s with side slope 1.5H:1V. Take n = 0.015 and S = 1/2500.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Best section condition for trapezoid:**
$$b = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m) = 2y(\sqrt{1+2.25} - 1.5) = 2y(1.803 - 1.5) = 0.606y$$

**Step 2 — Area:**
$$A = (b + my)y = (0.606y + 1.5y)y = 2.106 y^2$$

**Step 3 — Wetted perimeter:**
$$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2} = 0.606y + 2y(1.803) = 0.606y + 3.606y = 4.212y$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic radius:**
$$R = A/P = \frac{2.106y^2}{4.212y} = 0.5y = y/2 \;\; ✓ \text{ (confirms best section)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Manning’s equation:**
$$Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

$$10 = \frac{1}{0.015} (2.106 y^2) (0.5y)^{2/3} (1/2500)^{1/2}$$

$$10 = \frac{1}{0.015} (2.106) (0.5^{2/3}) y^{2+2/3} (0.02)$$

$$10 = \frac{1}{0.015} (2.106) (0.630) (0.02) y^{8/3}$$

$$10 = (66.67)(2.106)(0.630)(0.02) \, y^{8/3}$$

$$10 = 1.767 \, y^{8/3}$$

$$y^{8/3} = 5.66 \implies y = (5.66)^{3/8}$$

$$\boxed{y \approx 1.94 \text{ m}, \quad b = 0.606 \times 1.94 \approx 1.18 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of velocity:**
$$V = Q/A = 10 / (2.106 \times 1.94^2) = 10 / 7.93 = 1.26 \text{ m/s}$$

Check: $V_{min} = 0.75 \le 1.26 \le V_{max} = 2.5$ for concrete — **safe**.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Design Problem | Governing Equation | Unknowns | Methodology |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best section, trapezoidal | $A, b, y, P, R$ linked | 4 variables | Direct closed-form |
| Best section, circular | $A = (D^2/8)(\theta - \sin\theta)$ | θ, y | Solve by trial or tables |
| Stable erodible section | Manning + τ_perm | b, y, S | Iterative (chart) |
| Velocity distribution | Power law or log law | n, u_max | Empirical fit to data |
| Curvilinear pressure | $\partial p/\partial r = \rho v^2/r$ | p(r) | Integrate numerically |

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Best Hydraulic Section" of an open channel. State the geometric condition for the best hydraulic trapezoidal section.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A *best hydraulic section* is a channel cross-section which, for a given cross-sectional area, slope, and roughness, yields the **maximum possible discharge**, or equivalently, for a given discharge, requires the **least cross-sectional area** (i.e., minimum wetted perimeter).

> **Geometric Condition for Trapezoidal Section (2 Marks):**
> 1. The hydraulic radius equals half the depth: $R = y/2$
> 2. The top width equals twice the length of one sloping side: $T = 2 \times y\sqrt{1+m^2}$
> 3. Equivalently, the bottom width is: $b = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**What is meant by "Tractive Force" in an open channel? Write its expression for a wide channel and mention its significance in design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Tractive force is the **shear stress exerted by the flowing water on the channel boundary** (bed and sides). It is the force per unit wetted area that tends to dislodge the boundary material.

> **Expression (1 Mark):** For uniform flow, the average tractive force on the bed of a wide channel is:
> $$\tau_0 = \gamma R S \approx \gamma y S$$

> **Significance (1 Mark):** The actual tractive force $\tau_0$ must be **less than the permissible tractive force $\tau_{perm}$** of the boundary material to prevent scour. This forms the basis of the **stable hydraulic section design** for erodible channels (Lane’s method).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the condition for the **best hydraulic trapezoidal section** in terms of the depth of flow $y$ and side slope $m$. Show clearly that for the best section, $R = y/2$.

**(b)** A trapezoidal channel with side slopes 1.5H:1V is to be designed to carry 15 m³/s of water at a slope of 1 in 2000. The channel is to be lined with concrete having $n = 0.015$. Determine the dimensions of the **best hydraulic section** and check whether the velocity lies between the minimum and maximum permissible values. Take $V_{min} = 0.75$ m/s and $V_{max} = 2.5$ m/s.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** For a trapezoidal channel with bottom width b, depth y, and side slope m (H:V), the area and wetted perimeter are:
$$A = (b + my)y$$
$$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1 + m^2}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Express b in terms of A and y:
$$b = \frac{A}{y} - my$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute into P:
$$P = \frac{A}{y} - my + 2y\sqrt{1+m^2}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** For a given A, m, and S, P is minimum when:
$$\frac{dP}{dy} = -\frac{A}{y^2} - m + 2\sqrt{1+m^2} = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Solving for y:
$$y^2 = \frac{A}{2\sqrt{1+m^2} - m}$$
$$y = \sqrt{\frac{A}{2\sqrt{1+m^2} - m}}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** From Step 4:
$$A = y^2(2\sqrt{1+m^2} - m) = (b + my)y$$

Setting the two expressions for A equal and simplifying gives:
$$b = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Compute R for the best section:
$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{(b+my)y}{b+2y\sqrt{1+m^2}}$$

Substituting $b = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2} - m)$ and simplifying:
$$R = \frac{y}{2}$$

> This is the KTU board-favorite result. **QED.**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Apply best section conditions.
With m = 1.5:
- $\sqrt{1+m^2} = \sqrt{1+2.25} = \sqrt{3.25} = 1.8028$
- $b = 2y(1.8028 - 1.5) = 0.6056y$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Area and perimeter:
- $A = (b+my)y = (0.6056y + 1.5y)y = 2.1056y^2$
- $P = b + 2y(1.8028) = 0.6056y + 3.6056y = 4.2112y$
- $R = A/P = y/2$ ✓

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Manning's equation:
$$Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$
$$15 = \frac{1}{0.015} (2.1056 y^2) (0.5y)^{2/3} (0.0005)^{1/2}$$
$$15 = (66.67)(2.1056)(0.5^{2/3})(0.02236) y^{8/3}$$
$$15 = (66.67)(2.1056)(0.6300)(0.02236) y^{8/3}$$
$$15 = 1.978 \, y^{8/3}$$
$$y^{8/3} = 7.584 \implies y = (7.584)^{3/8}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Calculate y:
$$y = 7.584^{0.375}$$
$$y \approx 2.24 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Calculate b and V:
- $b = 0.6056 \times 2.24 = 1.357$ m
- $A = 2.1056 \times 2.24^2 = 10.56$ m²
- $V = Q/A = 15/10.56 = 1.42$ m/s

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Verification:
- $V_{min} = 0.75 \le 1.42 \le 2.5 = V_{max}$ ✓
- **Design is safe.**

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Final design summary:
- $\boxed{y = 2.24 \text{ m}, \quad b = 1.36 \text{ m}, \quad V = 1.42 \text{ m/s}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Tractive Force Method** for the design of stable hydraulic section in an erodible channel. How does it differ from the Best Hydraulic Section approach?

**(b)** An unlined trapezoidal canal in fine alluvial soil (n = 0.025) is to carry 8 m³/s at a slope of 1 in 4000. The permissible tractive force is 3.5 N/m². Side slopes are 2H:1V. Design the **stable hydraulic section** using Lane's method and check the non-silting velocity (assume $V_{min} = 0.6$ m/s).

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Tractive Force Method Explanation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Concept of tractive force.
The **tractive force** $\tau_0$ is the shear force per unit area exerted by the flowing water on the channel boundary. For uniform flow:
$$\tau_0 = \gamma R S$$

On the **horizontal bed**: $\tau_b = \gamma y S$  
On the **side slopes**: $\tau_s = 0.75 \gamma y S$ (Lane's empirical coefficient)

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Permissible tractive force.
Each boundary material has a **critical shear stress** $\tau_{perm}$ below which it remains stable. The design condition is:
$$\tau_{max} = \gamma y S \le \tau_{perm}$$
This gives the **maximum permissible depth**:
$$y_{max} = \frac{\tau_{perm}}{\gamma S}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Design procedure.
1. Assume trial depth y.
2. Compute $R = A/P$ and check $\gamma R S \le \tau_{perm}$.
3. Compute V from Manning's equation and check $V \ge V_{min}$.
4. Iterate until both conditions are satisfied.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Comparison with Best Hydraulic Section.

| Aspect | Best Hydraulic Section | Tractive Force Method |
|---|---|---|
| Channel type | Non-erodible (rigid) | Erodible (mobile) |
| Governing criterion | Maximum Q for given A | Maximum $\tau$ for given material |
| Output | Geometric proportions | Stable depth and slope |
| Velocity check | $V_{min} \le V \le V_{max}$ | $V \ge V_{min}$ and $\tau \le \tau_{perm}$ |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Trial. Assume y = 1.5 m, b = 3.0 m, m = 2.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute geometry.
- $A = (3.0 + 2 \times 1.5) \times 1.5 = 9.0$ m²
- $P = 3.0 + 2 \times 1.5 \times \sqrt{5} = 3.0 + 6.708 = 9.708$ m
- $R = 9.0/9.708 = 0.927$ m
- $\tau_b = 9810 \times 0.927 \times 0.00025 = 2.27$ N/m²

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Check tractive force.
$2.27 < 3.5$ ✓ (boundary stable)

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute velocity.
$$V = \frac{1}{0.025}(0.927)^{2/3}(0.00025)^{1/2}$$
$$V = 40 \times 0.952 \times 0.01581 = 0.602 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Check non-silting.
$0.602 \ge 0.6$ ✓ (just meets)

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Verification of Q.
$Q = 0.602 \times 9.0 = 5.42$ m³/s — **too low!** (need 8 m³/s)

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Increase size — Try y = 1.8 m, b = 4.0 m.
- $A = (4.0 + 3.6) \times 1.8 = 13.68$ m²
- $P = 4.0 + 2 \times 1.8\sqrt{5} = 4.0 + 8.05 = 12.05$ m
- $R = 13.68/12.05 = 1.135$ m
- $\tau_b = 9810 \times 1.135 \times 0.00025 = 2.78$ N/m² < 3.5 ✓
- $V = (1/0.025)(1.135)^{2/3}(0.00025)^{1/2} = 40 \times 1.087 \times 0.01581 = 0.687$ m/s
- $Q = 0.687 \times 13.68 = 9.40$ m³/s

Slightly overshot. Try y = 1.75 m, b = 3.8 m:
- $A = (3.8+3.5)\times 1.75 = 12.78$ m²
- $P = 3.8 + 2 \times 1.75 \times 2.236 = 3.8 + 7.826 = 11.63$ m
- $R = 12.78/11.63 = 1.099$ m
- $\tau_b = 9810 \times 1.099 \times 0.00025 = 2.69$ N/m² < 3.5 ✓
- $V = 40 \times (1.099)^{2/3} \times 0.01581 = 40 \times 1.066 \times 0.01581 = 0.674$ m/s
- $Q = 0.674 \times 12.78 = 8.61$ m³/s — closer to 8 m³/s ✓

**Final Design:**
$$\boxed{y \approx 1.75 \text{ m}, \quad b \approx 3.8 \text{ m}, \quad V \approx 0.67 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing tractive force with shear stress units:** Tractive force is **per unit area** (N/m²), not total force (N). Many students write the wrong units. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.75 factor for side slopes:** Side slope shear is **less** than bed shear. Using $\tau_s = \gamma y S$ on slopes is a common error. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not verifying R = y/2** for the best trapezoidal section: This is the **universal check**. If your answer doesn't satisfy it, your derivation has an error. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Using m as V:H instead of H:V:** A KTU-favorite trap. If m = 1.5 means 1.5V:1H (wrong), all geometry inverts. Always clarify. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting non-silting check** in erodible design: Even if τ is OK, you must check $V \ge V_{min}$. (-2 marks)
> 6. **Mixing up $\tau_{max}$ location:** The maximum tractive force is **on the bed**, NOT on the side slopes. Side slope has 75% of bed value. (-1 mark)
> 7. **Crest pressure calculation for ogee spillway:** If the profile is correctly designed (Creager), $p_{crest} = p_{atm}$. If you get a different answer, your radius of curvature assumption is wrong. (-1 mark)

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Rapid Revision Bullets**

### Non-Erodible Channels
- **Best Hydraulic Section** = section with **minimum wetted perimeter** for a given area.
- For a **trapezoidal** best section: $R = y/2$, $T = 2 \times$ side length, $b = 2y(\sqrt{1+m^2}-m)$.
- For a **rectangular** best section: $b = 2y$ (m = 0).
- For a **triangular** best section: $m = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (90° apex).
- For a **circular** best section: depth ≈ **0.95D** (partially full flow).
- Velocity design: $V_{min} \le V \le V_{max}$ — both must be checked.

### Erodible Channels
- **Tractive force** $\tau_0 = \gamma R S$ (N/m²) — shear on the bed.
- **Side slope tractive force** = **0.75** × bed tractive force (Lane's empirical factor).
- **Design criterion:** $\tau_0 \le \tau_{perm}$ (prevent scour); $V \ge V_{min}$ (prevent silting).
- **Permissible tractive force** depends on grain size and cohesion.
- For **coarse non-cohesive material:** $\tau_{perm} \approx 0.06(\gamma_s - \gamma)d$.

### Velocity Distribution
- **Power law:** $u/u_{max} = (y/y_{max})^{1/n}$, with $n \approx 6$ to 7.
- **Log law (Prandtl-von Kármán):** $u/u_* = (1/\kappa)\ln(y/y_0) + C$, with $\kappa = 0.41$.
- **Maximum velocity** occurs below the free surface, not at it.
- **V/u_max** ≈ 0.85 to 0.92 for typical channels.

### Curvilinear Flow — Spillway Crest & Bucket
- **Radial pressure gradient:** $\partial p/\partial r = \rho v^2/r$.
- **Ogee spillway crest:** designed so that $p_{crest} = p_{atm}$ (no cavitation).
- **Creager profile:** $y = -K x^{1.85}$ (standard ogee shape).
- **Spillway bucket:** uses centrifugal pressure to stabilize the hydraulic jump.
- **Cavitation** risk: $p_{crest} < p_{vapor}$ → use aeration, smooth concrete, correct profile.

### Must-Memorize Numbers
- $\gamma_{water} = 9810$ N/m³
- $\kappa$ (von Kármán) = **0.41**
- Shields parameter K ≈ **0.06** (fully rough)
- Lane's side-slope factor = **0.75**
- Best triangle apex angle = **90°**
- Best circle depth = **0.95D**

### Universal Engineering Truth
> *"The best hydraulic section minimizes perimeter, but the **stable** hydraulic section respects the soil's own strength."* — Always pick the right tool for the right job.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Open Channel Hydraulics (PECET523, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
