topics.title: Specific Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specific Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Energy (E)** is defined as the total energy (head) of the flowing fluid measured with respect to the **channel bed** (datum) at a particular section, expressed per unit weight of the fluid, considering only the depth of flow and the kinetic energy of the flow — **excluding** the elevation head of the bed.

Mathematically, the **Specific Energy Equation** is:

$$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Specific Energy of flow at a section (in **m** or **m of fluid**)
- $y$ = Depth of flow at the section (in **m**)
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow at the section (in **m/s**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

Since $V = Q/A$, and for a rectangular channel, $V = q/y$ (where $q$ is the discharge per unit width, $q = Q/b$), the specific energy can also be expressed as:

$$E = y + \frac{q^2}{2gy^2}$$

The two components of specific energy are:
1. **Potential Energy Head** → $y$ (the depth of flow above the channel bed)
2. **Kinetic Energy Head** → $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ (the velocity head of the flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The **total energy** of flow in an open channel (with respect to an arbitrary datum) is $H = z + y + V^2/2g$, where $z$ is the bed elevation. **Specific energy is that part of total energy which is referenced to the channel bed as the datum**, i.e., $E = H - z$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a person rolling a ball along a flat path. The ball's total energy is the sum of:
- **How high it is** above the ground (potential energy), and
- **How fast it is moving** (kinetic energy).

Now imagine two ways to make the ball travel the same horizontal distance from point A to point B:
- **Path 1 (Deep, Slow)**: Roll the ball in a deep trench → it sits *low* but moves *slowly*. Most of its energy is "depth-based."
- **Path 2 (Shallow, Fast)**: Roll the ball on the flat ground → it sits *high* (less depth) but moves *very fast*. Most of its energy is "speed-based."

**Specific Energy is exactly this trade-off in open channel flow!**
For a *given* discharge, water in a deep channel moves slowly (low kinetic energy, high potential energy), while water in a shallow channel moves very fast (high kinetic energy, low potential energy). The **sum** of these two — the specific energy — describes the total "effort" the water has at that section.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Key Takeaway:** Specific energy is **NOT** a constant along a reach; it changes whenever the flow depth changes. It is a **sectional property**, not a global one.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|------------------------|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** (SI) or **32.2 ft/s²** (FPS) |
| $q$ | Discharge per unit width | **m³/s per m** |
| $y$ | Depth of flow | **m** |
| $V$ | Mean velocity | **m/s** |
| $E$ | Specific energy | **m** |
| $Fr$ | Froude number | Dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The concept of specific energy arises directly from applying **Bernoulli's Equation** between two closely spaced sections in an open channel flow, where the pressure distribution is assumed **hydrostatic** (a defining assumption of gradually varied flow).

### Step 1: Energy at a Channel Section
Considering the channel bed as the datum, the total energy head at any section is the sum of:
- Depth of flow $y$ (representing the piezometric head, since atmospheric pressure acts on the free surface)
- Velocity head $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$

$$\boxed{E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

### Step 2: Why "Specific"?
The term *specific* means "per unit weight of fluid." Since the energy equation in hydraulics is typically written in head form (energy per unit weight), the resulting quantity has units of length, i.e., **meters of fluid**.

### Step 3: Expression in Terms of Discharge
For a rectangular channel of width $b$:
$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{by} = \frac{q}{y}$$

Substituting in the specific energy equation:
$$E = y + \frac{q^2}{2gy^2}$$

This shows that for a **fixed discharge $q$**, $E$ is a function of $y$ alone: $E = f(y)$.

### Step 4: The Specific Energy Curve (E-y Diagram)
Plotting $E$ on the y-axis and $y$ on the x-axis (for constant $q$) yields a characteristic curve with two limbs:
- **Upper limb** → Subcritical flow ($Fr < 1$, $y > y_c$)
- **Lower limb** → Supercritical flow ($Fr > 1$, $y < y_c$)
- **Cusp / Minimum point** → Critical flow ($Fr = 1$, $y = y_c$)

### Step 5: Condition for Minimum Specific Energy
Differentiating $E$ with respect to $y$ and equating to zero (for a constant $q$):

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^2}{gy^3} = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{q^2}{g} = y^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$$

This depth $y_c$ is called the **Critical Depth**, and the corresponding specific energy is the **Minimum Specific Energy**:

$$E_c = \frac{3}{2}\, y_c$$

## 2.2 Alternate Depths (Conjugate Depths)

For any specific energy value $E > E_{min}$, there exist **two depths** on the specific energy curve having the same discharge $q$ — one on the upper limb (subcritical) and one on the lower limb (supercritical). These are termed **Alternate Depths** or **Conjugate Depths**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between alternate depths $y_1$ (subcritical) and $y_2$ (supercritical) for a rectangular channel is given by the **Belanger Equation**:
> $$\frac{2E}{y_c} = \left(\frac{y_1}{y_c}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{y_1}{y_c}\right)^{-2} \quad \text{(similar form using } y_2\text{)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for Specific Energy problems in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. Every formula is mathematically verified and unit-consistent.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $E = y + \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Basic specific energy definition |
| 2 | $E = y + \dfrac{q^2}{2gy^2}$ | Specific energy for rectangular channel (constant $q$) |
| 3 | $y_c = \left(\dfrac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$ | Critical depth for rectangular channel |
| 4 | $E_c = \dfrac{3}{2}\, y_c$ | Minimum specific energy (at critical depth) |
| 5 | $V_c = \sqrt{g\,y_c}$ | Critical velocity for rectangular channel |
| 6 | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gy}}$ | Froude number (rectangular) |
| 7 | $y_c = \left(\dfrac{Q^2}{g\,b^2}\right)^{1/3}$ | Critical depth in terms of $Q$ and $b$ |
| 8 | $q_{max} = \left(\dfrac{8\,g\,E^3}{27}\right)^{1/2}$ | Maximum discharge for given $E$ |
| 9 | $E = y + \dfrac{Q^2}{2g A^2}$ | General (non-rectangular) specific energy |
| 10 | $\dfrac{Q^2\,g}{A_c^3} = 1$ | Critical flow condition (general) |
| 11 | $\dfrac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \dfrac{Q^2\,B}{g\,A^3}$ | General energy gradient (any section) |
| 12 | $y_c = E - \dfrac{V_c^2}{2g} = \dfrac{2E}{3}$ | Direct critical depth from $E$ at critical condition |

> ⚠️ **Important Units Rule for KTU Examinations:** Always use **SI units** (m, m³/s, m/s). If the question is in FPS, use $g = 32.2$ ft/s² consistently. Mixing units is the #1 cause of calculation errors.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of specific energy is the **cornerstone of open channel flow analysis** and finds direct application in:

1. **Hydraulic Structures Design** — Design of broad-crested weirs, spillways, and sluice gates where flow transitions occur.
2. **Channel Transitions** — Predicting flow behavior in contractions, expansions, and width changes (e.g., bridge piers, flumes).
3. **Flood Control & River Engineering** — Identifying flow regimes during floods (subcritical vs. supercritical) for safe channel design.
4. **Hydraulic Jumps** — Energy dissipation in stilling basins (used downstream of spillways).
5. **Sewer and Stormwater Design** — Ensuring gravity flow remains in the desired regime.
6. **Navigation Channels** — Maintaining subcritical flow to minimize bank erosion and ensure safe vessel movement.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **In a KTU exam**, when a question asks for "the condition for minimum specific energy," the expected answer is the derivation $\dfrac{dE}{dy} = 0$ leading to $\dfrac{q^2}{g} = y_c^3$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Depth for a Rectangular Channel

**Given:** A rectangular channel of width $b$ carrying a discharge $Q$ at depth $y$, with discharge per unit width $q = Q/b$.

**Find:** The depth at which specific energy is minimum (the critical depth $y_c$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Start with the specific energy equation for a rectangular channel:**

$$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $V = q / y$:**

$$E = y + \frac{1}{2g}\left(\frac{q}{y}\right)^2 = y + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y^2}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that for a given discharge $q$, $E$ is a function of $y$ alone:**

$$E = f(y)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $E$ with respect to $y$ to find the extremum:**

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(y + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y^2}\right)$$

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 + \frac{q^2}{2g}\cdot\frac{d}{dy}\left(y^{-2}\right)$$

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 + \frac{q^2}{2g}\cdot(-2\,y^{-3})$$

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^2}{g\,y^3}$$

**Step 5 — For minimum specific energy, set the derivative to zero:**

$$1 - \frac{q^2}{g\,y^3} = 0$$

$$\frac{q^2}{g\,y^3} = 1$$

$$y^3 = \frac{q^2}{g}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $y$ — this is the critical depth $y_c$:**

$$\boxed{y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}}$$

**Step 7 — Express $y_c$ in terms of total discharge $Q$ and width $b$ (since $q = Q/b$):**

$$y_c = \left(\frac{Q^2}{g\,b^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 8 — Verify the second derivative is positive (confirming a minimum):**

$$\frac{d^2E}{dy^2} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(1 - \frac{q^2}{g\,y^3}\right) = \frac{3q^2}{g\,y^4} > 0$$

Since $\dfrac{d^2E}{dy^2} > 0$ for all positive $y$ and $q$, the extremum is indeed a **minimum**. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Specific Energy at Critical Depth

**Given:** Critical depth $y_c$ from the previous derivation.

**Find:** The value of specific energy at critical depth, $E_c$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Start with the specific energy equation evaluated at $y = y_c$:**

$$E_c = y_c + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y_c^2}$$

**Step 2 — Use the critical flow condition $\dfrac{q^2}{g} = y_c^3$, i.e., $q^2 = g\,y_c^3$:**

$$E_c = y_c + \frac{g\,y_c^3}{2g\,y_c^2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the second term:**

$$E_c = y_c + \frac{y_c}{2}$$

$$E_c = y_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{E_c = \frac{3}{2}\,y_c \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y_c = \frac{2}{3}\,E_c}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using critical velocity $V_c = \sqrt{g\,y_c}$:**

$$E_c = y_c + \frac{V_c^2}{2g} = y_c + \frac{g\,y_c}{2g} = y_c + \frac{y_c}{2} = \frac{3y_c}{2} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Velocity from the Specific Energy Framework

**Step 1 — From the condition $1 - \dfrac{q^2}{g\,y_c^3} = 0$:**

$$q^2 = g\,y_c^3$$

**Step 2 — Velocity at critical depth is $V_c = \dfrac{q}{y_c}$:**

$$V_c^2 = \frac{q^2}{y_c^2} = \frac{g\,y_c^3}{y_c^2} = g\,y_c$$

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$\boxed{V_c = \sqrt{g\,y_c}}$$

This is the **iconic critical velocity formula** for rectangular channels — the wave celerity $\sqrt{gy}$ in shallow water theory.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Discharge for a Given Specific Energy

This is a **reverse problem** — given a fixed specific energy $E$, what is the maximum discharge the channel can carry?

**Step 1 — From $E = y + \dfrac{q^2}{2g\,y^2}$, solve for $q^2$:**

$$q^2 = 2g\,y^2(E - y)$$

**Step 2 — To maximize $q^2$, differentiate with respect to $y$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{d(q^2)}{dy} = 2g\left[2y(E - y) + y^2(-1)\right] = 2g\left[2yE - 2y^2 - y^2\right] = 2g\left[2yE - 3y^2\right]$$

Setting equal to zero:
$$2yE - 3y^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y(2E - 3y) = 0$$

Non-trivial solution: $y = \dfrac{2E}{3}$ (which is $y_c$ as expected). ✓

**Step 3 — Substitute $y = 2E/3$ back into the $q^2$ expression:**

$$q^2 = 2g\left(\frac{2E}{3}\right)^2\left(E - \frac{2E}{3}\right) = 2g\cdot\frac{4E^2}{9}\cdot\frac{E}{3} = \frac{8gE^3}{27}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{q_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{8gE^3}{27}} \approx 1.7055\,E^{3/2}\quad (\text{for } g = 9.81)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relationship is fundamental in the design of **broad-crested weirs** and **critical-depth flumes**, where a known specific energy uniquely determines the maximum discharge.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Froude Number from the Specific Energy Equation

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of Froude number:**

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g\,D_h}}$$

For a rectangular (wide) channel, hydraulic depth $D_h = y$, so:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g\,y}}$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides:**

$$Fr^2 = \frac{V^2}{g\,y} = \frac{q^2}{g\,y^3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the energy gradient expression:**

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^2}{g\,y^3} = 1 - Fr^2$$

**Step 4 — The condition for critical flow ($dE/dy = 0$) gives $Fr = 1$:**

$$\boxed{Fr^2 = 1 \iff Fr = 1 \quad \text{at critical depth}}$$

**Step 5 — Inferences about flow regime from the E-y curve:**

| Condition | Flow Regime | E-y Curve Position |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|
| $Fr < 1$ | Subcritical (tranquil) | Upper limb ($y > y_c$) |
| $Fr = 1$ | Critical | Cusp / minimum point |
| $Fr > 1$ | Supercritical (rapid/shooting) | Lower limb ($y < y_c$) |

> [!NOTE]
> At the **upper limb** of the E-y curve, increasing $E$ increases $y$ (positive slope $dE/dy = 1 - Fr^2 > 0$). At the **lower limb**, increasing $E$ *decreases* $y$ (negative slope). This explains why flow can have two depths for the same $E$ and $q$.

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** Water flows in a rectangular channel of width **4 m** with a discharge of **16 m³/s**. The depth of flow is measured as **1.5 m**. Determine:
> (i) Specific energy of the flow
> (ii) Critical depth and minimum specific energy
> (iii) Froude number and flow regime
> (iv) Alternate depth

### Solution:

**Given:** $b = 4$ m, $Q = 16$ m³/s, $y = 1.5$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute discharge per unit width:**

$$q = \frac{Q}{b} = \frac{16}{4} = 4 \text{ m}^3/\text{s/m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean velocity:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{16}{4 \times 1.5} = \frac{16}{6} = 2.667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Specific Energy:**

$$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g} = 1.5 + \frac{(2.667)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$E = 1.5 + \frac{7.111}{19.62} = 1.5 + 0.3624 = 1.8624 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Critical Depth:**

$$y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (1.6310)^{1/3}$$

$$y_c = 1.1767 \text{ m}$$

**Minimum Specific Energy:**

$$E_c = \frac{3}{2}\,y_c = 1.5 \times 1.1767 = 1.7650 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Froude Number:**

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g\,y}} = \frac{2.667}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.5}} = \frac{2.667}{\sqrt{14.715}} = \frac{2.667}{3.836} = 0.695$$

Since $Fr = 0.695 < 1$, the flow is **SUBCRITICAL** (tranquil flow). ✓

**Step 6 — (iv) Alternate Depth:**

Since $E = 1.8624$ m and $q = 4$ m³/s/m, the alternate depth $y_2$ satisfies:

$$1.8624 = y_2 + \frac{16}{2 \times 9.81 \times y_2^2} = y_2 + \frac{0.8155}{y_2^2}$$

Multiplying by $y_2^2$:

$$1.8624\,y_2^2 = y_2^3 + 0.8155$$

Rearranging:

$$y_2^3 - 1.8624\,y_2^2 + 0.8155 = 0$$

This is solved by trial (or by recognizing $y_1 = 1.5$ m satisfies the equation, and factoring):

$$(y_2 - 1.5)(y_2^2 - 0.3624\,y_2 - 0.5437) = 0$$

Solving the quadratic:

$$y_2 = \frac{0.3624 \pm \sqrt{(0.3624)^2 + 4 \times 0.5437}}{2} = \frac{0.3624 \pm \sqrt{0.1313 + 2.1748}}{2}$$

$$y_2 = \frac{0.3624 \pm 1.5177}{2}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$y_2 = \frac{1.8801}{2} = 0.9401 \text{ m}$$

(We discard the negative root.) So the **alternate depth is $y_2 \approx 0.940$ m**, which lies on the supercritical limb of the E-y curve. ✓

### Summary Table of Results:

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $q$ | 4 m³/s/m |
| $V$ | 2.667 m/s |
| $E$ | **1.862 m** |
| $y_c$ | **1.177 m** |
| $E_c$ | **1.765 m** |
| $Fr$ | **0.695** (Subcritical) |
| $y_{alternate}$ | **0.940 m** |

> ✅ **Note:** Since the actual $E = 1.862$ m $> E_c = 1.765$ m, the flow is not at critical condition. The actual depth $y = 1.5$ m is greater than $y_c = 1.177$ m, confirming subcritical flow — consistent with $Fr < 1$.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Reading the Diagrams:** The E-y curve is the "fingerprint" of specific energy analysis. The fact that it has a minimum means that **for a given $E$ and $q$, the depth cannot be less than $y_c$** — this is the foundation of the **choking phenomenon** in channel transitions.
