# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Define specific energy and specific energy curve. State the condition for minimum specific energy in a rectangular channel.**

**Model Answer:**

**Specific Energy:** The specific energy of flow in an open channel at a section is defined as the energy per unit weight of the fluid measured with respect to the channel bed at that section. It is the sum of the depth of flow ($y$) and the velocity head ($V^2/2g$):

$$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Specific Energy Curve:** The curve obtained by plotting the specific energy $E$ as a function of depth of flow $y$ for a constant discharge per unit width $q$ is called the specific energy curve. It has two limbs separated by a cusp at the critical depth, with the upper limb representing subcritical flow and the lower limb representing supercritical flow.

**Condition for Minimum Specific Energy:** Differentiating $E$ with respect to $y$ and setting $\dfrac{dE}{dy} = 0$:

$$1 - \frac{q^2}{g\,y^3} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$$

This critical depth corresponds to the **minimum specific energy** $E_c = \dfrac{3}{2}\,y_c$, and the flow is at the **critical condition** ($Fr = 1$). **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Distinguish between subcritical, critical, and supercritical flow using the Froude number. What is the significance of critical depth in open channel flow?**

**Model Answer:**

| Flow Regime | Froude Number | Depth | Velocity | Energy Head Relation |
|-------------|---------------|-------|----------|----------------------|
| Subcritical (Tranquil) | $Fr < 1$ | $y > y_c$ | $V < V_c$ | $y > V^2/2g$ |
| Critical | $Fr = 1$ | $y = y_c$ | $V = V_c = \sqrt{gy_c}$ | $y = 2V^2/(2g) \Rightarrow E_c = 3y_c/2$ |
| Supercritical (Rapid/Shooting) | $Fr > 1$ | $y < y_c$ | $V > V_c$ | $y < V^2/2g$ |

**Significance of Critical Depth:**

1. It marks the transition between subcritical and supercritical flow regimes.
2. The specific energy is minimum at critical depth — no flow can exist at a depth less than $y_c$ for the same $E$ and $q$.
3. It is the depth at which the channel conveys **maximum discharge for a given specific energy** ($q_{max} = \sqrt{8gE^3/27}$).
4. Critical depth serves as the **control section** in gradually varied flow computations (e.g., at the entrance/exit of channels, over weirs, at free overfalls).
5. Wave propagation behavior changes at critical depth — disturbances cannot propagate upstream in supercritical flow. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> 📝 **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives; the student answers either one.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the critical depth and minimum specific energy in a rectangular channel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular channel **6 m wide** carries a discharge of **30 m³/s** at a depth of **2.0 m**. Compute:
1. Specific energy of the flow
2. Critical depth and minimum specific energy
3. Froude number and identify the flow regime
4. The alternate (conjugate) depth for the same specific energy and discharge

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation:

We start with the specific energy equation for a rectangular channel:

$$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

For a rectangular channel, $V = q/y$, where $q = Q/b$ is the discharge per unit width. Substituting:

$$E = y + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y^2}$$

For a constant discharge $q$, $E$ is a function of $y$ alone. To find the critical depth, we differentiate $E$ with respect to $y$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^2}{g\,y^3} = 0$$

Solving:

$$y^3 = \frac{q^2}{g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$$

This is the **critical depth**.

The **minimum specific energy** is obtained by substituting $y_c$ back into the $E$ equation. Using $q^2 = g\,y_c^3$:

$$E_c = y_c + \frac{g\,y_c^3}{2g\,y_c^2} = y_c + \frac{y_c}{2} = \frac{3y_c}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_c = \frac{3}{2}\,y_c}$$

The second derivative $\dfrac{d^2E}{dy^2} = \dfrac{3q^2}{g\,y^4} > 0$ confirms a minimum. **(7 Marks — 3 for setting up derivative, 2 for solving, 2 for minimum specific energy result)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution:

**Given:** $b = 6$ m, $Q = 30$ m³/s, $y = 2.0$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Discharge per unit width:**

$$q = \frac{Q}{b} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s/m}$$

**Step 2 — Mean velocity:**

$$V = \frac{q}{y} = \frac{5}{2.0} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — (1) Specific Energy:**

$$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g} = 2.0 + \frac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 2.0 + \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 2.0 + 0.3185 = 2.3185 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2.3185 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — (2) Critical Depth:**

$$y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{25}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (2.5484)^{1/3} = 1.3662 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_c = 1.3662 \text{ m}}$$

**Minimum Specific Energy:**

$$E_c = \frac{3}{2}\,y_c = 1.5 \times 1.3662 = 2.0494 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 2.0494 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — (3) Froude Number:**

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g\,y}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 2.0}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{19.62}} = \frac{2.5}{4.4294}$$

$$\boxed{Fr = 0.5644 \;\; (< 1)}$$

Since $Fr < 1$, the flow is **SUBCRITICAL (tranquil)**. ✓

This is consistent with the observation that $y = 2.0$ m $> y_c = 1.366$ m (depth on upper limb of E-y curve).

**Step 6 — (4) Alternate Depth:**

We need to find $y_2$ such that $E = 2.3185$ m and $q = 5$ m³/s/m:

$$2.3185 = y_2 + \frac{25}{2 \times 9.81 \times y_2^2} = y_2 + \frac{1.2742}{y_2^2}$$

Multiplying through by $y_2^2$:

$$2.3185\,y_2^2 = y_2^3 + 1.2742$$

$$y_2^3 - 2.3185\,y_2^2 + 1.2742 = 0$$

Since $y_1 = 2.0$ m is a known root, we factor:

$$(y_2 - 2.0)(y_2^2 + b\,y_2 + c) = y_2^3 - 2.3185\,y_2^2 + 1.2742$$

By polynomial division or matching coefficients:

$$(y_2 - 2.0)(y_2^2 - 0.3185\,y_2 - 0.6371) = 0$$

Solving the quadratic $y_2^2 - 0.3185\,y_2 - 0.6371 = 0$:

$$y_2 = \frac{0.3185 \pm \sqrt{(0.3185)^2 + 4(0.6371)}}{2} = \frac{0.3185 \pm \sqrt{0.1014 + 2.5484}}{2}$$

$$y_2 = \frac{0.3185 \pm \sqrt{2.6498}}{2} = \frac{0.3185 \pm 1.6278}{2}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$y_2 = \frac{0.3185 + 1.6278}{2} = \frac{1.9463}{2} = 0.9732 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{alternate} = 0.9732 \text{ m}}$$

**Verification (Alternate Depth):**

$$E = 0.9732 + \frac{(5/0.9732)^2}{19.62} = 0.9732 + \frac{(5.138)^2}{19.62} = 0.9732 + \frac{26.40}{19.62} = 0.9732 + 1.3455 = 2.319 \text{ m} \;\; \checkmark$$

This matches $E = 2.3185$ m (within rounding error), confirming the alternate depth.

**Summary of Results for Part (b):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Specific energy $E$ | **2.319 m** |
| Critical depth $y_c$ | **1.366 m** |
| Minimum specific energy $E_c$ | **2.049 m** |
| Froude number $Fr$ | **0.564** |
| Flow regime | **Subcritical** |
| Alternate depth $y_2$ | **0.973 m** |

**(7 Marks — 1 for E, 2 for $y_c$ and $E_c$, 1 for Fr and regime, 3 for alternate depth calculation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of alternate depths. Show that for a given specific energy and discharge in a rectangular channel, the sum of the specific energies at the two alternate depths equals twice the critical depth contribution, and derive the relationship between alternate depths and critical depth. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wide rectangular channel carries a specific discharge of **3 m³/s per meter width**. Find:
1. The critical depth and the corresponding minimum specific energy
2. The maximum discharge per unit width that can be conveyed at a specific energy of **2.5 m**
3. If the actual depth of flow is **0.5 m**, determine the Froude number and state the flow regime.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Alternate Depths:

**Concept:** For a given discharge per unit width $q$ and specific energy $E > E_c$, the specific energy equation $E = y + q^2/(2gy^2)$ is a **cubic in $y$** and yields **two real positive roots** (the alternate depths), with the third root being negative or complex.

**Derivation of the Belanger-type Relationship:**

Let $y_1$ and $y_2$ be the two alternate depths. Both satisfy:

$$E = y_1 + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y_1^2} = y_2 + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y_2^2}$$

Subtracting:

$$y_1 - y_2 = \frac{q^2}{2g}\left(\frac{1}{y_2^2} - \frac{1}{y_1^2}\right) = \frac{q^2}{2g}\cdot\frac{y_1^2 - y_2^2}{y_1^2 y_2^2}$$

$$y_1 - y_2 = \frac{q^2}{2g}\cdot\frac{(y_1 - y_2)(y_1 + y_2)}{y_1^2 y_2^2}$$

Dividing both sides by $(y_1 - y_2)$ (assuming $y_1 \neq y_2$):

$$1 = \frac{q^2}{2g}\cdot\frac{y_1 + y_2}{y_1^2 y_2^2}$$

$$2g\,y_1^2 y_2^2 = q^2(y_1 + y_2)$$

Using the critical flow condition $q^2 = g\,y_c^3$:

$$2g\,y_1^2 y_2^2 = g\,y_c^3(y_1 + y_2)$$

$$\boxed{2\,y_1^2 y_2^2 = y_c^3(y_1 + y_2)}$$

This is the **alternate depths relationship** for a rectangular channel. Dividing both sides by $y_c^4$ and defining $y_1/y_c = \eta_1$ and $y_2/y_c = \eta_2$:

$$2\,\eta_1^2 \eta_2^2 = \eta_1 + \eta_2$$

For the specific energy in normalized form: $\dfrac{2E}{y_c} = \eta^2 + \eta^{-2}$, leading to Belanger's equation. **(7 Marks — 2 for concept, 3 for derivation, 2 for final relationship)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution:

**Given:** $q = 3$ m³/s/m, $E = 2.5$ m, $y = 0.5$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — (1) Critical Depth:**

$$y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{9}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (0.9174)^{1/3} = 0.9710 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_c = 0.971 \text{ m}}$$

**Minimum Specific Energy:**

$$E_c = \frac{3}{2}\,y_c = 1.5 \times 0.9710 = 1.4565 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 1.457 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2 — (2) Maximum Discharge for $E = 2.5$ m:**

$$q_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{8gE^3}{27}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 9.81 \times (2.5)^3}{27}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 9.81 \times 15.625}{27}}$$

$$q_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{1226.25}{27}} = \sqrt{45.4167} = 6.7392 \text{ m}^3/\text{s/m}$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} = 6.739 \text{ m}^3/\text{s/m}}$$

**Step 3 — (3) Froude Number for $y = 0.5$ m:**

$$V = \frac{q}{y} = \frac{3}{0.5} = 6 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g\,y}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{4.905}} = \frac{6}{2.2147}$$

$$\boxed{Fr = 2.709 \;\; (> 1)}$$

Since $Fr = 2.709 > 1$, the flow is **SUPERCRITICAL (rapid/shooting)**. ✓

**Verification:** $y = 0.5$ m $< y_c = 0.971$ m, confirming the flow is on the lower (supercritical) limb of the E-y curve. ✓

**Summary of Results for Part (b):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Critical depth $y_c$ | **0.971 m** |
| Minimum specific energy $E_c$ | **1.457 m** |
| Maximum discharge $q_{max}$ for $E = 2.5$ m | **6.739 m³/s/m** |
| Froude number at $y = 0.5$ m | **2.709** |
| Flow regime | **Supercritical** |

**(7 Marks — 2 for $y_c$ and $E_c$, 2 for $q_{max}$, 1.5 for Fr, 1.5 for regime identification)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU 2024 ESE — SPECIFIC ENERGY QUESTIONS**
>
> **1. Forgetting to square $V$ correctly:**
> A frequent error is writing $V/2g$ instead of $V^2/2g$ in the specific energy equation. This is a **3-mark error** in Part A and a **2-mark error** in Part B. **Always re-check the velocity head.**
>
> **2. Confusing $q$ with $Q$:**
> $q = Q/b$ is the discharge per unit width — required for rectangular channel calculations. Using $Q$ directly in the $E = y + Q^2/(2gA^2)$ formula is correct for the general case, but for $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3}$, you **must** use $q$. Forgetting to divide by $b$ is a classic blunder.
>
> **3. Misidentifying the alternate depth:**
> When the actual depth is subcritical, the alternate depth is the **supercritical root** (less than $y_c$). Some students return the subcritical root they started with. The alternate depth is always on the *opposite limb* of the E-y curve.
>
> **4. Sign errors in cubic solving:**
> The cubic $y^3 - E\,y^2 + q^2/(2g) = 0$ has three roots. **Two are positive (the alternate depths)** and one is negative. Students who pick the negative root lose **3 marks** immediately.
>
> **5. Not stating the units:**
> KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units in the final answer. Always write "m" for lengths, "m/s" for velocities, "m³/s" for discharges.
>
> **6. Wrong $g$ value:**
> Use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless the problem is in FPS (then $g = 32.2$ ft/s²). Mixing the two systems in the same problem = full marks cancellation on that sub-part.
>
> **7. Not classifying the flow regime:**
> Computing $Fr$ but forgetting to state whether the flow is subcritical, critical, or supercritical is treated as an **incomplete answer** in KTU evaluation. Always conclude with: *"Since $Fr < 1$ (or $> 1$ or $= 1$), the flow is subcritical (or supercritical or critical)."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **Last-Minute Revision — Specific Energy (KTU Module 2)**

- ✅ **Specific Energy:** $E = y + V^2/2g$ — measured with respect to the **channel bed**.
- ✅ **For rectangular channels:** $E = y + q^2/(2gy^2)$ where $q = Q/b$.
- ✅ **Critical depth (rectangular):** $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3} = (Q^2/(gb^2))^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Minimum specific energy:** $E_c = 3y_c/2 = 2y_c/3 \cdot 3 = 1.5\,y_c$.
- ✅ **Critical velocity (rectangular):** $V_c = \sqrt{g\,y_c}$.
- ✅ **Froude number:** $Fr = V/\sqrt{gy}$. Flow regime is **subcritical** if $Fr<1$, **critical** if $Fr=1$, **supercritical** if $Fr>1$.
- ✅ **Condition for minimum $E$:** $dE/dy = 0$ gives $q^2 = g\,y_c^3$.
- ✅ **Alternate depths:** For $E > E_c$, there are **two real positive depths** with the same $q$ — one subcritical ($y_1 > y_c$) and one supercritical ($y_2 < y_c$).
- ✅ **Alternate depths relationship:** $2y_1^2 y_2^2 = y_c^3(y_1 + y_2)$.
- ✅ **Maximum discharge for given $E$:** $q_{max} = \sqrt{8gE^3/27}$ (occurs at $y = 2E/3 = y_c$).
- ✅ **Energy gradient (general):** $dE/dy = 1 - Q^2B/(gA^3) = 1 - Fr^2$.
- ✅ **E-y curve shape:** Asymptotic to the line $E = y$ for large $y$ (subcritical limb); passes through the origin region on the supercritical limb.
- ✅ **Subcritical flow** is **deep and slow**; **supercritical flow** is **shallow and fast**.
- ✅ **Physical significance of $y_c$:** Control section in GVF, choking limit, discharge maximization, wave propagation transition.
- ✅ **Energy at critical depth:** $E_c = y_c + V_c^2/2g = y_c + y_c/2 = 3y_c/2$ (i.e., **2/3 potential + 1/3 kinetic**).
- ✅ **Common KTU problem types:** (i) Compute $E$, $y_c$, $Fr$; (ii) Find alternate depth; (iii) Maximum discharge; (iv) Classify flow regime; (v) Derive $E_c = 3y_c/2$.
- ✅ **Default constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² (SI), $g = 32.2$ ft/s² (FPS).
- ✅ **Unit discipline:** All lengths in **m**, discharges in **m³/s**, velocities in **m/s**.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip from KTU Board Perspective:** In 14-mark Part B questions, always draw the **E-y curve sketch** (even a rough one) and mark the two alternate depths and the critical depth. This visual aid demonstrates conceptual clarity and often earns **partial marks** even if the numerical answer has minor errors. Examiners reward **process over product** in KTU evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Energy Diagram and Discharge Diagram, Critical Flow and Its Computation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specific Energy Diagram and Discharge Diagram, Critical Flow and Its Computation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Specific Energy (E)

In open channel hydraulics, the **Specific Energy** of flow in a channel section is defined as the total energy (head) of the flow measured with respect to the channel bed at the section under consideration. It is the sum of the depth of flow (potential head) and the velocity head (kinetic head) at that section, and is expressed in metres (m) of fluid.

$$E = y + \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Specific Energy of flow at the section (m)
- $y$ = Depth of flow measured vertically from the channel bed to the free water surface (m)
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow at the section (m/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The specific energy does **not** include the elevation head of the bed above an arbitrary datum. The datum is always taken at the **channel bed** itself at the section of interest. This makes $E$ a section-specific property, independent of the longitudinal slope of the channel.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bump in a River"

Imagine a wide, slow-moving river carrying a constant discharge $Q$. Now drop a smooth, fixed **bump (hump)** on the riverbed. What happens to the water surface?

- As the water approaches the bump, the cross-sectional area is reduced, so by continuity ($Q = A \cdot V$), the velocity must **increase**.
- This increase in velocity means the **kinetic energy (velocity head)** increases.
- Since the **total energy** (minus losses) is approximately constant, the **depth must drop** to compensate.
- The water surface sags downward over the bump — yet still carries the same discharge.

This trade-off between *depth* and *velocity head* is the very essence of specific energy. The total head above the bed (i.e., $E$) can be partitioned in many ways between the depth $y$ and the velocity head $V^{2}/(2g)$, and a given value of $E$ generally allows **two possible depths** (called *alternate depths*) for the same discharge — one deep and slow, the other shallow and fast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The two branches of the $E$–$y$ curve (for a given $Q$ and channel shape) correspond to:
> 1. **Subcritical flow** (Froude Number $\mathrm{Fr} < 1$) — the upper, deep, slow branch.
> 2. **Supercritical flow** (Froude Number $\mathrm{Fr} > 1$) — the lower, shallow, fast branch.
>
> The two branches meet at the **critical depth** $y_c$, which represents the minimum possible specific energy for that discharge.

## 1.3 Definition of Critical Flow

**Critical flow** is the condition in an open channel at which the specific energy is at its **minimum value** for a given discharge, or alternatively, at which the discharge is at its **maximum value** for a given specific energy. At critical flow, the dimensionless **Froude Number (Fr)** is exactly equal to **1**.

$$\mathrm{Fr} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gD}} = 1 \quad \text{(at critical flow)}$$

Where $D = A/T$ is the **hydraulic depth** (m), with $A$ = cross-sectional area of flow and $T$ = top width of the free surface.

| Flow Regime | Froude Number | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Subcritical (Tranquil) | $\mathrm{Fr} < 1$ | Deep, slow; disturbances travel upstream |
| Critical | $\mathrm{Fr} = 1$ | Threshold; minimum $E$ / maximum $Q$ |
| Supercritical (Rapid/Shooting) | $\mathrm{Fr} > 1$ | Shallow, fast; disturbances cannot travel upstream |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Critical flow is the *transition state* between subcritical and supercritical flow. It is of immense practical importance in the design of **broad-crested weirs, venturi flumes, spillways, control sections, and channel transitions** (e.g., a free overfall).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Specific Energy Equation — General Form

For any cross-section (trapezoidal, circular, etc.), the specific energy is:

$$E = y + \frac{Q^{2}}{2gA^{2}}$$

The second term, $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g} = \dfrac{Q^{2}}{2gA^{2}}$, is the velocity head. As $y$ changes, $A$ changes, and so $V$ must change to keep $Q$ constant. This gives a non-linear relationship between $E$ and $y$.

## 2.2 The Specific Energy Diagram (E–y Curve) for a Rectangular Channel

For a wide rectangular channel of width $b$, with discharge $Q$ and discharge per unit width $q = Q/b$:

$$E = y + \frac{q^{2}}{2gy^{2}}$$

Plotting $E$ (y-axis) against $y$ (x-axis) at constant $Q$ (or $q$), the curve has the following properties:

- The curve is **asymptotic to the line $E = y$** for very large $y$ (where velocity head is negligible).
- The curve is **asymptotic to the y-axis ($y = 0$)** for very small $y$ (where velocity head $\to \infty$).
- The curve has a **single minimum point** at the critical depth $y_c$.
- For any $E > E_{\min}$, there are **two depths** (alternate depths): one subcritical and one supercritical.
- The lower limb (supercritical) is **steep**; the upper limb (subcritical) is **flat**.

## 2.3 Conditions for Minimum Specific Energy (Critical Depth)

Differentiating $E$ with respect to $y$ (at constant $Q$) and setting $\dfrac{dE}{dy} = 0$:

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{Q^{2}}{gA^{3}} \cdot \frac{dA}{dy} = 0$$

Since $\dfrac{dA}{dy} = T$ (top width), this becomes:

$$1 - \frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 1$$

This is the **general condition for critical flow**. In terms of the Froude Number:

$$\mathrm{Fr}^{2} = \frac{V^{2}}{gD} = \frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{Fr} = 1$$

## 2.4 Critical Depth for a Rectangular Channel

For a rectangular channel, $A = by$, $T = b$, so $A^{3}/T = b^{2}y^{3}$. Substituting:

$$y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}$$

Also:
$$V_{c} = \frac{q}{y_{c}} = \sqrt{g\,y_{c}}, \qquad E_{\min} = \frac{3}{2}\,y_{c} = y_{c} + \frac{V_{c}^{2}}{2g} = y_{c} + \frac{y_{c}}{2} = \frac{3y_{c}}{2}$$

## 2.5 The Discharge Diagram (q–y Curve)

For a given specific energy $E$, the discharge per unit width is:

$$q = y\sqrt{2g\,(E - y)}$$

The $q$–$y$ curve (at constant $E$) starts and ends at $q = 0$ (at $y = 0$ and $y = E$), reaching a **maximum** at $y = E/3$ (i.e., at the critical depth). For a given $E$, two depths are possible, but the **maximum discharge** occurs at the **critical depth** $y_c = 2E/3$.

## 2.6 Critical Slope ($S_c$)

The **critical slope** is the bed slope at which, for a given discharge and a given roughness, the flow is exactly critical (uniform flow depth = critical depth). It is computed by equating the Manning/Chézy equation for uniform flow to the critical flow condition.

Using Manning's equation for uniform flow: $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S_{0}^{1/2}$

And critical condition: $V_{c} = \sqrt{g\,A_c/T_c}$

Equating yields:

$$S_{c} = \frac{g\,n^{2}\,A_{c}^{2}}{T_{c}^{2}\,R_{c}^{4/3}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $S_0 < S_c$ → Mild slope → subcritical uniform flow
> - $S_0 > S_c$ → Steep slope → supercritical uniform flow
> - $S_0 = S_c$ → Critical slope → critical uniform flow
> - $S_0 = 0$ → Horizontal slope

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = y + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Specific energy (general) |
| 2 | $E = y + \dfrac{Q^{2}}{2gA^{2}}$ | Specific energy in terms of $Q$ |
| 3 | $E = y + \dfrac{q^{2}}{2gy^{2}}$ | Specific energy for rectangular channel |
| 4 | $\dfrac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 1$ | General critical flow condition |
| 5 | $y_{c} = \left(\dfrac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}$ | Critical depth — rectangular channel |
| 6 | $V_{c} = \sqrt{g\,y_{c}}$ | Critical velocity — rectangular channel |
| 7 | $E_{\min} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,y_{c}$ | Minimum specific energy — rectangular |
| 8 | $\mathrm{Fr} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gD}} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gA/T}}$ | Froude number (general) |
| 9 | $q = y\sqrt{2g(E-y)}$ | Discharge (rectangular) at given $E$ |
| 10 | $y_{c} = \dfrac{2}{3}E$ | Critical depth for given $E$ (rectangular) |
| 11 | $q_{\max} = \dfrac{2}{3}E\sqrt{\dfrac{2gE}{3}}$ | Max discharge for given $E$ (rectangular) |
| 12 | $S_{c} = \dfrac{g\,n^{2}\,A_{c}^{2}}{T_{c}^{2}\,R_{c}^{4/3}}$ | Critical slope (Manning) |
| 13 | $E = \dfrac{y_{c}}{2}\left(2 + \dfrac{y}{y_{c}}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{...}{...}$ *(general non-rect.)* | (Used for numerical solution) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict LaTeX Rule for Tables:** All absolute-value bars in this table are written as `\vert` to keep the markdown table syntax intact. The vertical pipe character `|` is reserved exclusively for cell separators.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Spillways and Weirs:** Critical depth occurs at the crest — used to compute discharge.
- **Channel Transitions:** A hump can choke supercritical flow (below $y_c$, $E$ rises again).
- **Free Overfall:** The brink depth is approximately $0.715\,y_c$ (end-depth concept).
- **Venturi Flumes & Parshall Flumes:** Use critical flow as a control for flow measurement.
- **Sluice Gates:** The gate opening sets the supercritical conjugate; specific energy relations predict the upstream depth (subcritical side).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Condition for Minimum Specific Energy (General)

Starting with the general specific energy equation:

$$E = y + \frac{Q^{2}}{2gA^{2}}$$

Differentiate $E$ with respect to $y$, treating $Q$ as a constant:

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 + \frac{Q^{2}}{2g} \cdot \frac{d}{dy}\left(A^{-2}\right) = 1 + \frac{Q^{2}}{2g}\left(-2A^{-3}\frac{dA}{dy}\right)$$

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{Q^{2}}{gA^{3}}\cdot\frac{dA}{dy}$$

Now, $\dfrac{dA}{dy} = T$ (since for an infinitesimal rise $dy$ in the water surface, the wetted area increases by a strip of width $T$ and height $dy$).

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}}$$

For a **minimum** of $E$, we set $\dfrac{dE}{dy} = 0$:

$$1 - \frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 1\;}$$

This is the **general critical flow condition**, valid for any channel cross-section.

### Substituting $V = Q/A$ and $D = A/T$:

$$\frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = \frac{V^{2}A^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = \frac{V^{2}}{gA/T} = \frac{V^{2}}{gD} = \mathrm{Fr}^{2} = 1$$

Therefore $\mathrm{Fr} = 1$ at the critical state — proven.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Depth for a Rectangular Channel

For a rectangular channel of width $b$: $A = by$, $T = b$, $q = Q/b$.

Substitute into the critical flow condition:

$$\frac{Q^{2} \cdot b}{g \cdot (by)^{3}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q^{2}}{g\,b^{2}\,y^{3}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{q^{2}}{g\,y^{3}} = 1$$

Solving for $y$:

$$y^{3} = \frac{q^{2}}{g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\;y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{Q^{2}}{g\,b^{2}}\right)^{1/3}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Velocity and $E_{\min}$ (Rectangular)

At critical depth, $V_c = \dfrac{q}{y_c} = \dfrac{q}{(q^{2}/g)^{1/3}} = \dfrac{q^{2/3}\cdot g^{1/3}}{q^{2/3}} = g^{1/3} q^{1/3}$.

Squaring: $V_c^{2} = g^{1/2\cdot 2} \cdot q^{2/3} = g^{2/3} q^{2/3} = g \cdot y_c$.

$$\boxed{\;V_{c} = \sqrt{g\,y_{c}}\;}$$

Now, the velocity head at critical depth:

$$\frac{V_{c}^{2}}{2g} = \frac{g\,y_{c}}{2g} = \frac{y_{c}}{2}$$

Therefore the minimum specific energy:

$$E_{\min} = y_{c} + \frac{V_{c}^{2}}{2g} = y_{c} + \frac{y_{c}}{2} = \frac{3y_{c}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{\min} = \frac{3}{2}\,y_{c} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y_{c} = \frac{2}{3}\,E_{\min}\;}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Discharge for Given Specific Energy (Rectangular)

From $E = y + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$, we have $V = \sqrt{2g(E-y)}$, and $q = yV$:

$$q = y\sqrt{2g(E-y)} = \sqrt{2g}\,y\,(E-y)^{1/2}$$

Differentiate $q$ with respect to $y$, setting $\dfrac{dq}{dy} = 0$:

$$\frac{dq}{dy} = \sqrt{2g}\left[(E-y)^{1/2} + y\cdot\frac{1}{2}(E-y)^{-1/2}(-1)\right] = 0$$

$$(E-y)^{1/2} - \frac{y}{2(E-y)^{1/2}} = 0$$

Multiply both sides by $2(E-y)^{1/2}$:

$$2(E-y) - y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2E - 2y - y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3y = 2E$$

$$\boxed{\;y = \frac{2E}{3}\;}$$

This is the depth at which $q$ is maximum — it is exactly the **critical depth** $y_c$. Substituting back:

$$q_{\max} = \frac{2E}{3}\sqrt{2g\left(E - \frac{2E}{3}\right)} = \frac{2E}{3}\sqrt{\frac{2gE}{3}} = \frac{2E}{3}\sqrt{\frac{2gE}{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\;q_{\max} = \frac{2E}{3}\sqrt{\frac{2gE}{3}} = E^{3/2}\sqrt{\frac{8g}{27}}\;}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Key Flow Relations at a Glance

| Flow State | Depth | Velocity Head | Specific Energy | Froude No. | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Subcritical (upper limb) | $y > y_c$ | $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g} < \dfrac{y_c}{2}$ | $E > \dfrac{3y_c}{2}$ | $\mathrm{Fr} < 1$ | Mild slope, deep & slow |
| **Critical** | $y = y_c$ | $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g} = \dfrac{y_c}{2}$ | $E = \dfrac{3y_c}{2} = E_{\min}$ | $\mathrm{Fr} = 1$ | Threshold; control section |
| Supercritical (lower limb) | $y < y_c$ | $\dfrac{V^{2}}{2g} > \dfrac{y_c}{2}$ | $E > \dfrac{3y_c}{2}$ | $\mathrm{Fr} > 1$ | Steep slope, shallow & fast |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Board Exams:** Both alternate depths (sub- and supercritical) share the **same specific energy** for a given $Q$. The two depths are *conjugate on the E–y diagram*, not to be confused with *conjugate depths* in a hydraulic jump (which is a momentum-based relation across the jump).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Specific Energy (E–y) Curve for a Rectangular Channel

The following flowchart-block diagram maps out the shape, asymptotes, and key points of the E–y diagram. It is rendered using a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** because the physical curve is best described topologically.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef axis fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef limb fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef crit fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef asym fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#7E22CE,color:#581C87

    A["ASYMPTOTE: E = y line (very large y, V approx 0)"]:::asym
    B["UPPER LIMB: Subcritical flow branch, y greater than yc, slope gentle, Fr less than 1"]:::limb
    C["CRITICAL POINT: y = yc, E = Emin = 1.5 yc, Fr = 1, minimum specific energy"]:::crit
    D["LOWER LIMB: Supercritical flow branch, y less than yc, slope steep, Fr greater than 1"]:::limb
    E["ASYMPTOTE: y = 0 axis (very small y, V head tends to infinity)"]:::asym
    F["X-AXIS: Depth of flow y (m)"]:::axis
    G["Y-AXIS: Specific Energy E (m)"]:::axis
    H["ALTERNATE DEPTHS: For any E greater than Emin, two depths y1 and y2 exist: y1 greater than yc greater than y2"]:::crit

    F --- G
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    C --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows that the E–y curve is U-shaped (in fact, asymmetric), opening upward with a sharp minimum at the critical point. The two limbs represent subcritical and supercritical flows, and the asymptotes capture the limiting behaviour at very large and very small depths.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Discharge (q–y) Curve at Constant Specific Energy E

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef axis fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef endpt fill:#FECACA,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef peak fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef limb fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F

    P1["START: y = 0, q = 0 (no flow depth)"]:::endpt
    P2["RISING LIMB: y less than 2E/3, supercritical side, q increasing with y"]:::limb
    P3["PEAK: y = 2E/3 = yc, q = qmax = E^1.5 * sqrt of 8g/27, critical condition Fr = 1"]:::peak
    P4["FALLING LIMB: y greater than 2E/3, subcritical side, q decreasing with y"]:::limb
    P5["END: y = E, q = 0 (depth equals specific energy, velocity head zero)"]:::endpt
    P6["X-AXIS: Depth y (m)"]:::axis
    P7["Y-AXIS: Discharge per unit width q (m^2/s)"]:::axis

    P6 --- P7
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Classification of Flow Regimes & Critical Slope

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef mild fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef crit fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#CA8A04,color:#713F12
    classDef steep fill:#FECACA,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef horiz fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#7E22CE,color:#581C87
    classDef adverse fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#9F1239,color:#881337

    R["GIVEN: A CHANNEL WITH DISCHARGE Q, ROUGHNESS n, AND SLOPE S0"]:::root
    S1["S0 less than Sc: MILD SLOPE: Normal depth yn greater than yc, flow is SUBCRITICAL, Fr less than 1, y-yc upward branch"]:::mild
    S2["S0 equals Sc: CRITICAL SLOPE: Normal depth yn equals yc, flow is CRITICAL, Fr equals 1, E equals Emin"]:::crit
    S3["S0 greater than Sc: STEEP SLOPE: Normal depth yn less than yc, flow is SUPERCRITICAL, Fr greater than 1, y-yc downward branch"]:::steep
    S4["S0 equals 0: HORIZONTAL SLOPE: Subcritical uniform flow dominates"]:::horiz
    S5["S0 less than 0: ADVERSE SLOPE: No uniform flow; flow decelerates downstream"]:::adverse

    R --> S1
    R --> S2
    R --> S3
    R --> S4
    R --> S5
```

## 4.4 Schematic Table — Comparison of E–y and q–y Diagrams

| Feature | E–y Diagram (constant Q) | q–y Diagram (constant E) |
|---|---|---|
| Variable on Y-axis | Specific Energy $E$ (m) | Discharge $q$ (m²/s) |
| Variable on X-axis | Depth $y$ (m) | Depth $y$ (m) |
| Curve shape | Asymmetric U (open upward) | Inverted parabola-like (single peak) |
| Extremum | **Minimum** at $y = y_c$ | **Maximum** at $y = 2E/3 = y_c$ |
| Two depths for given $E$/$Q$ | Yes — alternate depths | Yes — two possible $q$ values for given $y$ |
| Asymptotes | $E = y$ and $y$-axis | $q = 0$ at $y=0$ and $y=E$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Question A.1 (3 Marks)
**Define specific energy and state the condition for critical flow in terms of specific energy.**

**Model Answer:**

The **specific energy** $E$ at a section of an open channel is the total head (energy per unit weight) of the flow measured with respect to the channel bed at that section. It is given by:

$$E = y + \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

The condition for **critical flow** is that the specific energy is at its **minimum value** for the given discharge $Q$. Mathematically:

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 1 \quad \text{or} \quad \mathrm{Fr} = 1$$

At this minimum, the depth equals the **critical depth** $y_c$, and the flow regime changes from subcritical (above) to supercritical (below).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for stating the condition dE/dy = 0 / Fr = 1.)*

### Question A.2 (3 Marks)
**What are alternate depths? How are they related to critical depth on the E–y diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

For a given discharge $Q$ and a given value of specific energy $E > E_{\min}$, the E–y curve yields **two possible depths** of flow at the same section. These two depths are called **alternate depths**.

- The **upper alternate depth** $y_1 > y_c$ corresponds to **subcritical flow** ($\mathrm{Fr} < 1$).
- The **lower alternate depth** $y_2 < y_c$ corresponds to **supercritical flow** ($\mathrm{Fr} > 1$).
- Both depths share the **same specific energy** $E$ and lie symmetrically (in the $E$–$y$ sense, not geometrically) on either side of the **critical depth** $y_c$, which is the unique depth at which $E = E_{\min}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for definition, identification of sub/supercritical branches, and relation to $y_c$.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### ⭐ Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Derive the condition for minimum specific energy in an open channel of any cross-section, and show that the Froude number at this condition equals unity.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A rectangular channel of width **4 m** carries a discharge of **16 m³/s**. Compute the **critical depth**, the **critical velocity**, and the **minimum specific energy**. Also, determine the specific energy of the flow if the actual depth of flow is **2.0 m**. Comment on the flow regime.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Start with the general specific energy equation:**

$$E = y + \frac{Q^{2}}{2gA^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $y$ at constant $Q$:**

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 + \frac{Q^{2}}{2g} \cdot \frac{d}{dy}\left(A^{-2}\right) = 1 - \frac{Q^{2}}{gA^{3}} \cdot \frac{dA}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Use $dA/dy = T$:**

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Set $dE/dy = 0$ for minimum:**

$$\frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of $V$ and $D$:**

Since $V = Q/A$ and $D = A/T$:

$$\frac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = \frac{V^{2}A^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = \frac{V^{2}}{g \cdot A/T} = \frac{V^{2}}{gD} = \mathrm{Fr}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Therefore:**

$$\mathrm{Fr}^{2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\mathrm{Fr} = 1} \quad \text{(at minimum specific energy)}$$

*(7 marks: 1 mark for starting eq., 1 for differentiation, 1 for dA/dy = T, 1 for setting dE/dy = 0, 2 for Froude number derivation, 1 for the conclusion.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $b = 4$ m, $Q = 16$ m³/s, $y_{actual} = 2.0$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Discharge per unit width:**

$$q = \frac{Q}{b} = \frac{16}{4} = 4 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Critical depth:**

$$y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{4^{2}}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (1.63099)^{1/3}$$

$$\boxed{y_{c} \approx 1.176 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Critical velocity:**

$$V_{c} = \sqrt{g\,y_{c}} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 1.176} = \sqrt{11.536} \approx 3.396 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum specific energy:**

$$E_{\min} = \frac{3}{2}\,y_{c} = 1.5 \times 1.176 = \boxed{1.764 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — Actual specific energy at $y = 2.0$ m:**

First, actual area: $A = b \cdot y = 4 \times 2.0 = 8.0$ m².

Actual velocity: $V = Q/A = 16 / 8.0 = 2.0$ m/s.

Velocity head: $V^{2}/(2g) = (2.0)^{2}/(2 \times 9.81) = 4.0/19.62 = 0.2039$ m.

$$E_{actual} = y + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} = 2.0 + 0.2039 = \boxed{2.204 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on flow regime:**

Since $y_{actual} = 2.0$ m $> y_c = 1.176$ m, the flow is on the **upper limb** of the E–y curve, i.e., the flow is **SUBCRITICAL** ($\mathrm{Fr} < 1$).

Check: $D = A/T = 8.0/4 = 2.0$ m. $\mathrm{Fr} = V/\sqrt{gD} = 2.0/\sqrt{9.81 \times 2.0} = 2.0/4.429 = 0.452 < 1$ ✓.

*(7 marks: 1 for q, 1 for yc, 1 for Vc, 1 for Emin, 2 for E_actual computation, 1 for the comment.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* For a rectangular channel, derive expressions for: (i) critical depth, (ii) critical velocity, and (iii) minimum specific energy in terms of the discharge per unit width $q$. State all assumptions.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A discharge of **6 m³/s** flows in a rectangular channel of width **3 m** with a depth of **0.8 m**. Compute the **specific energy** and the **Froude number**. Determine the **alternate depth** for this specific energy, and state whether the alternate depth represents sub- or supercritical flow.

#### Model Solution for (a)

For a rectangular channel, $A = by$, $T = b$, $q = Q/b$. The specific energy is:

$$E = y + \frac{q^{2}}{2gy^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate w.r.t. $y$:**

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^{2}}{gy^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Set to zero:**

$$1 = \frac{q^{2}}{gy_{c}^{3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{c}^{3} = \frac{q^{2}}{g}$$

**Step 3 — Critical depth:**

$$\boxed{y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}}$$

**Step 4 — Critical velocity:**

$$V_{c} = \frac{q}{y_{c}} = \frac{q}{(q^{2}/g)^{1/3}} = q^{1-2/3} \cdot g^{1/3} = q^{1/3} g^{1/3}$$

Squaring: $V_{c}^{2} = (qg)^{2/3} = g \cdot (q^{2}/g)^{1/3} = g \cdot y_{c}$

$$\boxed{V_{c} = \sqrt{g\,y_{c}}}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum specific energy:**

$$\frac{V_{c}^{2}}{2g} = \frac{y_{c}}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{\min} = y_{c} + \frac{y_{c}}{2} = \frac{3y_{c}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\min} = \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}}$$

**Assumptions:** Rectangular cross-section, prismatic channel, hydrostatic pressure distribution, no air entrainment, $g = 9.81$ m/s² (assumed constant).

*(7 marks: 2 for yc, 2 for Vc, 2 for Emin, 1 for assumptions.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $Q = 6$ m³/s, $b = 3$ m, $y_1 = 0.8$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Discharge per unit width:**

$$q = Q/b = 6/3 = 2 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Actual velocity:**

$$A = b y_1 = 3 \times 0.8 = 2.4 \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$V_1 = Q/A = 6/2.4 = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Specific energy:**

$$\frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} = \frac{(2.5)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 0.3186 \text{ m}$$

$$E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} = 0.8 + 0.3186 = \boxed{1.1186 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Froude number:**

$$D_1 = A/T = 2.4/3 = 0.8 \text{ m}$$
$$\mathrm{Fr}_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{gD_1}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.8}} = \frac{2.5}{2.8014} = 0.892$$

Since $\mathrm{Fr}_1 = 0.892 < 1$, the flow is **SUBCRITICAL**.

**Step 5 — Critical depth (for reference):**

$$y_c = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{4}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (0.4077)^{1/3} \approx 0.7405 \text{ m}$$

Check: $y_1 = 0.8$ m $> y_c = 0.7405$ m → Subcritical ✓.

**Step 6 — Alternate depth $y_2$ (supercritical):**

Since $E_1 = E_2 = 1.1186$ m, the alternate depth satisfies:

$$E = y_2 + \frac{q^{2}}{2g\,y_2^{2}}$$

$$1.1186 = y_2 + \frac{(2)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times y_2^{2}} = y_2 + \frac{4}{19.62 \cdot y_2^{2}} = y_2 + \frac{0.20387}{y_2^{2}}$$

Multiply through by $y_2^{2}$:

$$1.1186 \cdot y_2^{2} = y_2^{3} + 0.20387$$

$$y_2^{3} - 1.1186 \cdot y_2^{2} + 0.20387 = 0$$

Solve by trial (supercritical, so $y_2 < y_c \approx 0.7405$):

Try $y_2 = 0.50$: $0.125 - 0.2797 + 0.20387 = 0.0492$ (positive, try smaller)

Try $y_2 = 0.45$: $0.0911 - 0.2265 + 0.20387 = 0.0685$ (positive, try smaller)

Try $y_2 = 0.40$: $0.0640 - 0.1790 + 0.20387 = 0.0889$ (still positive)

Try $y_2 = 0.30$: $0.027 - 0.1007 + 0.20387 = 0.1302$ (positive)

Hmm — the cubic has only one real root in the range. Let's re-check the calculation.

**Recheck:** $E_1 = 0.8 + 0.3186 = 1.1186$ m. Critical $E_{min} = 1.5 \times 0.7405 = 1.1108$ m. So $E_1 > E_{min}$ ✓ — alternate depth must exist.

Let me recompute the cubic more carefully. The cubic form is:

$$y_2^{3} - 1.1186 y_2^{2} + 0.20387 = 0$$

For $y_2$ in supercritical range $y_2 < y_c = 0.7405$ m:

Try $y_2 = 0.70$: $0.343 - 0.5481 + 0.20387 = -0.0012$ ≈ 0 ✓

So $y_2 \approx 0.70$ m. Let me refine: Try $y_2 = 0.701$: $0.3446 - 0.5497 + 0.20387 = -0.0013$. Try $y_2 = 0.699$: $0.3415 - 0.5467 + 0.20387 = -0.0013$. So the root is very close to $0.70$ m.

Verify directly: at $y_2 = 0.70$ m, $V_2 = q/y_2 = 2/0.70 = 2.857$ m/s, $V_2^{2}/(2g) = 8.163/19.62 = 0.4161$ m, $E_2 = 0.70 + 0.4161 = 1.1161$ m ≈ $E_1 = 1.1186$ m ✓ (small rounding).

**More precisely:** Try $y_2 = 0.696$: $V_2 = 2.874$ m/s, $V_2^{2}/(2g) = 8.26/19.62 = 0.4210$, $E_2 = 0.696 + 0.4210 = 1.1170$ m.

Try $y_2 = 0.694$: $V_2 = 2.882$ m/s, $V_2^{2}/(2g) = 8.305/19.62 = 0.4233$, $E_2 = 0.694 + 0.4233 = 1.1173$ m.

Try $y_2 = 0.693$: $V_2 = 2.886$ m/s, $V_2^{2}/(2g) = 8.330/19.62 = 0.4245$, $E_2 = 0.693 + 0.4245 = 1.1175$ m.

Try $y_2 = 0.690$: $V_2 = 2.899$ m/s, $V_2^{2}/(2g) = 8.404/19.62 = 0.4283$, $E_2 = 0.690 + 0.4283 = 1.1183$ m ≈ 1.1186 m ✓.

$$\boxed{y_2 \approx 0.69 \text{ m (supercritical alternate depth)}}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $y_2 = 0.69$ m $< y_c = 0.7405$ m, the alternate depth is in the **supercritical regime**.

*(7 marks: 1 for q, 1 for E1, 1 for Fr, 1 for yc check, 2 for alternate depth cubic, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $q$ from m²/s when substituting in formulas.
> 2. **Misidentifying the regime:** Comparing the wrong depth with $y_c$ — always state clearly that the *given* depth vs. *critical* depth comparison drives the regime.
> 3. **Forgetting the second root:** The cubic for alternate depth has up to three real roots; physically, only the one in the appropriate range (sub- or supercritical) is valid. KTU evaluators often deduct 1–2 marks if the student does not explicitly verify which regime the alternate depth belongs to.
> 4. **In the E–y diagram question:** Students often draw a *symmetric* U-curve — it is **asymmetric**! The supercritical limb is much steeper than the subcritical limb. Drawing it symmetric loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Sign error in dE/dy:** The velocity-head derivative yields a **negative** sign that cancels the leading 1; missing this leads to an incorrect critical condition. Always show the step $d(A^{-2})/dy = -2A^{-3}(dA/dy)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Specific Energy & Critical Flow):**

- ✅ **Specific energy** $E$ is measured **above the channel bed**, not above a horizontal datum. Its unit is **metres** of fluid.
- ✅ General form: $E = y + Q^{2}/(2gA^{2})$. For rectangular: $E = y + q^{2}/(2gy^{2})$.
- ✅ **E–y diagram:** Asymmetric U-curve with asymptotes $E = y$ (large $y$) and $y = 0$ axis (small $y$). Lower limb = supercritical, upper limb = subcritical.
- ✅ **General critical condition:** $Q^{2}T/(gA^{3}) = 1$, equivalent to $\mathrm{Fr} = 1$.
- ✅ **Rectangular critical depth:** $y_c = (q^{2}/g)^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Critical velocity (rectangular):** $V_c = \sqrt{g\,y_c}$.
- ✅ **Minimum specific energy (rectangular):** $E_{\min} = (3/2)\,y_c$, equivalently $y_c = (2/3)E_{\min}$.
- ✅ **Alternate depths:** Two depths sharing the same $E$ for a given $Q$ — one above $y_c$ (subcritical) and one below $y_c$ (supercritical).
- ✅ **Discharge (q–y) diagram at constant $E$:** Inverted parabolic curve with peak $q_{\max}$ at $y = 2E/3 = y_c$. $q_{\max} = (2E/3)\sqrt{2gE/3}$.
- ✅ **Critical slope $S_c$** is the slope at which normal flow depth equals critical depth; for $S_0 < S_c$ → mild (subcritical); $S_0 > S_c$ → steep (supercritical); $S_0 = 0$ → horizontal; $S_0 < 0$ → adverse.
- ✅ **Froude number:** $\mathrm{Fr} = V/\sqrt{gD}$ where $D = A/T$. $\mathrm{Fr} < 1$: subcritical; $\mathrm{Fr} = 1$: critical; $\mathrm{Fr} > 1$: supercritical.
- ✅ **Energy check at $y_c$:** At critical depth, **velocity head = $y_c/2$** and **depth = $y_c$**, giving $E_{\min} = 1.5\,y_c$ — a frequent 2-mark sub-question in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Free overfall** brink depth ≈ $0.715\,y_c$ (not part of the E–y derivation but related to the critical control).
- ✅ For non-rectangular sections, $y_c$ is found by trial from $Q^{2}T_c/(gA_c^{3}) = 1$ — KTU frequently gives such a numerical problem.
- ✅ **Mnemonic — "CDE":** **C**ritical, **D**epth, **E**nergy minimum — critical state = minimum specific energy for a given discharge.
- ✅ **Mnemonic — "MAX":** **MA**ximum discharge at critical depth for a given specific energy.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Note for KTU 2024 Students:**
> The E–y and q–y diagrams form the analytical backbone of *gradually varied flow* in later modules. A clear grasp of *alternate depths*, *critical depth*, and the *Froude number* is non-negotiable for both Module 2 numericals and the broader course. Practice at least 3 rectangular + 2 trapezoidal problems before the exam. Best of luck!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Exponents, Application of Specific Energy for Channel Transitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Exponents, Application of Specific Energy for Channel Transitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydraulic Exponents — Formal Definition

**Hydraulic Exponents** are dimensionless numerical parameters that express the rate of change of hydraulic quantities (area, wetted perimeter, conveyance, Chezy's coefficient, Manning's n) with respect to the depth of flow in a non-rectangular open channel. They are extensively used in:

1. Direct computation of **Critical Depth ($y_c$)** for non-rectangular sections.
2. Solution of **Gradually Varied Flow (GVF)** profiles by direct-integration methods.
3. Simplification of energy-momentum equations in **channel transitions**.

The principal hydraulic exponents used in KTU-level Open Channel Hydraulics are:

| Exponent | Associated Quantity | Formula Basis |
|----------|--------------------|--------------|
| $M$ | Chezy's Coefficient ($C$) | $C \propto R^M$ |
| $N$ | Manning's Coefficient ($K = 1/n$) | $K \propto R^N$ |
| $P$ | Wetted Perimeter ($P$) | $P \propto y^P$ |
| $X$ | Specific Energy Exponent | Used in $y_c$ eqn. |
| $Y$ | Critical Depth Exponent | Used in $y_c$ eqn. |
| $Z$ | Conveyance Exponent | Section factor $K \propto y^Z$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must master the determination of $M$, $N$, and the Critical Depth Exponents ($X$, $Y$, $Z$) using logarithmic plotting of channel geometry data, and apply the **Bakhmeteff / Lindquist** equations for critical depth.

## 1.2 Application of Specific Energy for Channel Transitions

**Channel Transition** is any change in the cross-section, shape, slope, or alignment of an open channel. The **Specific Energy ($E$)** principle, when applied across a transition, governs:

- The **upstream and downstream depths** in a smooth/well-behaved transition.
- The **minimum energy required** to convey a given discharge.
- The detection of **choking** (when $E_{available} < E_{critical}$ at the throat).
- The **backwater profile** caused by the transition.

A transition is classified as:
- **Stepped (abrupt) transition** — sudden change in bed level (e.g., hump, drop, sluice gate).
- **Ramped transition** — gradual change in bed level.
- **Width contraction / expansion** — change in channel width.
- **Combined transitions** — simultaneous change in width and bed elevation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Hump in a River:**
> 
> Imagine water flowing in a smooth river. If a submerged boulder (a *hump*) suddenly appears at the bed, the water momentarily speeds up over the hump, just like a person climbs a small speed-breaker. The water surface *dips* over the hump. This "dipping" is governed purely by **Specific Energy** — the total head (depth + velocity head) must remain constant if there's no friction loss.
> 
> **Hydraulic Exponents** are like the "personality traits" of a channel shape. A narrow, deep rectangular channel behaves very differently from a wide, shallow trapezoidal one. The exponents mathematically capture *how* a channel's area, perimeter, and conveyance scale with depth. They are the "DNA" of the cross-section.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Conventions

- Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$**
- Specific weight of water: **$\gamma_w = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$**
- Density of water: **$\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$**
- All depths in **metres (m)**, discharges in **$\text{m}^3/\text{s}$**, areas in **$\text{m}^2$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concept of Hydraulic Exponents — Structured Logic

For a non-rectangular channel, the geometric and hydraulic properties do not scale linearly with depth. We express them as power functions of depth $y$:

$$
A = \alpha_1 \, y^{b_1}, \quad T = \alpha_3 \, y^{b_3}, \quad P = \alpha_2 \, y^{b_2}
$$

Where $\alpha_1, \alpha_3, \alpha_2$ are constants and $b_1, b_3, b_2$ are the **shape exponents** of the channel.

The hydraulic exponents are derived by taking logarithmic derivatives of key equations:

### 2.1.1 Manning's Exponent ($N$)

Conveyance in Manning's formula: $K = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3}$
The exponent $N$ is defined such that:
$$
K \propto R^N
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a wide rectangular channel, $N = 1$ (since $A R^{2/3} \propto y \cdot y^{2/3} = y^{5/3}$ and $R \propto y$, so $K \propto R^{5/3}$, but for power-law definition: $K = C \cdot R^N$ where $N \approx 5/3 \times \text{(constant)}$). The general relationship is **$N = 2y / (1 + 2y)$** where $y$ is the shape exponent.

### 2.1.2 Chezy's Exponent ($M$)

Chezy's coefficient $C$ varies with hydraulic radius $R$ and roughness as:
$$
C = k_1 \, R^M
$$

Where $M$ depends on the **roughness coefficient**, the **Reynolds Number**, and the **relative roughness**. For practical KTU problems:

| Channel Condition | Typical Value of $M$ |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| Very smooth | $M \approx 0.50$ |
| Normal rough channels | $M \approx 0.17$ to $0.30$ |
| Very rough (mountain streams) | $M \approx -0.10$ to $0.05$ |

### 2.1.3 Critical Depth Exponents ($X$, $Y$, $Z$)

For a channel where $A = \alpha_1 y^{b_1}$, $T = \alpha_3 y^{b_3}$, $P = \alpha_2 y^{b_2}$, the critical flow equation $Q^2 \, T / g = A^3$ yields:

$$
y_c = \left( \frac{Q^2 \, g^{-1} \, \alpha_3}{\alpha_1^3} \right)^{\frac{1}{3b_1 - b_3}}
$$

Defining the exponent $\phi$:
$$
\phi = \frac{1}{3b_1 - b_3}
$$

And relating to $X$, $Y$, $Z$ via the conveyance $K$ at critical depth:
$$
K_c = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{S_c}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_c = A_c R_c^{2/3} / n
$$

The critical slope $S_c$ becomes:
$$
S_c = \frac{Q^2 \, n^2}{A_c^2 \, R_c^{4/3}}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $E = y + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = y + \dfrac{Q^2}{2gA^2}$ | Specific Energy |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \dfrac{Q^2 \, T}{gA^3} = 0$ | Critical flow condition |
| 3 | $\dfrac{A_c^3}{T_c} = \dfrac{Q^2}{g}$ | Critical depth equation (alternative form) |
| 4 | $y_c = \left( \dfrac{Q^2}{g \, T_c} \right)^{1/3}$ — Rectangular | Critical depth for rectangular section |
| 5 | $y_c = \left( \dfrac{q^2}{g} \right)^{1/3}$ | Critical depth (rectangular, $q = Q/b$) |
| 6 | $E_c = y_c + \dfrac{y_c}{2} = \dfrac{3}{2} y_c$ | Min. specific energy (rectangular) |
| 7 | $y_c = \left( \dfrac{Q^2}{g} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha_3}{\alpha_1^3} \right)^{1/(3b_1 - b_3)}$ | General critical depth |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{2y}{1 + 2y}$ | Manning's exponent (from shape exponent $y$) |
| 9 | $M \approx 0.17$ to $0.30$ | Chezy's exponent for normal channels |
| 10 | $C = k_1 R^M$ | Chezy coefficient (hydraulic exponent form) |
| 11 | $S_c = \dfrac{Q^2 n^2}{A_c^2 R_c^{4/3}}$ | Critical slope (Manning) |
| 12 | $K = \dfrac{1}{n} A R^{2/3}$ | Conveyance |
| 13 | $E_2 = E_1 \pm \Delta z$ (no friction) | Energy equation through transition |
| 14 | $E_1 = E_2 + h_L$ (with friction) | Energy equation with head loss |
| 15 | $\Delta y = E_1 - E_2$ at $\Delta z = 0$ | Depth change for width transition (no hump) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** Never confuse **hydraulic exponent $N$** (used in GVF and conveyance) with **Manning's $n$** (roughness coefficient). The $N$ in hydraulic exponents is dimensionless and > 1; Manning's $n$ has units of $\text{m}^{-1/3} \text{s}$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydraulic exponents and specific energy transitions are foundational in:

1. **Spillway design:** Computing crest profiles and energy dissipators.
2. **Sluice gate operation:** Determining supercritical depth $y_2$ from subcritical $y_1$ (and vice versa).
3. **Channel contractions** (e.g., bridge piers, flumes): Checking for **choking**.
4. **Sewer and irrigation networks:** Ensuring gravity flow without choking.
5. **Hydropower tunnels:** Transition from free-surface to pressurized flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Manning's Hydraulic Exponent ($N$)

### Starting Point — Manning's Equation

Manning's formula for discharge is:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
Q^2 = \frac{1}{n^2} A^2 R^{4/3} S
$$

### Relating Area and Perimeter via Shape Exponent

For a channel where $A \propto y^{b_1}$ and $P \propto y^{b_2}$:

$$
R = \frac{A}{P} \propto \frac{y^{b_1}}{y^{b_2}} = y^{b_1 - b_2}
$$

Let the shape exponent of $R$ with respect to $y$ be:

$$
R = k_0 \, y^{y_s} \quad \text{where} \quad y_s = b_1 - b_2
$$

### Definition of $N$

The hydraulic exponent $N$ is defined by the relation between conveyance and $R$:

$$
K = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} = C_R \cdot R^N
$$

We can write $A = R \cdot P$, so:

$$
K = \frac{1}{n} R \cdot P \cdot R^{2/3} = \frac{1}{n} P \cdot R^{5/3}
$$

Now $P \propto R^{1/y_s}$ (since $R = k_0 y^{y_s}$ implies $y = (R/k_0)^{1/y_s}$ and $P \propto y^{b_2} \propto R^{b_2/y_s}$). 

For a wide rectangular channel: $b_1 = 1$, $b_2 = 1$, so $y_s = 0$ — invalid for this derivation. We therefore use a general channel where $P \propto R^p$ with $p = b_2/y_s$.

$$
K \propto R^{p + 5/3}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = p + \frac{5}{3}}
$$

For typical channel shapes (where the shape exponent $y$ — the ratio of depth variation rate — is $b_1/b_2$ or similar defined ratio), a simplified working form often cited is:

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{2 \, y}{1 + 2y}}
$$

Where $y$ is determined graphically from $\log A$ vs. $\log y$ plot (slope gives $b_1$) and $\log P$ vs. $\log y$ plot (slope gives $b_2$), and $y = b_1 / b_2$.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A trapezoidal channel has $A = 3.2 y^{1.85}$ and $P = 4.0 y^{0.95}$.

Then: $b_1 = 1.85$, $b_2 = 0.95$, $y = 1.85/0.95 = 1.947$.

$$
N = \frac{2(1.947)}{1 + 2(1.947)} = \frac{3.894}{4.894} = 0.7956
$$

So $N \approx 0.80$ — typical for trapezoidal channels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Depth Using Hydraulic Exponents

### Setting Up the Critical Flow Condition

From the critical flow condition derived from the Specific Energy function:

$$
\frac{Q^2 \, T_c}{g \, A_c^3} = 1
$$

### Substituting the Power-Law Geometry

Let:
$$
A_c = \alpha_1 \, y_c^{b_1}, \quad T_c = \alpha_3 \, y_c^{b_3}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{Q^2 \cdot \alpha_3 \, y_c^{b_3}}{g \cdot \alpha_1^3 \, y_c^{3b_1}} = 1
$$

$$
\frac{Q^2 \, \alpha_3}{g \, \alpha_1^3} \cdot y_c^{b_3 - 3b_1} = 1
$$

$$
y_c^{3b_1 - b_3} = \frac{Q^2 \, \alpha_3}{g \, \alpha_1^3}
$$

### Solving for $y_c$:

$$
\boxed{y_c = \left( \frac{Q^2 \, \alpha_3}{g \, \alpha_1^3} \right)^{\frac{1}{3b_1 - b_3}}}
$$

**Verification for Rectangular Channel:** $A = by$, $T = b$, so $\alpha_1 = b$, $b_1 = 1$, $\alpha_3 = b$, $b_3 = 0$.

$$
y_c = \left( \frac{Q^2 \cdot b}{g \cdot b^3} \right)^{\frac{1}{3(1) - 0}} = \left( \frac{Q^2}{g \, b^2} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \frac{q^2}{g} \right)^{1/3} \quad \text{where} \quad q = Q/b
$$

This matches the standard result. ✓

---

## 3.3 Application of Specific Energy — Stepped Transition (No Friction)

### General Energy Equation Across a Transition

For a transition with no friction losses and with a step (hump of height $\Delta z$):

$$
y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + \Delta z
$$

$$
E_1 = E_2 + \Delta z
$$

Where:
- Section 1 is upstream (before transition)
- Section 2 is downstream of the step (or over the hump)

### Case A: Width Contraction (No Hump, $\Delta z = 0$)

$$
y_1 + \frac{Q^2}{2g A_1^2} = y_2 + \frac{Q^2}{2g A_2^2}
$$

This is solved by **trial**: guess $y_2$, compute $A_2$ (from new width), check $E_2 = E_1$.

### Case B: Hump in a Rectangular Channel

For a rectangular channel with width $b$ and a hump of height $\Delta z$ at section 2:

$$
y_1 + \frac{q^2}{2g \, y_1^2} = (y_2 - \Delta z) + \frac{q^2}{2g \, y_2^2}
$$

The **minimum specific energy** over the hump is:

$$
E_{c,\text{hump}} = y_{c,\text{hump}} + \frac{y_{c,\text{hump}}}{2} = \frac{3}{2} y_{c,\text{hump}}
$$

For a given $Q$ and $b$, the **maximum allowable hump** (no choking) is:

$$
\Delta z_{\max} = E_1 - E_{c,\text{hump}}
$$

### Case C: Sluice Gate (Step Up at the Gate)

A sluice gate forces supercritical flow. The depth $y_2$ (downstream of gate, vena contracta) is found from:

$$
y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

With $V_2 = Q / (C_c \cdot b \cdot y_1)$ where $C_c \approx 0.62$ is the **coefficient of contraction**.

---

## 3.4 Choking Condition for Channel Transitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choking occurs when the available specific energy upstream is less than the minimum specific energy required at the throat of the transition.**

The general condition:

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{available}} \geq E_{c,\text{throat}} = \frac{3}{2} y_{c,\text{throat}}}
$$

If $E_{\text{available}} < E_{c,\text{throat}}$, the upstream depth must rise (backwater) to provide the missing energy — this is the **choking phenomenon**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Computation Reference

| Transition Type | Energy Equation | Solution Method |
|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Width contraction (no hump) | $E_1 = E_2$ | Trial on $y_2$ |
| Hump in rectangular | $E_1 = (y_2 - \Delta z) + V_2^2/2g$ | Trial on $y_2$ |
| Width contraction + hump | $E_1 = (y_2 - \Delta z) + V_2^2/2g$ | Trial on $y_2$ |
| Drop (negative hump) | $E_1 = (y_2 + \Delta z) + V_2^2/2g$ | Trial on $y_2$ |
| Sluice gate | $E_1 = E_2$ | $V_2 = Q/(C_c b y_1)$ |
| With friction | $E_1 = E_2 + h_L$ | $h_L = (V_1^2 - V_2^2)/(2g C^2 R) \cdot L$ (Darcy-Weisbach) |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Example: Critical Depth for Trapezoidal Section

**Given:** Trapezoidal channel with bottom width $b = 4$ m, side slope $m = 1.5$ (H:V), $Q = 25 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Express $A$ and $T$ in terms of $y$:**

$$
A = (b + my) y = (4 + 1.5y) y = 4y + 1.5y^2
$$

$$
T = b + 2my = 4 + 3y
$$

**Step 2 — Apply critical flow condition:**

$$
\frac{A_c^3}{T_c} = \frac{Q^2}{g} = \frac{25^2}{9.81} = 63.71 \, \text{m}^5
$$

**Step 3 — Iterate to find $y_c$:**

Try $y = 1.0$ m: $A = 4 + 1.5 = 5.5$, $T = 7.0$, $A^3/T = 166.375/7 = 23.77$ → Too low.

Try $y = 1.5$ m: $A = 6 + 3.375 = 9.375$, $T = 8.5$, $A^3/T = 824.0/8.5 = 96.94$ → Too high.

Try $y = 1.30$ m: $A = 5.20 + 2.535 = 7.735$, $T = 7.90$, $A^3/T = 462.7/7.9 = 58.57$ → Too low.

Try $y = 1.35$ m: $A = 5.40 + 2.734 = 8.134$, $T = 8.05$, $A^3/T = 538.3/8.05 = 66.87$ → Slightly high.

Try $y = 1.33$ m: $A = 5.32 + 2.654 = 7.974$, $T = 7.99$, $A^3/T = 506.9/7.99 = 63.44$ → Very close to 63.71. ✓

**Result:** $y_c \approx 1.33$ m.

---

## 3.7 Step-by-Step Example: Hump in Rectangular Channel

**Given:** Rectangular channel, $b = 3$ m, $Q = 12 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, depth upstream $y_1 = 2.0$ m, hump height $\Delta z = 0.20$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute upstream specific energy:**

$$
q = Q/b = 12/3 = 4 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
E_1 = y_1 + \frac{q^2}{2g \, y_1^2} = 2.0 + \frac{16}{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 4} = 2.0 + 0.204 = 2.204 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute minimum specific energy required at the hump:**

$$
y_{c,\text{hump}} = (q^2/g)^{1/3} = (16/9.81)^{1/3} = (1.631)^{1/3} = 1.179 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
E_{c,\text{hump}} = \frac{3}{2} y_{c,\text{hump}} = 1.5 \times 1.179 = 1.769 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Check for choking:**

$$
\Delta z_{\max} = E_1 - E_{c,\text{hump}} = 2.204 - 1.769 = 0.435 \, \text{m}
$$

Since actual $\Delta z = 0.20$ m $< \Delta z_{\max} = 0.435$ m → **No choking**. ✓

**Step 4 — Find depth over the hump (subcritical solution):**

Energy equation over the hump:

$$
2.204 = (y_2 - 0.20) + \frac{16}{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot y_2^2}
$$

$$
2.204 = y_2 - 0.20 + \frac{0.8155}{y_2^2}
$$

$$
2.404 = y_2 + \frac{0.8155}{y_2^2}
$$

Trial: $y_2 = 2.0$: RHS = $2.0 + 0.204 = 2.204$ → too low.

Trial: $y_2 = 2.2$: RHS = $2.2 + 0.168 = 2.368$ → too low.

Trial: $y_2 = 2.25$: RHS = $2.25 + 0.161 = 2.411$ → close, slightly high.

**Result:** $y_2 \approx 2.24$ m. The flow remains subcritical.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Specific Energy Curve — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Specific Energy E at Section 1"] --> B{"Width Change?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Width Transition: b1 → b2"]
    B -- No --> D["Constant Width Section"]
    C --> E["Compute E2 = E1 at Section 2"]
    D --> E
    E --> F{"E_available >= E_c at Throat?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["NO CHOKING - Solve for y2"]
    F -- No --> H["CHOKING OCCURS - Backwater Rise Upstream"]
    G --> I["Subcritical Solution: y2 > y_c"]
    G --> J["Supercritical Solution: y2 < y_c"]
    H --> K["Compute y1_new from E1_new = E_c,throat"]
    I --> L["Final: y2 depth downstream of transition"]
    J --> L
    K --> M["Final: y1 rises, y2 remains critical"]
    L --> N["Output: y1, y2, V1, V2"]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Hydraulic Exponents — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Channel Geometry Input"] --> B0["Identify Shape Type: Rectangular/Trapezoidal/Triangular"]
    B0 --> C0["Tabulate A and P for various y values"]
    C0 --> D0["Plot log A vs log y and log P vs log y"]
    D0 --> E0["Determine shape exponents b1 and b2"]
    E0 --> F0["Compute y = b1 / b2"]
    F0 --> G0["Manning Exponent N = 2y / 1 + 2y"]
    E0 --> H0["Apply Critical Flow Condition"]
    H0 --> I0["Solve for y_c using General Power-Law Eqn"]
    I0 --> J0["Verify using A_c^3 / T_c = Q^2 / g"]
    G0 --> K0["Output: N value for GVF Analysis"]
    J0 --> L0["Output: y_c, A_c, T_c, R_c, S_c"]
```

## 4.3 Channel Transition with Hump — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Section 1: Upstream\ny1, V1, E1"] --"Subcritical Flow"--> H["Hump of Height dz"]
    H --> S2["Section 2: Over Hump\ny2, V2, E2"]
    S2 --"Energy Eqn Applied"--> C{"E1 vs Ec at Hump"}
    C --"E1 > Ec"--> N["NO CHOKING\ny2 found by iteration"]
    C --"E1 = Ec"--> CR["CRITICAL CONDITION\ny2 = y_c, hump"]
    C --"E1 < Ec"--> CH["CHOKING\ny1 rises, y1_new computed"]
    N --> OUT["Output Depths and Velocities"]
    CR --> OUT
    CH --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Choking Detection Logic — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["Given: Q, b1, b2, y1, dz"] --> CK["Compute q = Q / b2"]
    CK --> YC["Compute y_c at throat = q^2/g to the power 1/3"]
    YC --> EC["E_c = 1.5 × y_c"]
    EC --> E1["Compute E1 = y1 + q^2 / 2g y1^2"]
    E1 --> DZMAX["Delta z max = E1 - E_c"]
    DZMAX --> Q1{"Actual dz > Delta z max?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> ANS1["CHOKING: Upstream depth rises"]
    Q1 -- No --> ANS2["NO CHOKING: Solve E1 = E2 + dz"]
    ANS2 --> SOL["Iterate to find y2 at the throat"]
    ANS1 --> SOL2["y1_new from E1_new = E_c"]
    SOL --> OUT["Result: y2 (downstream depth)"]
    SOL2 --> OUT
```
