## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
> 
> **Q1.** Define Gradually Varied Flow. State any **four assumptions** made in its mathematical analysis.

**Model Answer:**

Gradually Varied Flow (GVF) is a steady, non-uniform open channel flow in which the depth changes gradually along the channel length such that the pressure distribution at every section can be assumed to be hydrostatic.

Assumptions:
1. Steady flow — discharge is constant with respect to time.
2. Incompressible fluid — density $\rho$ is constant.
3. Hydrostatic pressure distribution — vertical accelerations are negligible.
4. Small channel slope — $\cos\theta \approx 1$ and $S_0 = \sin\theta$.
5. Fixed rigid boundary — no scour or siltation.
6. The friction slope $S_f$ follows the same resistance law (Manning or Chezy) as for uniform flow at the same depth. **(Any four to be written)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
> 
> **Q2.** Write the dynamic equation of GVF in its standard form and explain the physical significance of the terms $S_0$, $S_f$, and $Fr$.

**Model Answer:**

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{S_0 - S_f}{1 - Fr^2}
$$

- $S_0$ (bed slope): the gravitational driving force per unit length. It is the energy supplied by the slope of the channel bed.
- $S_f$ (friction slope): the rate of energy loss per unit length due to boundary friction (and form losses, if any).
- $Fr$ (Froude number): $Fr = V/\sqrt{gD_h}$. It indicates the flow regime — subcritical ($Fr < 1$), critical ($Fr = 1$), or supercritical ($Fr > 1$).

Physical significance: The numerator $S_0 - S_f$ is the **net energy gradient** driving the depth change. The denominator $1 - Fr^2$ controls how that gradient is partitioned between depth change and velocity change. At $Fr = 1$, the equation becomes singular — a hallmark of the transition between subcritical and supercritical flow.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
> 
> **Question A.**
> **(a)** Derive the dynamic equation of gradually varied flow starting from the energy equation. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** For a wide rectangular channel, express the dynamic equation in terms of normal depth $y_n$ and critical depth $y_c$ using Manning's formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Step 1:** Write the energy equation between two adjacent sections separated by $dx$:
$$
z_1 + y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + S_f \, dx
$$

**Step 2:** Use $z_1 - z_2 = S_0 \, dx$ and rearrange:
$$
S_0 \, dx - dy - \frac{d(V^2)}{2g} = S_f \, dx
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $dx$:
$$
S_0 - \frac{dy}{dx} - \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dx} = S_f \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 4:** From continuity $Q = AV$ (constant), $A \, dV/dx + V \, dA/dx = 0$. Since $dA/dx = T(dy/dx)$:
$$
\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{VT}{A}\frac{dy}{dx} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute (ii) into (i):
$$
S_0 - \frac{dy}{dx} + \frac{V^2 T}{gA}\frac{dy}{dx} = S_f
$$

**Step 6:** With $Fr^2 = V^2 T / (gA)$:
$$
S_0 - \frac{dy}{dx}(1 - Fr^2) = S_f
$$

**Step 7:** Solving for $dy/dx$:
$$
\boxed{\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{S_0 - S_f}{1 - Fr^2}}
$$

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

For a wide rectangular channel, $A = by$, $T = b$, hydraulic depth $= y$, and $q = Q/b$.

**Froude number simplification:**
$$
Fr^2 = \frac{V^2}{gy} = \frac{q^2}{gy^3}
$$
At critical depth $y_c$: $1 = q^2/(gy_c^3) \Rightarrow q^2 = gy_c^3$. Hence:
$$
Fr^2 = \left(\frac{y_c}{y}\right)^3
$$

**Friction slope simplification (Manning):**
For wide rectangular channels, $R \approx y$:
$$
S_f = \frac{V^2 n^2}{R^{4/3}} = \frac{q^2 n^2}{y^{10/3}}
$$
At normal depth $y_n$, $S_f = S_0$, so $q^2 n^2 = S_0 y_n^{10/3}$. Therefore:
$$
S_f = S_0 \left(\frac{y_n}{y}\right)^{10/3}
$$

**Substituting into the standard form:**
$$
\boxed{\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{S_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{y_n}{y}\right)^{10/3}\right]}{1 - \left(\dfrac{y_c}{y}\right)^3}}
$$

This is the final form suitable for numerical integration using the Direct Step Method or Standard Step Method.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
> 
> **Question B.**
> **(a)** Classify the channel slopes used in gradually varied flow analysis. For each slope, state the relative magnitudes of $y_n$ and $y_c$. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A wide rectangular channel has $q = 3.0$ m²/s, Manning's $n = 0.020$, and bed slope $S_0 = 0.0016$. Compute the **normal depth $y_n$** and **critical depth $y_c$**, classify the slope, and compute $dy/dx$ at $y = 1.5$ m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

| Slope Type | Symbol | Condition | Comparison of Depths |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild | M | $S_0 > 0$ | $y_n > y_c$ |
| Steep | S | $S_0 > 0$ | $y_n < y_c$ |
| Critical | C | $S_0 > 0$ | $y_n = y_c$ |
| Horizontal | H | $S_0 = 0$ | $y_n = \infty$ (no normal depth exists) |
| Adverse | A | $S_0 < 0$ | $y_n$ undefined (no uniform flow) |

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Step 1: Critical depth**

$$
y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{3.0^2}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (0.9174)^{1/3} = 0.971 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Normal depth**

$$
q = \frac{1}{n} y_n^{2/3} S_0^{1/2}
$$
$$
3.0 = \frac{1}{0.020} y_n^{2/3} (0.0016)^{1/2} = \frac{1}{0.020} y_n^{2/3} \times 0.04 = 2 \, y_n^{2/3}
$$
$$
y_n^{2/3} = 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_n = (1.5)^{3/2} = 1.837 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Slope classification**

Since $y_n = 1.837$ m $> y_c = 0.971$ m, the slope is **MILD** (M).

**Step 4: Compute $dy/dx$ at $y = 1.5$ m**

At $y = 1.5$ m, we are between $y_c$ and $y_n$ (Zone 2):
$$
\left(\frac{y_n}{y}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\frac{1.837}{1.5}\right)^{10/3} = (1.2247)^{3.333} = 2.040
$$
$$
\left(\frac{y_c}{y}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{0.971}{1.5}\right)^3 = (0.6473)^3 = 0.2713
$$

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{0.0016 \left[1 - 2.040\right]}{1 - 0.2713} = \frac{0.0016 \times (-1.040)}{0.7287} = \frac{-0.001664}{0.7287} = -2.28 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Interpretation:** The water surface falls at a rate of $2.28 \times 10^{-3}$ (or 2.28 m per km). The negative sign confirms this is an **M2 drawdown profile**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Sign errors in the energy equation** — Students often forget that $z_1 - z_2 = S_0 \, dx$ and write it as $-S_0 \, dx$. This single sign error propagates and yields an incorrect final form. *Always draw a small sketch and label section 1 (upstream) and section 2 (downstream) before substituting.*
> 
> 2. **Confusing $S_0$ and $S_f$** — $S_0$ is the bed slope (a constant geometric property); $S_f$ is the friction slope (a function of local depth $y$). Swapping them in the numerator gives the wrong profile classification.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the energy correction factor $\alpha$** — In the derivation, $\alpha$ is often taken as 1 with a one-line justification. Failing to mention this assumption costs at least 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Using $y_n$ in Chezy form instead of $y_n^{10/3}$** — In Manning's form, the exponent is **10/3**, not 3. The exponent 3 belongs to the Chezy form. Mixing them up is a classic error.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the units** — KTU examiners are particular about $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ being mentioned and SI units being used throughout.
> 
> 6. **Singularity at $Fr = 1$ not addressed** — When asked "why can't GVF pass through $y = y_c$?", students must state that the dynamic equation becomes singular at $Fr = 1$, which is why the flow transitions to RVF (hydraulic jump or drop).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This to Your Wall:**

- **GVF definition:** Steady, non-uniform, gradually changing depth, hydrostatic pressure, $dy/dx$ is small but finite.
- **Dynamic equation (standard):** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{S_0 - S_f}{1 - Fr^2}$
- **Energy-gradient form:** $\dfrac{dE}{dx} = S_0 - S_f$
- **Wide rectangular, Manning:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{S_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{y_n}{y}\right)^{10/3}\right]}{1 - \left(\dfrac{y_c}{y}\right)^3}$
- **Wide rectangular, Chezy:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{S_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{y_n}{y}\right)^3\right]}{1 - \left(\dfrac{y_c}{y}\right)^3}$
- **Five slope types:** Mild (M), Steep (S), Critical (C), Horizontal (H), Adverse (A).
- **Twelve profiles:** M1, M2, M3, S1, S2, S3, C1, C3, H2, H3, A2, A3.
- **Critical depth formula (rectangular):** $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3}$.
- **Normal depth formula (rectangular, Manning):** $y_n = (q n / \sqrt{S_0})^{3/5}$.
- **Singularity:** At $Fr = 1$, $1 - Fr^2 = 0$ → equation breaks down → RVF.
- **Assumptions to always list:** Steady, incompressible, hydrostatic, $\alpha = 1$, fixed bed, small slope, friction law same as uniform flow.
- **Sign convention:** $x$ positive in flow direction; $S_0 > 0$ for mild/steep/critical; $S_0 = 0$ for horizontal; $S_0 < 0$ for adverse.
- **Numerator $S_0 - S_f$ flips sign at $y = y_n$.** **Denominator $1 - Fr^2$ flips sign at $y = y_c$.** The combination of sign changes determines the profile.
- **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s².** Always use SI units in derivations and numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computation of Length of Water Surface Profiles - Direct Step Method, Bresse's Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computation of Length of Water Surface Profiles - Direct Step Method, Bresse's Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why Do We Compute Water Surface Profiles?

In open channel hydraulics, the water surface is rarely horizontal along the channel length. Due to changes in slope, cross-section, or the presence of obstructions, the depth of flow gradually changes from section to section. This phenomenon is called **Gradually Varied Flow (GVF)** and is described by a first-order ordinary differential equation known as the **Dynamic Equation of GVF**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Computation of the length of a water surface profile means determining the **horizontal distance `x`** between two sections of known depth `y₁` and `y₂` along a prismatic channel under GVF conditions. The two most important classical methods taught at KTU level are the **Direct Step Method** and **Bresse's Method**.

## 1.2 The Governing Idea — A Simple Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Walking Down a Hill with a Flashlight"**
> Imagine you are standing at the top of a long, gently sloping hill at night. You cannot see the bottom, but you know your height above the ground at every point. You take small "steps" — drop your height a little (say 10 cm), measure how far ahead your flashlight points on the ground, and repeat. The "height drops" are the depth increments `Δy`, and the "flashlight distances" are the computed length increments `Δx`. By summing these little steps, you locate yourself at any desired depth.
> This is essentially the **Direct Step Method** — we assume the depth (height) and solve for the horizontal distance (length).
> **Bresse's Method** is like having a pre-calculated map of "how much horizontal distance corresponds to how much depth drop" — you simply look up values from a table.

## 1.3 Pre-Requisite — The Dynamic Equation of GVF

The fundamental governing equation (derived from energy conservation between two sections in a prismatic channel) is:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{S_0 - S_f}{1 - Fr^2}$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| `y` | Depth of flow | m |
| `x` | Horizontal distance along the channel | m |
| `S₀` | Bed slope of the channel | m/m |
| `S_f` | Friction (energy) slope = `V²n²/R^(4/3)` (Manning) | m/m |
| `Fr` | Froude number = `V / √(gD)` | dimensionless |
| `D` | Hydraulic depth = `A/T` | m |
| `V` | Mean velocity | m/s |
| `n` | Manning's roughness coefficient | — |
| `R` | Hydraulic radius = `A/P` | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **single equation** is the heart of BOTH methods. The difference lies purely in *how we integrate* it.

## 1.4 What Are We Solving For?

| Method | Known (Assumed) | Unknown (Computed) | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Step Method** | Increment of depth `Δy` | Increment of length `Δx` | General prismatic channels, hand-computation in exams |
| **Bresse's Method** | Depth ratio `η = y/y_c` | Length `L` via tabulated Bresse function | Wide rectangular channels with `S₀ < S_c` (mild slope) |

## 1.5 Classification of Profiles (Quick Recall)

The classification of water surface profiles uses the comparison of actual depth `y` with **normal depth `y_n`** and **critical depth `y_c`**:

| Bed Slope | Condition | Profile Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild (`S₀ < S_c`) | `y > y_n > y_c` | **M1** | Backwater curve (above normal) |
| Mild | `y_n > y > y_c` | **M2** | Drawdown curve |
| Mild | `y < y_c < y_n` | **M3** | Super-critical flood |
| Steep (`S₀ > S_c`) | `y < y_c < y_n` | **S2** | Drawdown |
| Steep | `y > y_n > y_c` | **S1** | Backwater |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam questions, M1, M2, S1, and S2 profiles are the most commonly tested.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deriving the Working Form of the GVF Equation

Starting from the specific energy equation between two sections (1) and (2) of a prismatic channel with discharge `Q`:

$$S_0 \cdot \Delta x + y_1 + \alpha_1 \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \alpha_2 \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + \bar{S_f} \cdot \Delta x$$

where `Δx = x₂ - x₁` and `S̄_f` is the **average friction slope** between the two sections.

Rearranging, with energy correction factor `α ≈ 1` assumed constant:

$$\Delta x = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{S_0 - \bar{S_f}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is THE master equation for the Direct Step Method.** Every numerical problem at KTU is solved by iterating this formula.

The specific energy at any section is:

$$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g} = y + \frac{Q^2}{2gA^2}$$

The friction slope using **Manning's equation**:

$$S_f = \frac{n^2 V^2}{R^{4/3}} = \frac{n^2 Q^2}{A^2 R^{4/3}}$$

## 2.2 The Froude Number (Compact Form)

For a general section:

$$Fr^2 = \frac{V^2}{gD} = \frac{Q^2 T}{g A^3}$$

For a **rectangular channel** of width `b`:

$$Fr^2 = \frac{q^2}{g y^3}, \quad \text{where } q = \frac{Q}{b}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — every KTU Part B (14-mark) question on this topic uses these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Δx = (E₂ − E₁) / (S₀ − S̄_f)` | **Direct Step Method — core equation** |
| 2 | `E = y + Q² / (2gA²)` | Specific energy at any section |
| 3 | `V = Q / A` | Continuity |
| 4 | `S_f = n² V² / R^(4/3)` | Friction slope (Manning) |
| 5 | `R = A / P` | Hydraulic radius |
| 6 | `Fr² = Q²T / (gA³)` | Froude number squared |
| 7 | `y_c = (q²/g)^(1/3)` | Critical depth (rectangular) |
| 8 | `y_n = (q n / √S₀)^(3/2) / b` *(rect)* | Normal depth (Manning, rectangular) |
| 9 | `S_c = (n² g) / (y_c^(1/3))` *(rect)* | Critical slope |
| 10 | `L = (y_c / S₀) · [φ(η₂) − φ(η₁)]` | **Bresse's Method — core equation** |
| 11 | `η = y / y_c` | Dimensionless depth ratio |
| 12 | `Π = S₀ / S_c` | Bresse parameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** In the Direct Step Method, the friction slope `S_f` is **evaluated at the chosen depth `y`**, not at normal depth. Confusing `S_f` with `S₀` is the #1 mistake costing 2–3 marks per question.

## 2.4 Conditions of Validity

| Condition | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Prismatic channel** | Both methods assume constant cross-section and slope |
| **Steady flow** | Discharge `Q` constant along the length |
| **Gradually varied flow** | Hydrostatic pressure assumption is valid |
| **Mild slope (`S₀ < S_c`)** | Bresse's Method strictly requires this |

## 2.5 Why These Methods Are Still Used in Modern Engineering

- **Software (HEC-RAS)** uses similar numerical schemes internally.
- Understanding these methods builds **physical intuition** for GVF — critical for design engineers.
- **Exam-friendly** — KTU frequently asks hand-computation for one step or two.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Direct Step Method Equation

### Step 1 — Write the Energy Equation Between Two Sections

For steady, gradually varied flow in a prismatic channel, applying the energy principle between section 1 (upstream) and section 2 (downstream):

$$y_1 + \alpha_1 \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + S_0 \Delta x = y_2 + \alpha_2 \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + \bar{S_f} \Delta x$$

> *Conversion logic:* The energy at section 1 plus the energy gained from the bed drop `S₀·Δx` equals the energy at section 2 plus the head loss `S̄_f·Δx` due to friction.

### Step 2 — Define Specific Energy

$$E = y + \alpha \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Substituting into Step 1:

$$E_1 + S_0 \Delta x = E_2 + \bar{S_f} \Delta x$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange for `Δx`

$$S_0 \Delta x - \bar{S_f} \Delta x = E_2 - E_1$$

$$\Delta x (S_0 - \bar{S_f}) = E_2 - E_1$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{S_0 - \bar{S_f}}}$$

This is the **working formula for the Direct Step Method**.

### Step 4 — Friction Slope Average

$$\bar{S_f} = \frac{S_{f1} + S_{f2}}{2}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Arithmetic mean of friction slopes at the two section depths — a valid approximation for small `Δy`.

## 3.2 Full Derivation of Bresse's Method

### Step 1 — Start from the Differential Form of GVF

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{S_0 - S_f}{1 - Fr^2}$$

### Step 2 — Use Chezy's Resistance Equation (Bresse's Original Form)

Bresse used Chezy's formula:

$$V = C \sqrt{R S_f}$$

which gives:

$$S_f = \frac{V^2}{C^2 R} = \frac{Q^2}{C^2 A^2 R}$$

### Step 3 — Use Critical Depth as Reference

Define:
- `η = y / y_c` (dimensionless depth)
- `Π = S₀ / S_c` (slope ratio)
- For a wide rectangular channel, `R ≈ y`, `A = by`, `T = b`

The critical slope `S_c` for a wide rectangular channel is:

$$S_c = \frac{g}{C^2 y_c} \quad \text{or} \quad S_c = \frac{n^2 g}{y_c^{1/3}} \text{ (Manning)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the GVF Equation

After substantial algebra (substituting `S_f = S₀ (y_n / y)^x` for rectangular channels with Manning's `x = 4/3`):

$$\frac{dx}{dy_c} = \frac{1 - \eta^3}{S_0 - S_f/S_c \cdot (1/\eta^x)}$$

### Step 5 — Integration via Bresse Function

Integrating between limits `η₁` and `η₂`:

$$L = x_2 - x_1 = \frac{y_c}{S_0} \left[ \phi(\eta_2) - \phi(\eta_1) \right]$$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{y_c}{S_0} \left[ \phi(\eta_2) - \phi(\eta_1) \right]}$$

where `φ(η)` is **Bresse's function**, tabulated in standard textbooks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Bresse function `φ(η)` depends on the parameter `Π = S₀/S_c`. For **mild slope** (`Π < 1`) and assuming Chezy (`x = 2`):
>
> $$\phi(\eta) = \int \frac{1 - \eta^3}{1 - \Pi \eta^{x}} d\eta$$
>
> For wide rectangular channels with Manning's resistance, `x = 10/3` and the integral is computed numerically and tabulated.

### Step 6 — Tabulated Bresse Function Values (Mild Slope, Wide Rectangular, `Π = S₀/S_c`)

| η = y/y_c | φ(η) for Π = 0.1 | φ(η) for Π = 0.5 | φ(η) for Π = 0.9 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 0.2 | 0.020 | 0.018 | 0.016 |
| 0.4 | 0.064 | 0.057 | 0.050 |
| 0.6 | 0.146 | 0.128 | 0.108 |
| 0.8 | 0.286 | 0.246 | 0.196 |
| 1.0 | 0.500 | 0.412 | 0.302 |
| 1.2 | 0.805 | 0.628 | 0.420 |
| 1.4 | 1.215 | 0.880 | 0.555 |
| 1.6 | 1.745 | 1.180 | 0.705 |
| 1.8 | 2.430 | 1.530 | 0.870 |
| 2.0 | 3.310 | 1.940 | 1.050 |

> *Note:* Values are approximate; actual KTU problems provide the table or `Π` value. Students must use the **provided** table in the exam.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Direct Step vs. Bresse

| Feature | Direct Step Method | Bresse's Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Unknown** | Length `Δx` (incremental) | Total length `L` |
| **Channel Shape** | Any prismatic shape | Wide rectangular only |
| **Slope Type** | Any (M, S, H, A, C) | Mild only (`S₀ < S_c`) |
| **Resistance Law** | Manning (most common) | Chezy (originally), Manning adapted |
| **Computation Tool** | Step-by-step summation | Table lookup |
| **Accuracy** | High (with small `Δy`) | Moderate (functional approximation) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ (very high) | ★★★ (moderate) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Direct Step Method (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A rectangular channel 5 m wide carries discharge `Q = 20 m³/s` on a bed slope `S₀ = 0.0009` with Manning's `n = 0.025`. Compute the length of the M1 profile between depths `y₁ = 3.5 m` and `y₂ = 3.0 m` using the Direct Step Method.

**Solution:**

| Section | y (m) | A = by (m²) | V = Q/A (m/s) | V²/2g (m) | E = y + V²/2g (m) | P = b+2y (m) | R = A/P (m) | S_f = n²V²/R^(4/3) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3.5 | 17.50 | 1.143 | 0.0666 | 3.5666 | 12.0 | 1.4583 | 0.000495 |
| 2 | 3.0 | 15.00 | 1.333 | 0.0906 | 3.0906 | 11.0 | 1.3636 | 0.000780 |

**Step-by-step calculation:**

> *Section 1 (y₁ = 3.5 m):*
> - `A₁ = 5 × 3.5 = 17.50 m²`
> - `V₁ = 20 / 17.50 = 1.143 m/s`
> - `V₁² / (2g) = (1.143)² / (2 × 9.81) = 0.0666 m`
> - `E₁ = 3.5 + 0.0666 = 3.5666 m`
> - `P₁ = 5 + 2(3.5) = 12.0 m`
> - `R₁ = 17.50 / 12.0 = 1.4583 m`
> - `S_f1 = (0.025)² × (1.143)² / (1.4583)^(4/3) = 0.000495`

> *Section 2 (y₂ = 3.0 m):*
> - `A₂ = 5 × 3.0 = 15.00 m²`
> - `V₂ = 20 / 15.00 = 1.333 m/s`
> - `V₂² / (2g) = (1.333)² / (2 × 9.81) = 0.0906 m`
> - `E₂ = 3.0 + 0.0906 = 3.0906 m`
> - `P₂ = 5 + 2(3.0) = 11.0 m`
> - `R₂ = 15.00 / 11.0 = 1.3636 m`
> - `S_f2 = (0.025)² × (1.333)² / (1.3636)^(4/3) = 0.000780`

> *Average friction slope:*
> - `S̄_f = (0.000495 + 0.000780) / 2 = 0.000638`

> *Apply Direct Step formula:*
> - `ΔE = E₂ − E₁ = 3.0906 − 3.5666 = −0.4760 m`
> - `S₀ − S̄_f = 0.0009 − 0.000638 = 0.000262`
> - `Δx = −0.4760 / 0.000262 = −1817 m`

> **Result:** `Δx ≈ 1817 m`. The negative sign indicates section 1 is **downstream** from section 2 (as expected for M1 — the profile rises as we go upstream).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Bresse's Method (KTU Style)

**Problem:** In a wide rectangular channel, `y_n = 2.0 m`, `y_c = 1.5 m`, `S₀ = 0.0004`, `S_c = 0.001`. Compute the length of M1 profile between depths `y₁ = 2.5 m` and `y₂ = 2.2 m` using Bresse's Method with `Π = 0.4`.

**Solution:**

> *Step 1 — Compute η values:*
> - `η₁ = y₁ / y_c = 2.5 / 1.5 = 1.667`
> - `η₂ = y₂ / y_c = 2.2 / 1.5 = 1.467`

> *Step 2 — Read Bresse function values (interpolating for Π = 0.4):*
> - At `η = 1.667`, `φ ≈ 1.45` (interpolated)
> - At `η = 1.467`, `φ ≈ 1.10` (interpolated)

> *Step 3 — Apply Bresse's formula:*
> - `L = (y_c / S₀) × [φ(η₁) − φ(η₂)]`
> - `L = (1.5 / 0.0004) × [1.45 − 1.10]`
> - `L = 3750 × 0.35`
> - **`L ≈ 1313 m`**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart — Direct Step Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Q, n, S0, channel shape, y1, y2] --> B[Compute yn and yc to identify profile type]
    B --> C[Divide depth range into N intervals: dy = y2-y1 / N]
    C --> D[For each depth yi compute Ai, Vi, Pi, Ri]
    D --> E[Compute specific energy Ei = yi + Vi^2 / 2g]
    E --> F[Compute friction slope Sfi = n^2 Vi^2 / Ri^4/3]
    F --> G[Compute average friction slope Sf_avg between sections i and i+1]
    G --> H[Compute delta x = E_i+1 - E_i divided by S0 - Sf_avg]
    H --> I{Last interval?}
    I -- No --> D
    I -- Yes --> J[Sum all delta x to get total length L]
    J --> K[End: Report L]
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — Bresse's Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given wide rectangular channel, y1, y2, yc, yn, S0, Sc] --> B[Compute slope ratio Pi = S0 divided by Sc]
    B --> C[Compute dimensionless depths eta1 = y1 divided by yc and eta2 = y2 divided by yc]
    C --> D[Verify mild slope: S0 less than Sc and both y greater than yc]
    D --> E[Look up Bresse function phi_eta1 and phi_eta2 from provided table for given Pi]
    E --> F[Compute length L = yc divided by S0 times phi_eta1 minus phi_eta2]
    F --> G[Verify result: M1 profile must have positive length]
    G --> H[End: Report L]
```

## 4.3 Water Surface Profile Classification (Mild Slope Focus)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[S0 compared to Sc] --> P1{S0 less than Sc: MILD}
    S0 --> P2{S0 greater than Sc: STEEP}
    S0 --> P3{S0 equal to Sc: CRITICAL}
    S0 --> P4{S0 equals zero: HORIZONTAL}
    S0 --> P5{S0 negative: ADVERSE}

    P1 --> M1[y greater than yn greater than yc: M1 BACKWATER]
    P1 --> M2[yn greater than y greater than yc: M2 DRAWDOWN]
    P1 --> M3[yn greater than yc greater than y: M3 SUPERCRITICAL]

    P2 --> S1[y greater than yn greater than yc: S1 BACKWATER]
    P2 --> S2[yc greater than y greater than yn: S2 DRAWDOWN]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Relationship Between Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GVF[Dynamic Equation of GVF: dy/dx = S0-Sf over 1-Fr^2] --> DSM[Direct Step Method]
    GVF --> BM[Bresse Method]

    DSM --> SUM1[Sum incremental delta x for assumed delta y]
    BM --> SUM2[Tabulated Bresse function: L = yc/S0 times delta phi]

    SUM1 --> OUT[Length L of Water Surface Profile]
    SUM2 --> OUT
```

## 4.5 Visual Schematic of an M1 Backwater Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Control Structure upstream] --> B[Depth y1 at upstream end]
    B --> C[Gradually rising M1 profile]
    C --> D[Asymptotic approach to normal depth yn]
    D --> E[Depth equals yn far downstream]
```

> *Annotation:* The M1 profile starts at a depth greater than normal depth near a control (e.g., a dam or weir) and gradually approaches `y_n` as we move downstream. The Direct Step Method computes this horizontal extent.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between the Direct Step Method and Bresse's Method for computing water surface profiles.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct Step Method | Bresse's Method |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Shape | Any prismatic | Wide rectangular |
| Slope | Mild, Steep, Horizontal, Adverse | Mild only |
| Computation | Incremental step-by-step | Tabulated Bresse function |
| Result | `Δx` per step, summed | Total length `L` directly |
| Resistance | Manning (commonly) | Chezy / Manning |

> The Direct Step Method is more **general and widely applicable**, while Bresse's Method is a **closed-form-style solution** for specific conditions using pre-tabulated functions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Write down the working equation of the Direct Step Method and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{S_0 - \bar{S_f}}$$

Where:
- `Δx` = horizontal length between two consecutive sections
- `E₁, E₂` = specific energy at sections 1 and 2 = `y + V²/(2g)`
- `S₀` = bed slope of the channel
- `S̄_f` = average friction slope between the two sections, computed as `(S_f1 + S_f2)/2`

This equation is obtained by applying the energy conservation principle to two sections of a prismatic channel under GVF.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A long prismatic rectangular channel 4 m wide has a bed slope `S₀ = 0.0008` and Manning's `n = 0.022`. The discharge is `Q = 15 m³/s`.

**(a)** Determine the normal depth `y_n` and critical depth `y_c`, and identify the bed slope type and the profile that will form if the depth at a section is `y = 2.5 m`. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the length of the water surface profile between depths `y₁ = 2.5 m` and `y₂ = 2.0 m` using the Direct Step Method. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Step 1 — Normal depth `y_n` (Manning's equation for rectangular channel):**
>
> $$Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S_0^{1/2}$$
>
> For rectangular: `A = by = 4y`, `P = b + 2y = 4 + 2y`, `R = 4y / (4 + 2y)`
>
> $$15 = \frac{1}{0.022} \times 4y_n \times \left(\frac{4y_n}{4 + 2y_n}\right)^{2/3} \times (0.0008)^{1/2}$$
>
> Solving iteratively: **`y_n ≈ 2.0 m`**

> **Step 2 — Critical depth `y_c` (rectangular):**
>
> $$y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{(15/4)^2}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{14.0625}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (1.433)^{1/3}$$
>
> **`y_c ≈ 1.13 m`**

> **Step 3 — Slope type:**
> Since `y_n > y_c`, the bed slope is **MILD**.

> **Step 4 — Profile identification:**
> With `y = 2.5 m > y_n = 2.0 m > y_c = 1.13 m`, the profile is **M1 (Backwater curve)**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute hydraulic parameters at each section:**

| Quantity | Section 1 (y₁ = 2.5 m) | Section 2 (y₂ = 2.0 m) |
|---|---|---|
| `A = 4y` (m²) | 10.0 | 8.0 |
| `V = Q/A` (m/s) | 1.500 | 1.875 |
| `V²/(2g)` (m) | 0.1147 | 0.1792 |
| `E = y + V²/(2g)` (m) | 2.6147 | 2.1792 |
| `P = 4 + 2y` (m) | 9.0 | 8.0 |
| `R = A/P` (m) | 1.1111 | 1.0000 |
| `S_f = n²V²/R^(4/3)` | 0.000752 | 0.001302 |

**Step 2 — Average friction slope:**
`S̄_f = (0.000752 + 0.001302) / 2 = 0.001027`

**Step 3 — Apply Direct Step formula:**
`Δx = (E₂ − E₁) / (S₀ − S̄_f) = (2.1792 − 2.6147) / (0.0008 − 0.001027) = (−0.4355) / (−0.000227)`

> **`Δx ≈ 1918.5 m`**

The negative sign in both numerator and denominator indicates that section 1 lies **downstream** — the M1 profile rises as we move upstream over a distance of approximately 1918.5 m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** State and derive the dynamic equation of gradually varied flow. Show clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A wide rectangular channel has `y_n = 1.8 m`, `y_c = 1.0 m`, bed slope `S₀ = 0.0005`, and critical slope `S_c = 0.001`. Using Bresse's Method (with `Π = 0.5`), compute the length of the M1 profile between depths `y₁ = 2.5 m` and `y₂ = 2.0 m`. The Bresse function table for `Π = 0.5` is provided. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Statement:**
The dynamic equation of GVF relates the rate of change of water depth `y` with respect to the longitudinal distance `x` in a prismatic channel:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{S_0 - S_f}{1 - Fr^2}$$

**Derivation:**

> *Step 1 — Apply energy equation between sections 1 and 2:*
>
> $$S_0 \Delta x + y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + \bar{S_f} \Delta x$$

> *Step 2 — Rearrange:*
>
> $$(S_0 - \bar{S_f}) \Delta x = (y_2 - y_1) + \left(\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}\right)$$

> *Step 3 — Apply continuity `Q = AV`, differentiate `V² = Q²/A²`:*
>
> $$d\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) = -\frac{Q^2}{gA^3} dA = -\frac{Q^2 T}{gA^3} dy$$

> *Step 4 — Substitute in the limit:*
>
> $$S_0 - S_f = \frac{dy}{dx} + \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) = \frac{dy}{dx}\left(1 - \frac{Q^2 T}{gA^3}\right) = \frac{dy}{dx}(1 - Fr^2)$$

> *Step 5 — Final form:*
>
> $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{S_0 - S_f}{1 - Fr^2}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, gradually varied flow.
2. Hydrostatic pressure distribution (valid for mild slopes).
3. Prismatic channel (constant cross-section, slope).
4. Energy correction factor `α ≈ 1`.
5. Manning's / Chezy's resistance law applies.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

> **Step 1 — Compute `η` values:**
> - `η₁ = y₁ / y_c = 2.5 / 1.0 = 2.5`
> - `η₂ = y₂ / y_c = 2.0 / 1.0 = 2.0`

> **Step 2 — Read Bresse function from table (for Π = 0.5):**
> - `φ(η₁ = 2.5) ≈ 2.85` *(interpolated from table)*
> - `φ(η₂ = 2.0) ≈ 1.94` *(interpolated from table)*

> **Step 3 — Apply Bresse's formula:**
>
> $$L = \frac{y_c}{S_0}\left[\phi(\eta_1) - \phi(\eta_2)\right] = \frac{1.0}{0.0005}[2.85 - 1.94] = 2000 \times 0.91$$
>
> **`L ≈ 1820 m`**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error** — Students forget that for M1 profile, `Δx` comes out **negative** and panic. The correct interpretation: section 1 is downstream of section 2 (the water surface rises going upstream).
> 2. **Confusing `S_f` with `S₀`** — Friction slope must be computed at the **chosen depth** using `n²V²/R^(4/3)`, not just `S₀`.
> 3. **Wrong Average Friction Slope** — Forgetting to take the mean of `S_f1` and `S_f2`.
> 4. **Hydraulic Radius Mistake** — For rectangular channel, `R = by / (b + 2y)`, NOT `R = y`.
> 5. **Bresse Method Misapplication** — Using Bresse's method for a non-wide rectangular channel or steep slope.
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing `mm` with `m`, or `cm` with `m` in slope values.
> 7. **Forgetting Profile Identification** — KTU questions always award 1–2 marks for correctly identifying the profile type (M1, M2, etc.).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, GVF Profile Computation**

- **GVF Dynamic Equation:** `dy/dx = (S₀ − S_f) / (1 − Fr²)` — the foundation.
- **Direct Step Method (DSM):**
  - Assumes `Δy`, computes `Δx` = `(E₂ − E₁) / (S₀ − S̄_f)`.
  - Works for **any prismatic channel** and **any slope**.
  - Use **Manning's equation** for `S_f`: `S_f = n²V² / R^(4/3)`.
  - Hydraulic radius: `R = A / P`.
  - Specific energy: `E = y + V²/(2g)`.
- **Bresse's Method:**
  - Restricted to **wide rectangular channels** on **mild slopes**.
  - Formula: `L = (y_c / S₀) × [φ(η₁) − φ(η₂)]`.
  - Uses **tabulated Bresse function** values.
  - Compute `η = y / y_c` and look up `φ(η)`.
- **Profile Identification:** Always state whether profile is M1, M2, M3, S1, S2, etc.
- **Units:** Use SI throughout (m, m³/s, m/m for slopes).
- **Sign of `Δx`:** Negative means going upstream; report magnitude.
- **KTU Mark Distribution (typical):** Identification (2) + Computation (5) + Final answer with units (1) = 8 marks for a 14-mark question split with theory.
- **Memorize the DSM table** — it is the single most exam-relevant artifact.
- **Bresse's table** is usually provided in the question paper — practice interpolation.
- **Both methods** trace back to the same GVF equation; only the integration technique differs.
- **Sanity Check:** For M1 profiles, computed length should be in **hundreds to thousands of meters** for typical small channels. Lengths in cm indicate unit errors.

---
**End of Notes — Module 3, Computation of Length of Water Surface Profiles**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Step Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Standard Step Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Standard Step Method (SSM)** is a numerical, iterative, backward-stepping computational procedure used to determine the **gradually varied flow (GVF)** water surface profile in open channels. It is derived from the **one-dimensional steady-flow energy equation** applied between two adjacent cross-sections separated by a finite reach length $\Delta x$.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the Standard Step Method is treated as the **most general and versatile numerical technique** for water surface profile computation, applicable to channels of **any cross-sectional shape**, **any longitudinal slope** (mild, steep, critical, horizontal, adverse), and **non-prismatic geometry** (varying cross-section or roughness), where direct integration methods fail or become analytically intractable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The Standard Step Method is a mandatory numerical tool. Students must be able to (a) state the governing energy equation, (b) write the friction slope expression using Manning's / Chezy's equation, (c) outline the iteration algorithm, and (d) apply it to a worked numerical problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking along the bank of a river and want to determine the **height of the water surface at every point** along a stretch, but you can only measure the depth at one known location (say, at a gauging station at the downstream end). The river has a bridge pier (contraction), then a wider section (expansion), and the bed is not uniform.

The **Standard Step Method** is like placing a series of "energy check-points" along the river. At each check-point, you:
1. **Guess** the water depth (and hence the water surface elevation).
2. **Calculate** the energy at that cross-section.
3. **Compare** it with the energy at the previous check-point, accounting for friction losses along the way and turbulence losses at geometric changes.
4. **Adjust** the guess and repeat until the energy equation balances (tolerates a small error, e.g., $\varepsilon \leq 0.01$ m or $0.5\%$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Designing **flood control channels** and computing **backwater profiles** upstream of bridges, culverts, weirs, and dams.
> - Evaluating **afflux** (rise in water level) caused by hydraulic structures.
> - **FEMA Floodplain Mapping** in the United States (HEC-RAS software uses exactly this method).
> - Designing **irrigation canals** with varying cross-sections and roughness.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$ |
| Manning's roughness coefficient | $n$ | dimensionless ($\mathbf{s/m^{1/3}}$) |
| Chezy's coefficient | $C$ | $\mathbf{m^{1/2}/s}$ |
| Bed slope | $S_0$ | dimensionless (m/m) |
| Energy loss coefficient (contraction) | $K_c$ | dimensionless ($\mathbf{0.0 - 0.5}$) |
| Energy loss coefficient (expansion) | $K_e$ | dimensionless ($\mathbf{0.3 - 1.0}$) |
| Convergence tolerance | $\varepsilon$ | $\mathbf{0.001 - 0.01 \ m}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

The Standard Step Method is fundamentally an application of the **conservation of energy principle** for steady, one-dimensional open channel flow. Between any two cross-sections (labelled 1 and 2, separated by a reach length $\Delta x$), the total energy at section 2 plus the energy losses between sections 1 and 2 must equal the total energy at section 1.

> [!NOTE]
> The method proceeds in a **backward direction** (i.e., computations are performed against the flow direction when going upstream from downstream, or with the flow when starting from an upstream control) using a finite step $\Delta x$. This is why it is called a "step" method.

### 2.2 The Core Operational Logic

1. **Establish a control section** where the water surface elevation (WSE) is known. This is typically a location where critical depth occurs, or a gauging station, or the downstream/upstream boundary of the reach.
2. **Divide the channel reach** into discrete sub-reaches at cross-sections where geometry, slope, or roughness changes. Let $\Delta x_i$ be the length of the $i$-th sub-reach.
3. **Select a sub-reach** for which the WSE at one end is known.
4. **Assume the water depth** (and hence the WSE) at the unknown end of the sub-reach.
5. **Compute the total energy head** at both sections: $H = z + y + \alpha \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$, where $z$ = bed elevation, $y$ = depth, $V$ = mean velocity, $\alpha$ = kinetic energy correction (Coriolis) coefficient.
6. **Compute the friction slope** $S_f$ at each section using Manning's equation: $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S_f^{1/2}$.
7. **Compute the energy losses** in the sub-reach:
   - Friction loss: $h_f = \overline{S_f} \cdot \Delta x$, where $\overline{S_f} = \dfrac{S_{f1} + S_{f2}}{2}$.
   - Eddy loss (contraction/expansion): $h_e = K \left| \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} - \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} \right|$.
8. **Apply the energy equation**:
   $$H_1 = H_2 + h_f + h_e$$
9. **Check the residual**: $\Delta H = H_1 - (H_2 + h_f + h_e)$. If $\vert \Delta H \vert > \varepsilon$, revise the assumed $y_2$ and repeat from step 5.
10. **Move to the next sub-reach** and repeat until the entire water surface profile is computed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = z + y + \alpha \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Total energy head at any section |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Mean velocity from discharge and area |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{A}{P}$ | Hydraulic radius (Area / Wetted Perimeter) |
| 4 | $S_f = \dfrac{n^2 V^2}{R^{4/3}}$ | Friction slope (Manning's, SI units) |
| 5 | $S_f = \dfrac{V^2}{C^2 R}$ | Friction slope (Chezy's form) |
| 6 | $\overline{S_f} = \dfrac{S_{f1} + S_{f2}}{2}$ | Average friction slope in the sub-reach |
| 7 | $h_f = \overline{S_f} \cdot \Delta x$ | Frictional head loss over sub-reach |
| 8 | $h_e = K \left\vert \Delta \dfrac{V^2}{2g} \right\vert$ | Eddy (transition) loss at contraction / expansion |
| 9 | $z_1 + y_1 + \alpha_1 \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + y_2 + \alpha_2 \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f + h_e$ | Governing energy equation between two sections |
| 10 | $S_0 = \dfrac{z_1 - z_2}{\Delta x}$ | Bed slope of the sub-reach |
| 11 | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gD_h}}$ | Froude number ($D_h = A/T$ = hydraulic depth) |
| 12 | $\varepsilon$ tolerance $= 0.001 \text{ m}$ (or $0.5\%$ of $H$) | Convergence criterion for iteration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** When using Manning's formula in SI, $V$ is in m/s, $R$ in metres, $n$ in s/m$^{1/3}$, and $S_f$ is dimensionless (m/m). Always keep $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$ consistent.

### 2.4 Why This Method is Universally Used

The Standard Step Method is the **backbone of modern river-hydraulics software** (e.g., **HEC-RAS** by the US Army Corps of Engineers, **MIKE 11** by DHI, **SWMM** by EPA). It is preferred because:

- It handles **arbitrary cross-section geometry** (natural rivers with compound channels, irregular banks).
- It accommodates **spatially varying roughness** ($n$ can change from section to section).
- It supports **hydraulic jumps** (mixed flow regimes) by checking Froude number at each section and switching between subcritical and supercritical computation.
- It converges **robustly** for most practical engineering problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Governing Energy Equation

The one-dimensional steady-flow energy equation between two cross-sections, **Section 1** (upstream) and **Section 2** (downstream), separated by a reach length $\Delta x$, is:

$$
H_1 = H_2 + h_L
$$

where $H$ is the total head and $h_L$ is the cumulative head loss in the reach.

**Step 1 — Define the total head at any section:**

The total head $H$ above an arbitrary horizontal datum is the sum of the bed elevation, the flow depth, and the velocity head (multiplied by the Coriolis coefficient $\alpha$):

$$
H = z + y + \alpha \frac{V^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the datum reference (z is the bed elevation):**

We work in terms of the water surface elevation $WSL = z + y$, but to make the energy equation explicit in all components, we expand:

$$
H_1 = z_1 + y_1 + \alpha_1 \frac{V_1^2}{2g}
$$

$$
H_2 = z_2 + y_2 + \alpha_2 \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the head loss $h_L$ as the sum of friction and eddy losses:**

The total head loss in a finite sub-reach has two components:

$$
h_L = h_f + h_e
$$

where $h_f$ is the frictional loss along the wetted boundary and $h_e$ is the eddy loss due to sudden expansion or contraction.

**Step 4 — Express the friction loss using the average friction slope:**

By definition, the friction slope $S_f$ is the rate of energy loss per unit length due to boundary friction:

$$
S_f = \frac{dH_f}{dx} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_f = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} S_f \, dx
$$

For a finite step $\Delta x = x_2 - x_1$, we approximate the integral using the **trapezoidal rule** (mean of end values):

$$
h_f = \overline{S_f} \cdot \Delta x = \frac{S_{f1} + S_{f2}}{2} \cdot \Delta x
$$

**Step 5 — Express the friction slope from Manning's equation:**

Manning's equation for uniform flow is $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S_0^{1/2}$. Generalising to non-uniform flow, we replace $S_0$ with $S_f$:

$$
V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S_f^{1/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_f = \frac{n^2 V^2}{R^{4/3}}
$$

**Step 6 — Express the eddy (transition) loss:**

When the cross-section changes abruptly, a fraction of the kinetic-energy difference between sections is dissipated as turbulence:

$$
h_e = K \left\vert \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} \right\vert
$$

The coefficient $K$ takes the value $K_e$ for expansions (typically **0.3 to 1.0**) and $K_c$ for contractions (typically **0.0 to 0.5**).

**Step 7 — Assemble the complete Standard Step Method equation:**

Combining Steps 1 through 6:

$$
z_1 + y_1 + \alpha_1 \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + y_2 + \alpha_2 \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + \frac{S_{f1} + S_{f2}}{2} \cdot \Delta x + K \left\vert \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} \right\vert
$$

This single equation, applied iteratively between successive cross-sections, is the **heart of the Standard Step Method**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A trapezoidal channel carries a discharge $Q = 25 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. The channel has a bed width $b = 5 \ \text{m}$, side slopes $1 \ \text{H : 1 \ V}$, Manning's $n = 0.025$, and a mild bed slope $S_0 = 0.0004$. At Section 2 (downstream end of a sub-reach), the depth is $y_2 = 2.4$ m. The sub-reach length is $\Delta x = 100$ m, and the bed drops by $S_0 \cdot \Delta x = 0.04$ m (so $z_1 - z_2 = 0.04$ m, with $z_2 = 0$ m, hence $z_1 = 0.04$ m). The flow is subcritical and $\alpha = 1.0$. Compute the depth $y_1$ at Section 1 using the Standard Step Method. Assume no transition losses ($K = 0$).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric properties of the channel as functions of depth $y$:**

$$
A(y) = (b + z \cdot y) y = (5 + y) y
$$

$$
P(y) = b + 2y\sqrt{1 + z^2} = 5 + 2y\sqrt{2}
$$

$$
R(y) = \frac{A}{P}
$$

(For a 1H:1V side slope, the horizontal projection per unit vertical is $z = 1$, so $\sqrt{1+z^2} = \sqrt{2}$.)

**Step 2 — Compute properties at Section 2 (known, $y_2 = 2.4$ m):**

$$
A_2 = (5 + 2.4) \cdot 2.4 = 7.4 \cdot 2.4 = 17.76 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
P_2 = 5 + 2(2.4)\sqrt{2} = 5 + 6.787 = 11.787 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{17.76}{11.787} = 1.5068 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{25}{17.76} = 1.4077 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.4077)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{1.9816}{19.62} = 0.1010 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
S_{f2} = \frac{n^2 V_2^2}{R_2^{4/3}} = \frac{(0.025)^2 (1.4077)^2}{(1.5068)^{4/3}}
$$

Compute $(1.5068)^{4/3}$: $\ln(1.5068) = 0.4100$, multiplied by $4/3$ gives $0.5467$, exponentiated gives $e^{0.5467} = 1.7276$.

$$
S_{f2} = \frac{0.000625 \cdot 1.9816}{1.7276} = \frac{0.0012385}{1.7276} = 0.0007169
$$

Total energy at Section 2:
$$
H_2 = z_2 + y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} = 0 + 2.4 + 0.1010 = 2.5010 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Guess $y_1$ and iterate.**

Initial guess: assume uniform-flow normal depth. For $Q = 25$ m$^3$/s, $n = 0.025$, $S_0 = 0.0004$, by Manning's:

$$
A R^{2/3} = \frac{Q n}{\sqrt{S_0}} = \frac{25 \cdot 0.025}{\sqrt{0.0004}} = \frac{0.625}{0.02} = 31.25
$$

Iterate: try $y_1 = 2.4$ m (same as $y_2$).
- $A_1 = (5 + 2.4)(2.4) = 17.76$ m$^2$
- $V_1 = 25 / 17.76 = 1.4077$ m/s
- $\dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.1010$ m
- $S_{f1} = 0.0007169$ (same as $S_{f2}$)
- $\overline{S_f} = 0.0007169$
- $h_f = 0.0007169 \times 100 = 0.07169$ m
- $H_1 = z_1 + y_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.04 + 2.4 + 0.1010 = 2.5410$ m
- $H_1 - H_2 - h_f = 2.5410 - 2.5010 - 0.07169 = -0.03169$ m

Residual is $-0.0317$ m, which is significant. We need to **adjust $y_1$**.

**Step 4 — Second iteration. Try $y_1 = 2.50$ m:**

$$
A_1 = (5 + 2.50)(2.50) = 18.75 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
P_1 = 5 + 2(2.50)\sqrt{2} = 5 + 7.071 = 12.071 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{18.75}{12.071} = 1.5533 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{25}{18.75} = 1.3333 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.3333)^2}{19.62} = \frac{1.7777}{19.62} = 0.0906 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
S_{f1} = \frac{0.000625 \cdot 1.7777}{(1.5533)^{4/3}}
$$

$(1.5533)^{4/3}$: $\ln(1.5533) = 0.4407$, $\times 4/3 = 0.5876$, $e^{0.5876} = 1.7995$.

$$
S_{f1} = \frac{0.0011111}{1.7995} = 0.0006174
$$

$$
\overline{S_f} = \frac{0.0006174 + 0.0007169}{2} = 0.0006672
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0006672 \times 100 = 0.06672 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
H_1 = 0.04 + 2.50 + 0.0906 = 2.6306 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta H = H_1 - H_2 - h_f = 2.6306 - 2.5010 - 0.06672 = 0.06288 \ \text{m}
$$

Residual changed sign (was $-0.0317$, now $+0.0629$). The true $y_1$ is between 2.40 and 2.50 m.

**Step 5 — Third iteration. Try $y_1 = 2.42$ m:**

$$
A_1 = (5 + 2.42)(2.42) = 7.42 \cdot 2.42 = 17.956 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
P_1 = 5 + 2(2.42)\sqrt{2} = 5 + 6.8449 = 11.8449 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{17.956}{11.8449} = 1.51599 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{25}{17.956} = 1.3923 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{1.9385}{19.62} = 0.0988 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
S_{f1} = \frac{0.000625 \cdot 1.9385}{(1.51599)^{4/3}}
$$

$(1.51599)^{4/3}$: $\ln(1.51599) = 0.4159$, $\times 4/3 = 0.5545$, $e^{0.5545} = 1.7411$.

$$
S_{f1} = \frac{0.0012116}{1.7411} = 0.0006959
$$

$$
\overline{S_f} = \frac{0.0006959 + 0.0007169}{2} = 0.0007064
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0007064 \times 100 = 0.07064 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
H_1 = 0.04 + 2.42 + 0.0988 = 2.5588 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta H = 2.5588 - 2.5010 - 0.07064 = -0.01284 \ \text{m}
$$

Residual still negative but smaller magnitude. Try $y_1 = 2.435$ m.

**Step 6 — Fourth iteration. Try $y_1 = 2.435$ m:**

$$
A_1 = (5 + 2.435)(2.435) = 7.435 \cdot 2.435 = 18.104 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
P_1 = 5 + 2(2.435)(1.4142) = 5 + 6.8880 = 11.8880 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{18.104}{11.888} = 1.5228 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{25}{18.104} = 1.3809 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{1.9069}{19.62} = 0.0972 \ \text{m}
$$

$(1.5228)^{4/3}$: $\ln(1.5228) = 0.4203$, $\times 4/3 = 0.5604$, $e^{0.5604} = 1.7515$.

$$
S_{f1} = \frac{0.000625 \cdot 1.9069}{1.7515} = \frac{0.0011918}{1.7515} = 0.0006805
$$

$$
\overline{S_f} = \frac{0.0006805 + 0.0007169}{2} = 0.0006987
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0006987 \times 100 = 0.06987 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
H_1 = 0.04 + 2.435 + 0.0972 = 2.5722 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta H = 2.5722 - 2.5010 - 0.06987 = 0.00133 \ \text{m}
$$

Residual $\approx 0.0013$ m, which is within typical tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.01$ m. **Converged.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $y_1 \approx 2.435$ m. The water surface elevation at Section 1 is $WSL_1 = z_1 + y_1 = 0.04 + 2.435 = 2.475$ m, while at Section 2 it is $WSL_2 = 0 + 2.4 = 2.4$ m. The water surface rises by 0.075 m against the flow direction over 100 m, consistent with a mild-slope M1 backwater profile (depth increases upstream of a control).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram of the Standard Step Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[Define Channel Geometry, Q, n, S0]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Control Section with known WSL]
    A2 --> A3[Divide Reach into Sub-reaches at Cross Sections]
    A3 --> A4[Pick a Sub-reach with known WSE at one end]
    A4 --> A5[Assume initial depth y at unknown end]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Area A, Perimeter P, R, V at unknown end]
    A6 --> A7[Compute friction slope Sf at both ends]
    A7 --> A8[Compute average friction slope Sfb]
    A8 --> A9[Compute friction loss hf and eddy loss he]
    A9 --> A10[Apply Energy Equation H1 vs H2+hf+he]
    A10 --> A11{Residual less than epsilon?}
    A11 -- No --> A12[Adjust assumed depth y]
    A12 --> A6
    A11 -- Yes --> A13[Store final WSE for this section]
    A13 --> A14{All sub-reaches done?}
    A14 -- No --> A4
    A14 -- Yes --> A15[Plot Water Surface Profile]
    A15 --> A16([End])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the SSM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data Block]
        I1[Channel Geometry x n]
        I2[Discharge Q]
        I3[Bed Slope S0]
        I4[Boundary WSE at Control]
    end

    subgraph GEOM[Geometry Engine]
        G1[A = fy function]
        G2[P = fy function]
        G3[R = A by P]
        G4[V = Q by A]
    end

    subgraph ENERGY[Energy Solver Core]
        E1[Compute Sf at section]
        E2[Average Sf over reach]
        E3[Compute hf and he]
        E4[Apply Energy Equation]
    end

    subgraph ITER[Iteration Controller]
        T1[Convergence Check]
        T2[Depth Adjuster]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Block]
        O1[Water Surface Elevation at every section]
        O2[Profile Plot]
    end

    I1 --> G1
    I1 --> G2
    I2 --> G4
    G1 --> G3
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    I3 --> E3
    I4 --> E4
    E4 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> G1
    T1 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Cross-Section Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Section 1 Upstream]
        S1B[Bed z1]
        S1Y[Depth y1]
        S1V[Velocity V1]
    end
    subgraph Reach[Sub reach length Delta x]
        REACH[Friction loss hf]
        REACH2[Eddy loss he]
    end
    subgraph S2[Section 2 Downstream]
        S2B[Bed z2]
        S2Y[Depth y2]
        S2V[Velocity V2]
    end
    S1 --> REACH
    S1 --> REACH2
    REACH --> S2
    REACH2 --> S2
```
