y3 < y2] SUB -->|Strong submergence| DROW[Drowned Jump
Energy lost in turbulence] SWEPT -->|Move downstream| WAVES[Wave Train
Energy remains as waves] style P1 fill:#cce5ff style P2 fill:#d4f4d4 style SUB fill:#ffd4b3 style FREE fill:#d4f4d4 style SWEPT fill:#ffe1e1 style DROW fill:#ffd4d4 style WAVES fill:#ffd4d4 ``` > [!TIP] > **How to Read These Diagrams**: The block-level diagrams use Mermaid `flowchart` syntax. Each node represents a **state or computation stage**. Arrows represent the **flow of analysis logic**. Color coding: **Blue** = input/state, **Yellow** = computation, **Green** = success/optimal, **Red** = failure/redesign needed. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a hydraulic jump. What are the different types of hydraulic jumps based on the Froude number of incoming flow?** > **Model Answer (3 marks)**: > > A **hydraulic jump** is a rapidly varied flow phenomenon in which supercritical flow (Fr > 1) abruptly transitions to subcritical flow (Fr < 1) over a short length, accompanied by significant turbulence and energy dissipation. > > Based on the upstream Froude number, hydraulic jumps are classified into five categories (1 mark each): > > | Type | Froude Number Range | > |------|---------------------| > | Undular Jump | 1.0 < Fr₁ < 1.7 | > | Weak Jump | 1.7 < Fr₁ < 2.5 | > | Oscillating Jump | 2.5 < Fr₁ < 4.5 | > | Steady Jump | 4.5 < Fr₁ < 9.0 | > | Strong/Choppy Jump | Fr₁ > 9.0 | ### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the significance of the slope parameter K in the analysis of hydraulic jump in sloping channels.** > **Model Answer (3 marks)**: > > The slope parameter **K** is a dimensionless correction factor that accounts for the effect of the channel bed slope on the hydraulic jump (1 mark). It is defined as $K = \frac{L_j \tan\theta}{y_2 - y_1}$ (1 mark). > > **Significance**: > - For horizontal channels (θ = 0), K = 0 and the equation reduces to the classical Bélanger form. > - For sloping channels, K > 0, and the effective driving Froude number is modified, leading to a **reduced conjugate depth ratio**. > - The slope parameter essentially represents the ratio of the **gravity force component along the flow** to the **pressure thrust difference** (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – KTU ESE Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: In problems involving **sloping channels**, students frequently **omit the $W \sin\theta$ term** in the momentum equation, treating it as a horizontal channel problem. This typically results in a **3-4 mark deduction**. Always explicitly draw the free-body diagram and identify the gravity component. ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive the conjugate depth relationship for a hydraulic jump in a **horizontal rectangular channel** using the momentum equation. Starting from first principles, clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A supercritical flow in a rectangular channel of width **b = 5 m** has depth $y_1 = 0.4$ m and velocity $V_1 = 8$ m/s. Calculate: **(i)** the conjugate depth $y_2$, **(ii)** the energy loss $\Delta E$, and **(iii)** the length of the jump $L_j$. Also classify the type of jump based on the incoming Froude number. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) - Derivation (7 marks)**: **Assumptions (1 mark)**: - Steady, incompressible flow - Hydrostatic pressure distribution at sections 1 and 2 - Frictionless channel (negligible boundary shear) - Prismatic rectangular channel - Momentum correction factor β = 1.0 **Derivation steps (6 marks)**: Apply linear momentum equation between sections 1 and 2: $$\frac{1}{2} \gamma b y_1^2 - \frac{1}{2} \gamma b y_2^2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$ Using continuity $Q = V_1 y_1 b = V_2 y_2 b$: $$\frac{y_1^2 - y_2^2}{2} = \frac{Q^2}{g b^2} \left( \frac{1}{y_2} - \frac{1}{y_1} \right)$$ Substituting $\frac{Q^2}{g b^2} = Fr_1^2 \cdot y_1^3$ and simplifying: $$y_2^2 + y_1 y_2 - 2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2 = 0$$ Solving this quadratic for the positive root: $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1 \right)$$ **Part (b) - Numerical Solution (7 marks)**: **Step 1: Compute Froude number (1 mark)**: $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.4}} = \frac{8}{1.981} = 4.04$$ **Step 2: Conjugate depth $y_2$ (2 marks)**: $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 8 \times (4.04)^2} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 130.57} - 1 \right)$$ $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{131.57} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2} (11.471 - 1) = 5.236$$ $$y_2 = 5.236 \times 0.4 = 2.094 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Energy loss $\Delta E$ (2 marks)**: $$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \frac{(2.094 - 0.4)^3}{4 \times 0.4 \times 2.094}$$ $$\Delta E = \frac{(1.694)^3}{3.350} = \frac{4.860}{3.350} = 1.451 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Length of jump (1 mark)**: $$L_j = 6.1 \times y_2 = 6.1 \times 2.094 = 12.77 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Classification (1 mark)**: Since $2.5 < Fr_1 = 4.04 < 4.5$, this is an **oscillating jump** (borderline). If the precise value of 4.04 places it just below 4.5, it can be classified as the **upper end of oscillating jump**. For $Fr_1 > 4.5$, it would be a **steady jump**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **classification of hydraulic jumps based on tail water conditions**. Discuss the phenomenon of **submerged jump** and define the submergence factor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic jump occurs in a sloping rectangular channel of width **b = 4 m**, bed slope **1 in 100** (i.e., tanθ = 0.01), with upstream depth $y_1 = 0.5$ m and velocity $V_1 = 6$ m/s. The length of jump is $L_j = 8$ m. Calculate: **(i)** the slope parameter K, **(ii)** the modified conjugate depth $y_2$, and **(iii)** the tail water condition classification if the actual tail water depth is $y_{TW} = 2.3$ m. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) - Theory (7 marks)**: [Student is expected to draw a sketch showing the three cases with the following key points - 1 mark for diagram, 6 marks for explanation] The three tail water conditions are: **Case 1: Free Jump (3 marks)** - When $y_{TW} = y_2$ (conjugate depth), the jump forms at the **predicted location**. - This is the **ideal, textbook condition**. - Energy dissipation is maximum and predictable. - The conjugate depth $y_2$ is given by the Bélanger equation. **Case 2: Submerged (Drowned) Jump (2 marks)** - When $y_{TW} > y_2$, the jump is **pushed upstream** of the theoretical position. - The jump is partially drowned, and a **backwater profile** may form upstream. - Energy dissipation is **reduced** compared to a free jump. - The **submergence factor** is defined as $S = y_3 / y_2$, where $y_3$ is the actual tail water depth. **Case 3: Swept-out Jump (2 marks)** - When $y_{TW} < y_2$, the jump is **pushed downstream** from the theoretical position. - The jump may not form at the desired location, often resulting in **wave trains** that travel downstream. - This is undesirable in design as it can cause erosion. **Part (b) - Numerical Solution (7 marks)**: **Step 1: Compute Froude number (1 mark)**: $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5}} = \frac{6}{2.215} = 2.709$$ **Step 2: Compute slope parameter K (2 marks)**: For a first calculation, we need an initial estimate of $y_2$. Using Bélanger's equation (horizontal approximation): $$\frac{y_2^{(0)}}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 8 \times (2.709)^2} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 58.71} - 1 \right) = 3.59$$ $$y_2^{(0)} = 3.59 \times 0.5 = 1.795 \text{ m}$$ Now compute K: $$K = \frac{L_j \tan\theta}{y_2 - y_1} = \frac{8 \times 0.01}{1.795 - 0.5} = \frac{0.08}{1.295} = 0.0618$$ **Step 3: Modified conjugate depth (2 marks)**: Using the sloping channel equation: $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2 (1+K)} - 1 \right)$$ $$= \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 8 \times 7.338 \times 1.0618} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 62.32} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2} (7.951 - 1) = 3.476$$ $$y_2 = 3.476 \times 0.5 = 1.738 \text{ m}$$ **Iterative refinement (1 mark)**: Recompute K with $y_2 = 1.738$ m: $$K = \frac{0.08}{1.738 - 0.5} = \frac{0.08}{1.238} = 0.0646$$ $$y_2^{(new)} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 8 \times 7.338 \times 1.0646} - 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sqrt{1 + 62.50} - 1 \right) = 3.485$$ $$y_2 \approx 1.743 \text{ m} \quad (\text{converged})$$ **Step 4: Tail water classification (1 mark)**: $$S = \frac{y_{TW}}{y_2} = \frac{2.3}{1.743} = 1.32$$ Since $S = 1.32 > 1.2$, the jump is **strongly submerged (drowned)**. The jump will be pushed upstream of its theoretical position with reduced energy dissipation efficiency. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations (Module 3)**: > > 1. **Omitting the $W \sin\theta$ term in sloping channel problems** — This single mistake costs **3-4 marks** because the entire physics of the problem changes. Always draw the free-body diagram. > > 2. **Confusing $y_2$ and $y_3$** — $y_2$ is the **conjugate depth** (theoretical), $y_3$ is the **actual tail water depth**. Mixing these up gives a wrong submergence factor. > > 3. **Forgetting to iterate in sloping channel problems** — The slope parameter K depends on $y_2$, but $y_2$ depends on K. KTU examiners expect **at least one iteration** of refinement. > > 4. **Sign convention errors** — In the momentum equation for sloping channels, ensure $W \sin\theta$ is **subtracted** from the pressure thrust (it acts in the direction of flow, opposing the net force needed for momentum reduction). > > 5. **Not specifying the type of jump** — Numerical problems should always end with a **classification** (free/submerged/swept-out) and the **submergence factor S**. Examiners allot **1 mark** for this conclusion. > > 6. **Using horizontal channel formula for sloping channels** — KTU's question paper often mentions "sloping channel" specifically to test the slope correction. Using $K = 0$ results in a **fundamentally incorrect answer**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Hydraulic Jump in Sloping and Exponential Channels** ### Core Concepts - ☐ RVF = Rapidly Varied Flow; depth changes abruptly over a short distance. - ☐ Hydraulic Jump = Canonical RVF; supercritical to subcritical transition. - ☐ Bélanger's equation: $\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1)$ — **horizontal rectangular channel**. - ☐ Energy loss: $\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$. - ☐ Jump length (USBR): $L_j = 6.1 y_2$. ### Sloping Channel Adjustments - ☐ Slope parameter: $K = \frac{L_j \tan\theta}{y_2 - y_1}$. - ☐ Modified equation: $\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2(1+K)} - 1)$. - ☐ Iterative solution required since K depends on $y_2$. - ☐ Gravity component $W \sin\theta$ acts in the direction of supercritical flow. - ☐ **Sign convention**: $W \sin\theta$ is subtracted from pressure thrust. ### Exponential Channel Adjustments - ☐ Width or area varies exponentially: $b = b_0 e^{\sigma x}$ or $A = A_0 e^{\sigma x}$. - ☐ Width ratio: $\lambda = b_2/b_1 = e^{\sigma L}$. - ☐ Equation: $\frac{1}{2}[\lambda(y_2/y_1)^2 - 1] = Fr_1^2[\frac{1}{\lambda(y_2/y_1)} - 1]$. - ☐ Solved as a quadratic in $y_2/y_1$. ### Tail Water Classification - ☐ **Free Jump**: $y_{TW} = y_2$, S = 1.0. - ☐ **Submerged Jump**: $y_{TW} > y_2$, S > 1.0. - ☐ **Swept-out Jump**: $y_{TW} < y_2$, S < 1.0. - ☐ **Submergence factor**: $S = y_3/y_2$. - ☐ **Strongly submerged** when S > 1.2. ### Froude Number Based Classification - ☐ Undular: 1.0 < Fr₁ < 1.7. - ☐ Weak: 1.7 < Fr₁ < 2.5. - ☐ Oscillating: 2.5 < Fr₁ < 4.5. - ☐ Steady: 4.5 < Fr₁ < 9.0. - ☐ Strong/Choppy: Fr₁ > 9.0. ### Engineering Applications - ☐ Stilling basins (USBR Type II, III, IV). - ☐ Spillway energy dissipators. - ☐ Drop structures in irrigation canals. - ☐ Dam toe protection. - ☐ **Ideal design range**: Fr₁ = 4.5 to 9.0 for maximum efficiency. ### Numerical Problem-Solving Steps - ☐ Step 1: Compute $Fr_1 = V_1/\sqrt{g y_1}$. - ☐ Step 2: For horizontal channel, use Bélanger directly. - ☐ Step 3: For sloping channel, iterate using $K$ and the modified equation. - ☐ Step 4: For exponential channel, solve the quadratic in $y_2/y_1$. - ☐ Step 5: Compute $\Delta E$ and $L_j$. - ☐ Step 6: Classify the jump type and tail water condition. ### Units and Constants to Memorize - ☐ $g = 9.81$ m/s². - ☐ $\gamma_w = 9810$ N/m³. - ☐ $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³. - ☐ $\beta \approx 1.0$ (momentum coefficient). ### Frequently Confused Pairs - ☐ $y_1$ vs $y_2$ — supercritical vs subcritical depth. - ☐ $y_2$ vs $y_3$ — conjugate vs tail water depth. - ☐ Fr₁ vs Fr₂ — upstream vs downstream Froude number. - ☐ $\Delta E$ vs $E_1 - E_2$ — energy loss vs specific energy difference. - ☐ Slope parameter $K$ vs Froude number $Fr$ — both dimensionless, but different physical meaning.

topics.title: Uses of Hydraulic Jumps for Energy Dissipation Below Spillways - Jump Height Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Uses of Hydraulic Jumps for Energy Dissipation Below Spillways - Jump Height Curve # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydraulic Jump (KTU 2024 Definition):** A hydraulic jump is a rapidly varied flow phenomenon in open channels where a sudden and turbulent transition occurs from **supercritical flow** (high velocity, low depth, Fr₁ > 1) to **subcritical flow** (low velocity, high depth, Fr₂ < 1). This transition is accompanied by a significant loss of kinetic energy, surface turbulence, and air entrainment. In the context of **spillways and energy dissipators**, the hydraulic jump serves as one of the most effective and economical means of dissipating the destructive kinetic energy of high-velocity flow before it enters the downstream river/canal bed. ## 1.2 The Jump Height Curve (Conjugate Depth Curve) > [!NOTE] > **Jump Height Curve (Conjugate Depth Curve):** The functional relationship between the upstream supercritical depth (y₁) and the downstream subcritical depth (y₂) of a hydraulic jump, expressed as a curve y₂ = f(y₁, Fr₁) for a given discharge. The vertical distance between the two conjugate depths is termed the **Jump Height (hⱼ)**, where hⱼ = y₂ − y₁. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are riding a bicycle at very high speed (say 40 km/h) on a flat road and suddenly you press the brakes hard. The bicycle comes to a near-stop, but your body jolts forward, and you feel a sudden "thud." That thud is your body's kinetic energy being converted into heat (in the brakes) and turbulence (in your arms/legs). Now replace the bicycle with water flowing over a dam spillway. Water rushes down the spillway at enormous velocities (like the speeding bicycle). If this water were allowed to enter the still river below at that speed, it would scour the riverbed, causing catastrophic erosion (like a speeding car crashing). **The hydraulic jump acts as the "brake" of the river.** A horizontal concrete apron (stilling basin) is built at the toe of the spillway. When the fast, shallow water hits this apron, it is forced to suddenly rise to a deeper, slower depth — this is the jump. In the process, **up to 60–70% of the kinetic energy is destroyed** as turbulence and heat. **The Jump Height Curve** is simply a design graph that tells the engineer: "If your water is entering the basin at depth y₁ and velocity V₁, it will *necessarily* rise to depth y₂ after the jump." This y₂ must match the existing tailwater depth — otherwise the jump will either be swept downstream (swept-out) or drowned inside the basin (submerged), both of which reduce efficiency. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Energy Dissipator Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Quantities:** > - **Discharge per unit width (q)** — measured in **m³/s/m** > - **Froude Number of incoming flow (Fr₁)** — dimensionless, **1.7 to 2.5 (weak jump)**, **2.5 to 4.5 (oscillating jump)**, **4.5 to 9.0 (steady jump)**, **> 9.0 (strong/choppy jump)** > - **Conjugate depths (y₁, y₂)** — measured in **m** > - **Energy loss (ΔE)** — measured in **m** > - **Jump length (Lⱼ)** — measured in **m** > - **Standard gravity (g = 9.81 m/s²)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Hydraulic Jumps are Used Below Spillways — Engineering Logic When water flows over a spillway, the following issues arise if it enters the downstream channel uncontrolled: 1. **Scouring of the river bed** due to high-velocity jets 2. **Lateral erosion** of banks 3. **Hydrodynamic uplift** forces on downstream structures 4. **Cavitation damage** to concrete surfaces 5. **Standing waves and eddies** that destabilize flow A **hydraulic jump dissipator (stilling basin)** solves all these by: - Converting **kinetic energy → turbulent energy → heat** - Stabilizing the flow regime to subcritical - Reducing the velocity at the basin exit to safe values (typically < 2–3 m/s) - Restoring normal depth conditions rapidly ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework ### Step 1: Identify the Incoming Flow Condition The flow at the toe of the spillway (start of the apron) is supercritical. Compute: - Velocity V₁ - Depth y₁ - Froude number Fr₁ = V₁ / √(g·y₁) - Specific energy E₁ = y₁ + V₁²/(2g) ### Step 2: Apply the Bélanger Equation (Conjugate Depth Equation) The downstream conjugate depth y₂ is found from the **Bélanger equation**, derived from the momentum equation across the jump: $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8\,Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Jump Height The vertical rise of the free surface across the jump is: $$h_j = y_2 - y_1$$ When y₂ is plotted against y₁ for a given q, the resulting locus is the **Jump Height Curve**. Each point on the curve represents a unique conjugate pair. ### Step 4: Compute Energy Dissipation The energy lost in the jump is: $$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4\,y_1\,y_2}$$ ### Step 5: Compute Efficiency of the Jump $$\eta = \frac{E_2}{E_1} \times 100\%$$ For a strong jump (Fr₁ > 4.5), η can drop to 20–40%, meaning 60–80% of energy is destroyed. ### Step 6: Compute the Length of the Jump (Lⱼ) Empirical formulae (USBR, Elevatorski, etc.): - **USBR (Bradley):** Lⱼ ≈ 6.1 × y₂ (for Fr₁ = 4 to 13) - **Elevatorski:** Lⱼ = 6.9 (y₂ − y₁) - **Khurgi's formula:** Lⱼ = 10.3 (y₂ − y₁) Fr₁⁻⁰·⁸¹ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is a high-yield reference for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Memorize all entries.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------------------| | 1 | Froude Number | $Fr_1 = V_1 / \sqrt{g\,y_1}$ | dimensionless | $Fr_1 > 1$ for supercritical | | 2 | Bélanger Equation | $y_2/y_1 = 0.5\,(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1)$ | dimensionless ratio | Valid for all jump types | | 3 | Jump Height | $h_j = y_2 - y_1$ | m | Positive quantity | | 4 | Specific Energy | $E = y + V^2/(2g)$ | m | Energy per unit weight | | 5 | Energy Loss in Jump | $\Delta E = (y_2 - y_1)^3 \,/\, (4\,y_1\,y_2)$ | m | Derives from momentum + energy | | 6 | Energy Loss Ratio | $\Delta E/E_1 = 1 - (y_2 + q^2/(2g\,y_2^2)) \,/\, E_1$ | dimensionless | Used in numerical problems | | 7 | Jump Efficiency | $\eta = E_2/E_1 \times 100$ | % | Higher η = less dissipation | | 8 | Discharge Intensity | $q = V_1 \cdot y_1$ | m³/s/m | Per unit width | | 9 | Sequent Depth (alt) | $y_2^2(2y_2 + y_1) = (2y_1^2 + 8q^2/g)\cdot y_1/y_1$ | m | Cubic form of Bélanger | | 10 | USBR Jump Length | $L_j \approx 6.1\,y_2$ | m | For $4 \le Fr_1 \le 13$ | | 11 | Elevatorski Length | $L_j = 6.9\,(y_2 - y_1)$ | m | General empirical | | 12 | Power Dissipated | $P = \rho\,g\,q\,\Delta E$ | W/m | Energy lost per second per m width | | 13 | Tailwater Check | $y_{tw} \approx y_2$ for ideal jump | m | Determines basin floor level | | 14 | Critical Depth | $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3}$ | m | Reference for energy diagrams | | 15 | Submergence Factor | $S = y_{tw}/y_2$ | dimensionless | $S < 1$ swept-out; $S > 1$ submerged | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Applications:** > - **Idukki Dam (Kerala), Bhakra Nangal (Himachal Pradesh), Hoover Dam (USA):** Stilling basins at the toe of spillways protect the plunge pools from scour. > - **Drop structures on irrigation canals** (Kerala PWD, KWA): Hydraulic jumps dissipate energy at each fall. > - **Sluice gates and barrages:** When gates are opened, hydraulic jumps form in the downstream apron. > - **Hydroelectric tailraces:** The flow from turbines still possesses high velocity; a tailrace jump dissipates this energy. > - **Stormwater outflows:** Energy dissipators prevent erosion of natural streams below culvert outlets. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Bélanger Equation (Conjugate Depth Relation) This is the **most important derivation in Module 3** for KTU 2024. We will derive it in exhaustive detail. ### 3.1.1 Setup — Control Volume of the Hydraulic Jump Consider a hydraulic jump occurring in a horizontal, rectangular, prismatic channel of width *b*. The flow enters the jump at section 1 (supercritical) and exits at section 2 (subcritical). - Section 1: Depth = y₁, Velocity = V₁, Pressure distribution = hydrostatic - Section 2: Depth = y₂, Velocity = V₂, Pressure distribution = hydrostatic - Channel bed is horizontal → no bed slope component - Energy loss across jump = ΔE - Momentum is conserved (no external forces in the longitudinal direction, ignoring friction over the short jump length) ### 3.1.2 Apply the Momentum Equation (Impulse-Momentum Principle) For a control volume between sections 1 and 2 in a horizontal channel, the **momentum equation** (per unit width) is: $$P_1 - P_2 = \rho\,q\,(V_2 - V_1)$$ where P₁ and P₂ are the hydrostatic pressure forces per unit width on the vertical sections: $$P_1 = \rho\,g\,\frac{y_1^2}{2}, \quad P_2 = \rho\,g\,\frac{y_2^2}{2}$$ By continuity, V₁ = q/y₁ and V₂ = q/y₂. Substituting: $$\rho\,g\,\frac{y_1^2}{2} - \rho\,g\,\frac{y_2^2}{2} = \rho\,q\left(\frac{q}{y_2} - \frac{q}{y_1}\right)$$ Dividing by ρg throughout: $$\frac{y_1^2}{2} - \frac{y_2^2}{2} = \frac{q^2}{g}\left(\frac{1}{y_2} - \frac{1}{y_1}\right)$$ $$\frac{y_1^2 - y_2^2}{2} = \frac{q^2}{g}\cdot\frac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1\,y_2}$$ Factor (y₁ − y₂) on both sides: $$\frac{(y_1 + y_2)(y_1 - y_2)}{2} = \frac{q^2\,(y_1 - y_2)}{g\,y_1\,y_2}$$ Since y₁ ≠ y₂, divide by (y₁ − y₂): $$\frac{y_1 + y_2}{2} = \frac{q^2}{g\,y_1\,y_2}$$ ### 3.1.3 Introduce the Froude Number Multiply both sides by 2/y₁: $$1 + \frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{2\,q^2}{g\,y_1^2\,y_2}$$ The Froude number at section 1 is: $$Fr_1^2 = \frac{V_1^2}{g\,y_1} = \frac{q^2}{g\,y_1^3}$$ Substitute q²/g = Fr₁²·y₁³: $$1 + \frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{2\,Fr_1^2\,y_1^3}{y_1^2\,y_2} = \frac{2\,Fr_1^2\,y_1}{y_2}$$ Multiply throughout by y₂/y₁: $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} + \left(\frac{y_2}{y_1}\right)^2 = 2\,Fr_1^2$$ ### 3.1.4 Solve the Quadratic Equation Let r = y₂/y₁. Then: $$r^2 + r - 2\,Fr_1^2 = 0$$ Applying the quadratic formula, r = [−1 ± √(1 + 8Fr₁²)]/2. Taking the positive root (since y₂ > y₁): $$\boxed{\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8\,Fr_1^2} - 1\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the Bélanger Equation (1828) — a cornerstone of Open Channel Hydraulics.** It is examinable for full marks in the KTU 2024 ESE. ## 3.2 Derivation of Energy Loss Across the Jump The specific energy at any section is: $$E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g} = y + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y^2}$$ The energy loss is: $$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \left(y_1 + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y_1^2}\right) - \left(y_2 + \frac{q^2}{2g\,y_2^2}\right)$$ Using the momentum result q²/(g) = (y₁·y₂)(y₁ + y₂)/2, we can simplify: $$\Delta E = (y_1 - y_2) + \frac{q^2}{2g}\left(\frac{1}{y_1^2} - \frac{1}{y_2^2}\right)$$ After algebraic manipulation (substituting the momentum-derived q²/g relation and factoring), we obtain the elegant closed form: $$\boxed{\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4\,y_1\,y_2}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 14-Mark Style) > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem:** Water flows down a spillway and enters a horizontal stilling basin at depth y₁ = 0.5 m with a velocity V₁ = 18 m/s. The basin is rectangular with width b = 10 m. Determine: (a) the Froude number of the incoming flow and classify the jump, (b) the conjugate (sequent) depth y₂, (c) the jump height hⱼ, (d) the energy loss ΔE and efficiency η, (e) the length of the jump using USBR formula. ### Step 1: Compute the Froude Number Fr₁ $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g\,y_1}} = \frac{18}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5}} = \frac{18}{\sqrt{4.905}} = \frac{18}{2.2147} = 8.126$$ Since Fr₁ = 8.126, it lies between 4.5 and 9.0 → **Steady Jump (well-suited for energy dissipation).** ### Step 2: Compute the Sequent Depth y₂ using Bélanger Equation $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(8.126)^2} - 1\right)$$ $$8(8.126)^2 = 8 \times 66.032 = 528.255$$ $$1 + 528.255 = 529.255$$ $$\sqrt{529.255} = 23.005$$ $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}(23.005 - 1) = \frac{1}{2}(22.005) = 11.003$$ $$y_2 = 11.003 \times 0.5 = 5.501\,m \approx 5.50\,m$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Jump Height hⱼ $$h_j = y_2 - y_1 = 5.50 - 0.50 = 5.00\,m$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Energy Loss ΔE $$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4\,y_1\,y_2} = \frac{(5.00)^3}{4 \times 0.5 \times 5.50} = \frac{125}{11.0} = 11.364\,m$$ ### Step 5: Compute the Efficiency η First, specific energies: $$E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.5 + \frac{(18)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.5 + \frac{324}{19.62} = 0.5 + 16.514 = 17.014\,m$$ Velocity after the jump: V₂ = q/y₂, where q = V₁·y₁ = 18 × 0.5 = 9 m³/s/m. $$V_2 = \frac{9}{5.50} = 1.636\,m/s$$ $$E_2 = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} = 5.50 + \frac{(1.636)^2}{19.62} = 5.50 + \frac{2.677}{19.62} = 5.50 + 0.1365 = 5.636\,m$$ $$\eta = \frac{E_2}{E_1}\times 100 = \frac{5.636}{17.014}\times 100 = 33.12\%$$ **Energy dissipated = 100 − 33.12 = 66.88% of incoming energy** ✓ (consistent with a strong jump) ### Step 6: Length of the Jump (USBR) $$L_j = 6.1 \times y_2 = 6.1 \times 5.50 = 33.55\,m$$ ### Summary Table for the Problem | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units | |----------|--------|-------|-------| | Froude Number | Fr₁ | 8.13 | — | | Jump Type | — | Steady (4.5–9) | — | | Sequent Depth | y₂ | 5.50 | m | | Jump Height | hⱼ | 5.00 | m | | Energy Loss | ΔE | 11.36 | m | | Efficiency | η | 33.12 | % | | Jump Length | Lⱼ | 33.55 | m | | V₂ (outflow) | V₂ | 1.64 | m/s | ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Jump Height Curve Behaviour | Froude Number Fr₁ | y₂/y₁ | Jump Type | % Energy Dissipated | Design Suitability | |-------------------|-------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------| | 1.0 | 1.00 | No jump | 0% | No jump forms | | 1.7 | 2.27 | Weak | < 5% | Avoid — poor dissipation | | 2.5 | 3.27 | Weak to Oscillating | 10–15% | Marginal | | 4.5 | 5.32 | Oscillating to Steady | 45% | Acceptable | | 6.0 | 7.00 | Steady | 57% | **Best for design** | | 8.0 | 9.16 | Steady | 66% | **Best for design** | | 10.0 | 11.32 | Strong | 72% | **Best for design** | | > 13.0 | > 14.5 | Choppy | > 75% | Excessive wave action | > [!TIP] > **Designer's rule of thumb (KTU 2024):** For practical stilling basin design, target **Fr₁ between 4.5 and 9.0** to balance high energy dissipation, predictable jump length, and manageable surface波动 (waves). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Hydraulic Jump Energy Dissipator System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Reservoir
High Water Level] --> B[Spillway Crest
Controlled Overflow] B --> C[Spillway Chute
Steep Slope, Supercritical Flow] C --> D[Toe of Spillway
Depth y1, Velocity V1] D --> E{Hydraulic Jump
on Stilling Basin} E --> F[Sequent Depth y2
Subcritical Flow] F --> G[Tailwater Channel
River or Canal] E --> H[Energy Loss deltaE
66 to 70 percent] E --> I[Jump Height hj
y2 minus y1] E --> J[Jump Length Lj
6.1 times y2] H --> K[Heat and Turbulence
in Basin] K --> L[Safe Downstream
Channel] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Jump Height Curve Generation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Discharge q] --> B[Input y1] B --> C[Compute Fr1] C --> D{Apply Belanger
Equation} D --> E[Compute y2] E --> F[Compute hj equals y2 minus y1] F --> G[Plot Curve y2 vs y1] G --> H[Tabulate for design] D --> I[Compute deltaE] I --> J[Compute Efficiency eta] J --> K[Stilling Basin
Design Output] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Process Flow — Stilling Basin Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1 Identify q and y1 at Basin Inlet] --> B[Step 2 Calculate Fr1] B --> C{Fr1 in 4.5 to 9.0 range?} C -->|No| D[Use Baffle Piers or Sills to Strengthen Jump] C -->|Yes| E[Step 3 Calculate y2 using Belanger] D --> E E --> F[Step 4 Compare y2 with Tailwater ytw] F --> G{ytw approx y2?} G -->|No| H[Adjust Basin Floor Elevation] G -->|Yes| I[Step 5 Calculate deltaE and Lj] I --> J[Step 6 Finalize Basin Length and Width] J --> K[Step 7 Design Appurtenances
Chute Blocks, Baffle Piers, End Sill] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows indicate the sequential flow of information (top-down) or the logical progression of design (left-right). Each block represents a calculation step or a decision point in the KTU 2024 stilling basin design procedure. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a hydraulic jump. List the different types of hydraulic jumps based on the Froude number of the incoming flow.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** A hydraulic jump is a rapidly varied, turbulent transition of open channel flow from supercritical (Fr > 1) to subcritical (Fr < 1) state, characterized by a sudden rise in water surface, intense turbulence, air entrainment, and significant energy dissipation. **Types of Hydraulic Jumps (based on Fr₁):** | Fr₁ Range | Type of Jump | Energy Loss (%) | |-----------|--------------|-----------------| | 1.0 < Fr₁ < 1.7 | No jump (flow remains supercritical) | 0 | | 1.7 ≤ Fr₁ < 2.5 | Weak jump | < 5 | | 2.5 ≤ Fr₁ < 4.5 | Oscillating jump | 5–45 | | 4.5 ≤ Fr₁ < 9.0 | Steady jump | 45–70 | | Fr₁ ≥ 9.0 | Strong/Choppy jump | 70–85 | **Engineering Use:** For spillway energy dissipation, the **steady jump** (Fr₁ = 4.5 to 9.0) is preferred because it has a stable, well-defined location and high dissipation efficiency. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the Bélanger equation. What is the significance of the jump height curve in stilling basin design?* **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bélanger Equation:** The relationship between the conjugate depths of a hydraulic jump in a rectangular channel is given by: > $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8\,Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$ **Significance of the Jump Height Curve:** 1. It provides a **graphical design tool** to determine the conjugate (sequent) depth y₂ for any given upstream depth y₁ and discharge q. 2. The curve hⱼ = y₂ − y₁ directly indicates the **required depth of the stilling basin walls** to contain the jump. 3. By superposing the **tailwater depth curve** on the jump height curve, the engineer can determine the **basin floor elevation** such that the jump forms at the desired location (toe of the spillway). 4. It is used to identify the **most efficient jump (Fr₁ ≈ 6 to 8)** that gives maximum energy dissipation with minimum basin length. 5. The curve forms the **basis for selecting appurtenances** (chute blocks, baffle piers, end sills) when tailwater conditions are non-ideal. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the Bélanger equation (conjugate depth relation) for a hydraulic jump in a horizontal, rectangular, prismatic channel. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Water flows at a depth of **0.6 m** and a velocity of **15 m/s** at the toe of a spillway. The discharge is uniformly distributed over a width of **8 m**. Calculate: (i) the Froude number of the incoming flow, (ii) the conjugate (sequent) depth y₂, (iii) the jump height hⱼ, (iv) the energy dissipated in the jump ΔE, (v) the efficiency of the jump η, and (vi) the length of the jump using the USBR formula. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) — Derivation of the Bélanger Equation **Assumptions:** 1. The channel is **horizontal** (no bed slope). 2. The channel is **rectangular and prismatic** (constant width). 3. The **hydrostatic pressure distribution** is valid at sections 1 and 2. 4. **Momentum is conserved** across the jump (friction is negligible over the short length of the jump). 5. **No external forces** (other than hydrostatic pressure) act on the control volume. **Derivation:** Apply the momentum equation per unit width across the control volume between sections 1 and 2: $$\rho g \frac{y_1^2}{2} - \rho g \frac{y_2^2}{2} = \rho q (V_2 - V_1)$$ By continuity, V₁ = q/y₁ and V₂ = q/y₂. Substituting and dividing by ρg: $$\frac{y_1^2 - y_2^2}{2} = \frac{q^2}{g}\left(\frac{1}{y_2} - \frac{1}{y_1}\right)$$ Factoring: $$\frac{(y_1 + y_2)(y_1 - y_2)}{2} = \frac{q^2 (y_1 - y_2)}{g\,y_1\,y_2}$$ Since y₁ ≠ y₂, divide by (y₁ − y₂): $$\frac{y_1 + y_2}{2} = \frac{q^2}{g\,y_1\,y_2}$$ Using Fr₁² = V₁²/(g·y₁) = q²/(g·y₁³), we substitute q²/g = Fr₁²·y₁³: $$1 + \frac{y_2}{y_1} = 2 Fr_1^2\,\frac{y_1}{y_2}$$ Letting r = y₂/y₁ and rearranging into quadratic form: $$r^2 + r - 2 Fr_1^2 = 0$$ Solving for the positive root: $$\boxed{r = \frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)}$$ #### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** y₁ = 0.6 m, V₁ = 15 m/s, b = 8 m. **Step (i): Froude Number** $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g\,y_1}} = \frac{15}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.6}} = \frac{15}{\sqrt{5.886}} = \frac{15}{2.4262} = 6.183$$ Since 4.5 < Fr₁ < 9.0, the jump is a **Steady Jump** (good for energy dissipation). **Step (ii): Sequent Depth y₂** $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = 0.5\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(6.183)^2} - 1\right) = 0.5\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \times 38.229} - 1\right)$$ $$= 0.5\left(\sqrt{1 + 305.832} - 1\right) = 0.5\left(\sqrt{306.832} - 1\right)$$ $$= 0.5(17.5165 - 1) = 0.5 \times 16.5165 = 8.258$$ $$y_2 = 8.258 \times 0.6 = 4.955\,m \approx 4.96\,m$$ **Step (iii): Jump Height hⱼ** $$h_j = y_2 - y_1 = 4.96 - 0.6 = 4.36\,m$$ **Step (iv): Energy Loss ΔE** $$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4\,y_1\,y_2} = \frac{(4.36)^3}{4 \times 0.6 \times 4.96} = \frac{82.880}{11.904} = 6.962\,m \approx 6.96\,m$$ **Step (v): Efficiency η** First compute q = V₁·y₁ = 15 × 0.6 = 9 m³/s/m. $$E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.6 + \frac{225}{19.62} = 0.6 + 11.468 = 12.068\,m$$ $$V_2 = \frac{q}{y_2} = \frac{9}{4.96} = 1.815\,m/s$$ $$E_2 = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} = 4.96 + \frac{(1.815)^2}{19.62} = 4.96 + 0.1679 = 5.128\,m$$ $$\eta = \frac{E_2}{E_1} \times 100 = \frac{5.128}{12.068} \times 100 = 42.49\%$$ **% Energy dissipated = 100 − 42.49 = 57.51%** **Step (vi): Jump Length (USBR)** $$L_j = 6.1 \times y_2 = 6.1 \times 4.96 = 30.26\,m$$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Fr₁ | 6.18 (Steady Jump) | | y₂ | 4.96 m | | hⱼ | 4.36 m | | ΔE | 6.96 m | | η | 42.49% | | Lⱼ | 30.26 m | ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 — Alternative]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **use of a hydraulic jump as an energy dissipator below a spillway**. Discuss the various components of a typical USBR Type II stilling basin. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A spillway has a discharge of **120 m³/s** entering a horizontal rectangular stilling basin of width **10 m** at a depth of **0.8 m** and velocity **15 m/s**. Find: (i) the type of jump, (ii) the conjugate depth y₂, (iii) the power dissipated per metre width, (iv) the length of the jump using the Elevatorski formula. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) **Use of Hydraulic Jump Below a Spillway:** > [!NOTE] > A hydraulic jump is used below a spillway to **dissipate the destructive kinetic energy** of the supercritical flow issuing from the spillway before it enters the downstream river. Without dissipation, the high-velocity jet would scour the riverbed, damage the apron, and threaten the foundation of the dam. **Schematic of a Spillway with Hydraulic Jump Energy Dissipator:** ``` _______________ | | | RESERVOIR | ~~~~~~ HIGH WATER LEVEL |_______________| | | Spillway Crest \ / \ / (Piers, Gates) \/ | | Spillway Face (Chute) | | | | | | y1 (supercritical) | | V1 (high) |____|_________________________ |oooo| STILLING BASIN |oooo| - Chute Blocks |oooo| - Baffle Piers |oooo| - End Sill |~~~~| Hydraulic Jump occurs here | | y2 (subcritical) |____| V2 (low) / \ / \ TAILWATER ~~~~~~~~~~ (RIVER / CANAL) ``` **Components of a USBR Type II Stilling Basin:** 1. **Chute Blocks** (at the basin entrance): Large concrete blocks placed at the toe of the spillway. They: - Diffuse the incoming jet - Lift a portion of the flow, creating artificial roughness - Reduce the effective Froude number, shortening the jump length - Provide a 40–50% reduction in basin length 2. **Baffle Piers / Floor Blocks** (in the middle of the basin): Intermediate blocks that: - Impinge on the high-velocity flow - Generate additional turbulence - Force the jump to occur at a smaller Froude number - Reduce basin length by 30–50% 3. **End Sill / Dentated Sill** (at the basin exit): A raised step at the downstream end that: - Prevents scour at the basin exit - Controls the exit velocity profile - Creates a slight backwater effect to ensure jump confinement 4. **Horizontal Concrete Apron**: The floor of the basin, typically **1–2 m thick**, designed to resist the impact forces and uplift pressure. 5. **Side Walls**: Vertical or sloped retaining walls to contain the raised water surface during the jump (depth y₂ can be 5–10 times y₁). #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** Q = 120 m³/s, b = 10 m, y₁ = 0.8 m, V₁ = 15 m/s. **Step (i): Type of Jump** q = Q/b = 120/10 = 12 m³/s/m $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g\,y_1}} = \frac{15}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.8}} = \frac{15}{\sqrt{7.848}} = \frac{15}{2.8014} = 5.354$$ Since 4.5 < Fr₁ < 9.0 → **Steady Jump.** **Step (ii): Conjugate Depth y₂** $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = 0.5\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(5.354)^2} - 1\right) = 0.5\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \times 28.665} - 1\right)$$ $$= 0.5\left(\sqrt{1 + 229.32} - 1\right) = 0.5\left(\sqrt{230.32} - 1\right) = 0.5(15.176 - 1)$$ $$= 0.5 \times 14.176 = 7.088$$ $$y_2 = 7.088 \times 0.8 = 5.670\,m$$ **Step (iii): Power Dissipated per Metre Width** First, ΔE: $$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4\,y_1\,y_2} = \frac{(5.670 - 0.8)^3}{4 \times 0.8 \times 5.670} = \frac{(4.870)^3}{18.144} = \frac{115.502}{18.144} = 6.366\,m$$ Power dissipated per metre width: $$P = \rho\,g\,q\,\Delta E = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 6.366 = 749{,}255\,W/m \approx 749.26\,kW/m$$ **Step (iv): Length of Jump (Elevatorski Formula)** $$L_j = 6.9 (y_2 - y_1) = 6.9 \times 4.870 = 33.60\,m$$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Jump Type | Steady (Fr₁ = 5.35) | | y₂ | 5.67 m | | Power Dissipated | 749.26 kW/m | | Lⱼ (Elevatorski) | 33.60 m | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the value of g:** Use g = 9.81 m/s² unless stated otherwise. Some students use 9.8 or 10 — this is a 1-mark deduction. > 2. **Taking the wrong root in the quadratic:** r = (−1 + √(1+8Fr₁²))/2 is the **positive** root. Taking the negative root is mathematically wrong and yields y₂ < y₁ (impossible for a jump). > 3. **Unit mismatch in the power calculation:** ρ·g·q·ΔE is in **W/m** when ρ is in kg/m³, g in m/s², q in m²/s, ΔE in m. Convert MW/kW correctly. > 4. **Confusing "energy loss" with "efficiency":** ΔE is the **loss** (high = good), η is the **efficiency** of the jump in retaining energy (high = less dissipation). For a spillway, we WANT a LOW η and HIGH ΔE. > 5. **Using USBR formula outside its range:** Lⱼ = 6.1 y₂ is valid only for 4 ≤ Fr₁ ≤ 13. Outside this range, use Elevatorski or Khurgi. > 6. **Not specifying the type of jump:** Always state whether the jump is Weak/Oscillating/Steady/Strong — this carries marks. > 7. **Skipping the momentum equation derivation:** Even if the question is "design-oriented," the examiner expects you to show the **derivation of Bélanger equation** in sub-part (a) of a 14-mark question. Memorize it. > 8. **Forgetting to check the tailwater condition:** A stilling basin is only as good as its tailwater match. Always compute and compare y₂ with the available tailwater depth y_tw. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3):** > > ✅ **Definition:** Hydraulic jump = supercritical to subcritical transition with energy dissipation. > ✅ **Bélanger Equation:** y₂/y₁ = 0.5(√(1 + 8Fr₁²) − 1) — derived from momentum conservation. > ✅ **Energy Loss:** ΔE = (y₂ − y₁)³ / (4 y₁ y₂) — derived from energy equation + momentum result. > ✅ **Jump Height:** hⱼ = y₂ − y₁. > ✅ **Efficiency:** η = E₂/E₁ × 100%. For Fr₁ = 6 to 8, η ≈ 30 to 45%. > ✅ **Optimal Design Froude Number:** 4.5 ≤ Fr₁ ≤ 9.0 → Steady Jump. > ✅ **USBR Jump Length:** Lⱼ ≈ 6.1 y₂ (for Fr₁ in 4–13). > ✅ **Elevatorski Jump Length:** Lⱼ = 6.9(y₂ − y₁). > ✅ **Power Dissipated per unit width:** P = ρ g q ΔE (in W/m). > ✅ **Tailwater Match:** y_tw must be ≈ y₂ for jump to be confined in basin. > ✅ **Stilling Basin Components:** Chute blocks + Baffle piers + End sill + Apron + Side walls. > ✅ **Submerged Jump:** When y_tw > y₂, jump shifts upstream — not ideal; design for y_tw = y₂. > ✅ **Swept-out Jump:** When y_tw < y₂, jump moves downstream — causes erosion beyond basin. > ✅ **Critical Depth Formula:** y_c = (q²/g)^(1/3) — useful for plotting E-y curves. > ✅ **Rectangular Channel Assumption:** All equations above assume a rectangular cross-section. > ✅ **Real-world structures using this design:** Idukki, Bhakra Nangal, Hirakud, Tehri Dams in India. **End of Topic Notes — PECET523 Module 3 — Uses of Hydraulic Jumps for Energy Dissipation Below Spillways — Jump Height Curve**

topics.title: Tail Water Rating Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tail Water Rating Curve ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tail Water Rating Curve (TWRC)** is a graphical (or functional) relationship between the **stage (depth of flow)** immediately downstream of a hydraulic structure and the corresponding **discharge** passing through the structure. It is a site-specific curve that captures the **backwater influence** of the downstream channel on the hydraulic behaviour of the structure. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$T = f(Q)$$ where: - $T$ = Tail water depth (m) measured downstream of the structure - $Q$ = Discharge (m³/s) passing through the structure > [!NOTE] > The term "**Tail Water**" refers to the water level in the downstream channel immediately below (i.e., at the tail end of) a hydraulic structure such as a weir, barrage, dam spillway, or canal head regulator. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a kitchen sink with the tap fully open. The water rushes out and forms a **frothy, turbulent patch** (the hydraulic jump) before settling into the sink's drain. Now, if you **partially block the drain**, the water in the sink rises, and the frothy patch **moves closer to the tap**. If you **fully block the drain**, the frothy patch gets **drowned** right at the tap. In this analogy: - The **tap** represents the **hydraulic structure** (weir/barrage) - The **drain blockage** represents the **downstream channel's resistance** (which determines the TWRC) - The **frothy patch** is the **hydraulic jump** - The **water level in the sink** is the **tail water depth $T$** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The deeper the downstream channel "blocks" the flow (higher $T$ for the same $Q$), the closer — and more submerged — the hydraulic jump migrates toward the structure.** ### 1.3 Why TWRC is Needed in KTU Syllabus For Module 3 of PECET523, the TWRC forms the **backbone concept** for analyzing: 1. **Submerged vs. Free Flow** over weirs and barrages 2. **Location and type of hydraulic jump** forming at the downstream toe 3. **Afflux (backwater rise)** on the upstream side 4. **Design of energy dissipators** (stilling basins, roller buckets, etc.) ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Tail water depth | $T$ | 0.5 – 10 | **m** | | Discharge | $Q$ | 1 – 10000 | **m³/s** | | Manning's roughness | $n$ | 0.020 – 0.060 | **s/m$^{1/3}$** | | Channel bed slope | $S$ | 0.0001 – 0.01 | **m/m** | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | 9.81 | **m/s²** | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Basis of the Tail Water Rating Curve The downstream water level is **not** determined by the hydraulic structure itself — it is governed by the **downstream channel's conveyance capacity**. For a given discharge $Q$, the tail water depth $T$ is the normal depth (or controlled depth) that the downstream channel can sustain under its specific boundary conditions. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The TWRC is essentially a **Manning's equation curve** in the downstream channel, drawn for a range of discharges. ### 2.2 Manning's Equation for Tail Water Computation For a wide rectangular downstream channel, the discharge is: $$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$ For a rectangular channel of width $B$ and tail water depth $T$: $$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot (BT) \cdot \left(\frac{BT}{B+2T}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$ For a **wide channel** ($B \gg T$, so $R \approx T$): $$Q \approx \frac{1}{n} \cdot B \cdot T^{5/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$ Solving for $T$: $$T = \left(\frac{Q \cdot n}{B \cdot S^{1/2}}\right)^{3/5}$$ ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Behind TWRC Generation - **Step 1:** Identify downstream channel geometry (width $B$, slope $S$, side slopes $z$, Manning's $n$). - **Step 2:** Assume a series of trial tail water depths $T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots$ - **Step 3:** For each $T_i$, compute area $A_i$, wetted perimeter $P_i$, and hydraulic radius $R_i$. - **Step 4:** Apply Manning's equation to get the corresponding $Q_i$. - **Step 5:** Plot $T$ on the **Y-axis** vs. $Q$ on the **X-axis**. - **Step 6:** The smooth curve passing through the points is the **TWRC**. > [!TIP] > In KTU exam problems, the TWRC is often **given graphically** or as a tabulated function, and students must read off $T$ for a specified $Q$ (or vice versa). ### 2.4 Jump Height Curve (JHC) — The Companion Curve The **Jump Height Curve** is the plot of the **conjugate (sequent) depth** $y_2$ of a hydraulic jump as a function of discharge, **for a given supercritical depth $y_1$** at the toe of the structure. For a hydraulic jump in a **rectangular channel**: $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8\,Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$ where the upstream Froude number is: $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g\,y_1}} = \frac{Q}{B\,y_1 \sqrt{g\,y_1}}$$ ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Application | Role of TWRC | |---|---| | **Barrage design** (e.g., across Ganga, Kaveri) | Determines whether gates operate as **free or submerged** orifice | | **Spillway energy dissipation** | Controls whether **hydraulic jump forms inside the stilling basin** or is swept out | | **Canal head regulators** | Governs **backwater effect** on the parent channel | | **Bridge afflux** | TWRC + structure's head-discharge curve = **upstream rise** | | **Drainage sluices** | Prevents **choking** in downstream channels during high floods | ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = f(Q)$ | Tail Water Rating Curve definition | m, m³/s | | 2 | $Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's equation (general) | m³/s | | 3 | $Q = \frac{1}{n} B T \left(\frac{BT}{B+2T}\right)^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's (rectangular) | m³/s | | 4 | $Q = \frac{B}{n} T^{5/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's (wide channel, $B \gg T$) | m³/s | | 5 | $T = \left(\frac{Q n}{B S^{1/2}}\right)^{3/5}$ | Inverse Manning's (wide rectangular) | m | | 6 | $Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}}$ | Froude number at jump toe | dimensionless | | 7 | $\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$ | Bélanger equation (conjugate depth) | dimensionless | | 8 | $E_L = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$ | Energy loss in jump | m | | 9 | $\frac{T}{y_2} \geq 1 \Rightarrow$ drowned | Submergence criterion | dimensionless | | 10 | $V_1 = \frac{Q}{B y_1}$ | Velocity at supercritical depth | m/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > The intersection (or relative position) of the **TWRC** and the **JHC** in the $T$–$Q$ plane is the **single most important graphical tool** for KTU Module 3 problems on hydraulic jump classification. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Conjugate Depth Equation (Bélanger Equation) We start with the **momentum equation** applied across a hydraulic jump in a horizontal, rectangular prismatic channel (neglecting friction over the short jump length): $$\frac{Q^2}{g A_1} + A_1 \bar{y_1} = \frac{Q^2}{g A_2} + A_2 \bar{y_2}$$ For a **rectangular** channel: $A_1 = B y_1$, $A_2 = B y_2$, and $\bar{y_1} = y_1/2$, $\bar{y_2} = y_2/2$. Substituting: $$\frac{Q^2}{g B y_1} + \frac{B y_1^2}{2} = \frac{Q^2}{g B y_2} + \frac{B y_2^2}{2}$$ Divide throughout by $B$: $$\frac{Q^2}{g B^2 y_1} + \frac{y_1^2}{2} = \frac{Q^2}{g B^2 y_2} + \frac{y_2^2}{2}$$ Recognize that $\frac{Q^2}{g B^2} = \frac{(B y_1 V_1)^2}{g B^2} = y_1^2 V_1^2 / g = y_1^2 \cdot Fr_1^2 \cdot y_1 / 1$... let us define the **discharge per unit width** $q = Q/B$. Then: $$\frac{q^2}{g y_1} + \frac{y_1^2}{2} = \frac{q^2}{g y_2} + \frac{y_2^2}{2}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{q^2}{g}\left(\frac{1}{y_1} - \frac{1}{y_2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(y_2^2 - y_1^2)$$ $$\frac{q^2}{g} \cdot \frac{y_2 - y_1}{y_1 y_2} = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)(y_2 + y_1)}{2}$$ Dividing both sides by $(y_2 - y_1)$ (since $y_2 \neq y_1$ for a jump): $$\frac{q^2}{g y_1 y_2} = \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2}$$ Multiply both sides by $2y_2$: $$\frac{2 q^2}{g y_1} = y_2 (y_1 + y_2) = y_1 y_2 + y_2^2$$ But $\frac{q^2}{g y_1^3} = Fr_1^2$, so $\frac{q^2}{g y_1} = Fr_1^2 y_1^2$. Therefore: $$2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2 = y_1 y_2 + y_2^2$$ Rearranging as a quadratic in $y_2$: $$y_2^2 + y_1 y_2 - 2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2 = 0$$ Applying the quadratic formula ($y_2 = \frac{-y_1 + \sqrt{y_1^2 + 8 Fr_1^2 y_1^2}}{2}$): $$y_2 = \frac{y_1}{2}\left(-1 + \sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)}$$ This is the celebrated **Bélanger equation**, fundamental to all KTU JHC problems. --- ### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Aligned with KTU Pattern) > **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** A barrage is proposed across a rectangular downstream channel of width $B = 30$ m, bed slope $S = 1/1500$, and Manning's roughness coefficient $n = 0.030$. For a design discharge $Q = 200$ m³/s, the supercritical depth at the toe of the barrage (just downstream of the gates) is $y_1 = 0.80$ m. > **(a)** Compute the tail water depth $T$ from the Manning's equation. > **(b)** Determine the conjugate depth $y_2$ and classify the jump (free, submerged, or swept-out). > **(c)** Calculate the head loss across the jump. #### Part (a): Tail Water Depth from Manning's Equation We use the rectangular Manning's formula: $$Q = \frac{1}{n} B T \left(\frac{BT}{B+2T}\right)^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$ Substitute known values ($Q = 200$, $B = 30$, $n = 0.030$, $S = 1/1500$): $$200 = \frac{1}{0.030} \cdot 30 \cdot T \cdot \left(\frac{30T}{30+2T}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1500}\right)^{1/2}$$ Compute the constant factors: $$\frac{1}{0.030} = 33.333 \quad ; \quad \sqrt{\frac{1}{1500}} = 0.02582$$ $$200 = 33.333 \times 30 \times 0.02582 \times T \cdot \left(\frac{30T}{30+2T}\right)^{2/3}$$ $$200 = 25.82 \times T \cdot \left(\frac{30T}{30+2T}\right)^{2/3}$$ This is **implicit in $T$** — solve by **trial and error (iteration)**. **Trial 1:** Assume $T = 3.5$ m $$\frac{30 \times 3.5}{30 + 2 \times 3.5} = \frac{105}{37} = 2.838$$ $$(2.838)^{2/3} = e^{(2/3)\ln(2.838)} = e^{(0.6667)(1.043)} = e^{0.696} = 2.005$$ $$Q_{\text{calc}} = 25.82 \times 3.5 \times 2.005 = 181.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{(too low)}$$ **Trial 2:** Assume $T = 3.8$ m $$\frac{30 \times 3.8}{30 + 7.6} = \frac{114}{37.6} = 3.032$$ $$(3.032)^{2/3} = e^{(0.6667)(1.110)} = e^{0.740} = 2.096$$ $$Q_{\text{calc}} = 25.82 \times 3.8 \times 2.096 = 205.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{(slightly high)}$$ **Trial 3:** Assume $T = 3.73$ m $$\frac{30 \times 3.73}{30 + 7.46} = \frac{111.9}{37.46} = 2.987$$ $$(2.987)^{2/3} = e^{(0.6667)(1.094)} = e^{0.730} = 2.075$$ $$Q_{\text{calc}} = 25.82 \times 3.73 \times 2.075 = 199.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \checkmark$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 3.73 \text{ m}}$$ --- #### Part (b): Conjugate Depth and Jump Classification **Step 1 — Compute velocity at supercritical depth:** $$V_1 = \frac{Q}{B \cdot y_1} = \frac{200}{30 \times 0.80} = 8.333 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Froude number $Fr_1$:** $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g \cdot y_1}} = \frac{8.333}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.80}} = \frac{8.333}{\sqrt{7.848}} = \frac{8.333}{2.801}$$ $$Fr_1 = 2.975 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Fr_1^2 = 8.851$$ **Step 3 — Apply Bélanger equation:** $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \times 8.851} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 70.81} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{71.81} - 1\right)$$ $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}(8.474 - 1) = \frac{7.474}{2} = 3.737$$ $$y_2 = 3.737 \times 0.80 = 2.99 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{y_2 \approx 2.99 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 4 — Compare with tail water depth:** $$T = 3.73 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad y_2 = 2.99 \text{ m}$$ $$T > y_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T}{y_2} = \frac{3.73}{2.99} = 1.247$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Since **$T > y_2$**, the tail water is **higher** than the natural conjugate depth. The hydraulic jump is therefore **DROWNED (SUBMERGED)** at the toe of the barrage. The jump is pushed upstream of its natural location, and a portion of its energy is dissipated as **turbulence + submerged jet**. The submergence ratio $S_r = T/y_2 = 1.247$ (about **24.7% submergence**) is a critical parameter for designing the **stilling basin length** and **apron elevation**. --- #### Part (c): Head Loss Across the Jump The energy loss in a hydraulic jump is: $$E_L = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$$ $$E_L = \frac{(2.99 - 0.80)^3}{4 \times 0.80 \times 2.99} = \frac{(2.19)^3}{9.568} = \frac{10.50}{9.568}$$ $$\boxed{E_L = 1.097 \text{ m}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Afflux-Discharge Relationship (Conceptual) When a hydraulic structure is built, the upstream water level rises by an amount $\Delta h$ (the **afflux**). The energy balance across the structure (ignoring losses) is: $$H_{\text{up}} + \frac{V_{\text{up}}^2}{2g} = H_{\text{down}} + \frac{V_{\text{down}}^2}{2g} + h_L$$ The downstream level is read directly from the **TWRC** (i.e., $H_{\text{down}} = T$). The upstream level is unknown, but it must be consistent with the structure's **head-discharge relationship** (e.g., for a weir: $Q = C_d L H^{3/2}$). Iterating on $H_{\text{up}}$ until both equations are satisfied yields the afflux. > [!NOTE] > KTU questions on afflux in Module 3 typically **provide the TWRC graphically** and the structure's discharge equation, asking the student to find the upstream water level for a given $Q$. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TWRC–JHC Interaction ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hydraulic Structure
Weir or Barrage] -->|Releases Discharge Q| B[Toe of Structure
Supercritical Flow y1] B --> C[Stilling Basin or Apron] C --> D{Hydraulic Jump Location} D -->|T greater than y2| E[Drowned Jump
at Toe] D -->|T equals y2| F[Jump Forms
at Toe] D -->|T less than y2| G[Jump Swept
Downstream] H[Downstream Channel
Geometry, Slope, n] --> I[Tail Water Rating Curve] I --> J[Tail Water Depth T] J --> D K[Bélanger Equation
y2 per y1 and Fr1] --> L[Jump Height Curve] L --> M[Conjugate Depth y2] M --> D I -.intersection point.-> L ``` ### 4.2 Plot Schematic — Three Cases of TWRC vs. JHC ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "Case 1: TWRC Above JHC" c1a[TWRC Curve] --- c1b[JHC Curve] c1b -.lies below.-> c1a c1a --> c1r[Result: Drowned Jump at Structure] end subgraph "Case 2: TWRC Below JHC" c2a[TWRC Curve] --- c2b[JHC Curve] c2a -.lies below.-> c2b c2a --> c2r[Result: Jump Swept Far Downstream] end subgraph "Case 3: TWRC Crosses JHC" c3a[TWRC Curve] --- c3b[JHC Curve] c3a -.intersects.-> c3b c3a --> c3r[Result: Jump Forms at Toe of Structure] end ``` ### 4.3 Energy Dissipation System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Upstream Reservoir
High Energy] --> W[Weir or Barrage

Submerged Jumping] Cmp -->|T equals y2| Out2[Perfect Jump
at Toe] Cmp -->|T less than y2| Out3[Swept-Out Jump
Downstream] ``` > [!NOTE] > In KTU board exam questions, the diagram showing the **relative position of the TWRC and JHC** (Case 1, 2, or 3 above) is often asked as a **5-mark sub-question**. The student must label the axes, sketch the two curves, and indicate the jump location clearly. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — 3 Mark Questions #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a Tail Water Rating Curve. What is its significance in the design of hydraulic structures?** **Model Answer:** A Tail Water Rating Curve (TWRC) is a curve showing the relationship between the depth of water (stage) in the downstream channel of a hydraulic structure and the corresponding discharge flowing through it. It is generally obtained by applying **Manning's equation** to the downstream channel for a range of discharges. **Significance:** 1. It determines whether a hydraulic jump will form at the toe of the structure or be swept downstream. 2. It is essential for the design of **energy dissipators** (stilling basins, buckets). 3. It governs the **submerged or free flow** condition over weirs and barrages. 4. It is required to compute **afflux (backwater rise)** on the upstream side. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between a Tail Water Rating Curve (TWRC) and a Jump Height Curve (JHC).** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | TWRC | JHC | |---|---|---| | **Plot** | Tail water depth $T$ vs. Discharge $Q$ | Conjugate depth $y_2$ vs. Discharge $Q$ | | **Origin** | Downstream channel hydraulics (Manning's eq.) | Bélanger momentum equation (for fixed $y_1$) | | **Shape** | Monotonically rising curve | Curved with a minimum near critical depth | | **Use** | Identifies actual downstream water level | Identifies required downstream depth for jump | | **Together** | Their relative position classifies the **hydraulic jump** | | --- ### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) > **Common Problem Stem (5 marks):** *A concrete-lined rectangular channel of width 40 m, bed slope 1 in 2000, and Manning's n = 0.025 forms the downstream of a barrage. The supercritical depth at the toe is $y_1 = 1.0$ m. The design discharge is $Q = 250$ m³/s.* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** **(a) [7 Marks]** Develop the Tail Water Rating Curve equation for the given channel and compute the tail water depth $T$ for $Q = 250$ m³/s. **(b) [7 Marks]** Using the computed $T$, determine the conjugate depth $y_2$, classify the hydraulic jump, and compute the energy loss $E_L$ across it. **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** Using Manning's equation for a rectangular channel: $$Q = \frac{1}{n} B T \left(\frac{BT}{B+2T}\right)^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$ Substituting $Q = 250$, $B = 40$, $n = 0.025$, $S = 1/2000$: $$250 = \frac{1}{0.025} \cdot 40 \cdot T \cdot \left(\frac{40T}{40+2T}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2000}\right)^{1/2}$$ $$250 = 40 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.02236 \cdot T \cdot \left(\frac{40T}{40+2T}\right)^{2/3}$$ $$250 = 35.78 \cdot T \cdot \left(\frac{40T}{40+2T}\right)^{2/3}$$ By **trial and error**: - Try $T = 3.5$ m: $R = 140/47 = 2.979$, $R^{2/3} = 2.073$, $Q = 35.78 \times 3.5 \times 2.073 = 259.6$ (high) - Try $T = 3.4$ m: $R = 136/46.8 = 2.906$, $R^{2/3} = 2.045$, $Q = 35.78 \times 3.4 \times 2.045 = 248.8$ (close) - Try $T = 3.41$ m: $Q \approx 250.0$ ✓ $$\boxed{T \approx 3.41 \text{ m}}$$ **Part (b):** Velocity at toe: $$V_1 = \frac{250}{40 \times 1.0} = 6.25 \text{ m/s}$$ Froude number: $$Fr_1 = \frac{6.25}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.0}} = \frac{6.25}{3.132} = 1.995 \approx 2.0$$ Conjugate depth: $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \times 4.0} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{33} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}(5.745 - 1) = 2.372$$ $$y_2 = 2.372 \times 1.0 = 2.37 \text{ m}$$ **Comparison:** $T = 3.41$ m vs. $y_2 = 2.37$ m Since $T > y_2$, the jump is **DROWNED / SUBMERGED** at the toe. Submergence ratio: $S_r = T/y_2 = 3.41/2.37 = 1.439$ (about 44% submergence) **Energy Loss:** $$E_L = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \frac{(2.37 - 1.00)^3}{4 \times 1.00 \times 2.37} = \frac{(1.37)^3}{9.48} = \frac{2.572}{9.48}$$ $$\boxed{E_L \approx 0.271 \text{ m}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three possible relative positions** of the TWRC and JHC in the $T$–$Q$ plane, and indicate the corresponding jump behaviour in each case. **(b) [7 Marks]** A wide rectangular channel has $S = 1/2500$, $n = 0.028$, and $B = 50$ m. For $Q = 400$ m³/s, compute $T$ using the wide-channel approximation. If the supercritical depth at the structure toe is $y_1 = 1.2$ m and the conjugate depth is found to be $y_2 = 3.85$ m, classify the jump and compute the energy dissipation efficiency $\eta$. **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** The three cases are: **Case 1 — TWRC lies entirely above JHC:** The available tail water depth is always greater than the required conjugate depth. The hydraulic jump is **drowned (submerged)** at the toe of the structure for all discharges. The structure operates under **submerged flow** conditions. This is the **most common case** in low-lying downstream channels. **Case 2 — TWRC lies entirely below JHC:** The tail water is always too low to sustain the jump at the toe. The jump is **swept downstream** of the structure, possibly into an **erodible reach**, causing severe scour. This case requires **artificial energy dissipation** (e.g., longer apron, roller bucket, or impact block). **Case 3 — TWRC intersects JHC:** The two curves cross at a specific discharge $Q^*$. For $Q < Q^*$, the jump is at the toe (free), and for $Q > Q^*$, it is submerged. This is the **ideal design condition** for most barrages, allowing stable jump formation over a range of flows. **Part (b):** For a **wide channel** ($B = 50$ m, expected $T \ll B$): $$T = \left(\frac{Q \cdot n}{B \cdot S^{1/2}}\right)^{3/5}$$ $$T = \left(\frac{400 \times 0.028}{50 \times (1/2500)^{1/2}}\right)^{3/5} = \left(\frac{11.2}{50 \times 0.02}\right)^{3/5} = \left(\frac{11.2}{1.0}\right)^{3/5}$$ $$T = (11.2)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln(11.2)} = e^{0.6 \times 2.416} = e^{1.450} = 4.263 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 4.26 \text{ m}}$$ **Verification of wide-channel assumption:** $T/B = 4.26/50 = 0.085 \ll 1$ ✓ (wide-channel assumption valid) **Jump classification:** $T = 4.26$ m vs. $y_2 = 3.85$ m Since $T > y_2$, the jump is **DROWNED**. Submergence ratio: $S_r = 4.26/3.85 = 1.106$ (about 10.6% submergence — mild) **Energy Dissipation Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{E_L}{E_1} = 1 - \frac{E_2}{E_1}$$ Where $E_1$ and $E_2$ are specific energies before and after the jump. Specific energy before the jump: $$E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 1.2 + \frac{(Q/B/y_1)^2}{2g} = 1.2 + \frac{(400/50/1.2)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$ $$E_1 = 1.2 + \frac{(6.667)^2}{19.62} = 1.2 + \frac{44.44}{19.62} = 1.2 + 2.265 = 3.465 \text{ m}$$ Specific energy after the jump: $$V_2 = \frac{Q}{B y_2} = \frac{400}{50 \times 3.85} = 2.078 \text{ m/s}$$ $$E_2 = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} = 3.85 + \frac{(2.078)^2}{19.62} = 3.85 + \frac{4.318}{19.62} = 3.85 + 0.220 = 4.070 \text{ m}$$ $$\eta = \frac{E_1 - E_2}{E_1} = \frac{3.465 - 4.070}{3.465} = \frac{-0.605}{3.465} = -0.175$$ > [!WARNING] > A **negative efficiency** is a **classic indicator of a drowned jump**! For a true free jump, $E_2 < E_1$ and $\eta > 0$. The negative value confirms that the downstream submergence forces the jump to occur at a **higher specific energy location** (closer to critical), and the **dissipated energy** must instead be computed from the **momentum-derived** formula $E_L = (y_2 - y_1)^3 / (4 y_1 y_2) = (3.85-1.2)^3/(4 \times 1.2 \times 3.85) = (2.65)^3/18.48 = 18.61/18.48 \approx 1.007$ m. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (per KTU Valuation Key):** > 1. **Forgetting to iterate** — Students often substitute assumed $T$ in Manning's and present one trial as final. The examiner expects **at least 2–3 iterations** with explicit calculation. > 2. **Confusing $T$ and $y_2$** — Always clearly label which is the **tail water depth** (from channel) and which is the **conjugate depth** (from Bélanger). Mixing them up leads to wrong jump classification. > 3. **Sign error in $\eta$** — A negative efficiency **does not mean "no dissipation"**. It indicates a drowned jump; use the momentum-based $E_L$ formula instead. > 4. **Skipping the wide-channel check** — Always verify $B/T \geq 5$ (or similar) before applying the simplified $T^{5/3}$ form of Manning's. > 5. **Units inconsistency** — Express slope as a ratio (e.g., 1/1500), not as a percentage, in all KTU solutions. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tail Water Rating Curve** - **Definition:** $T = f(Q)$ curve giving tail water depth vs. discharge, derived from **Manning's equation** of the downstream channel. - **Source curve:** Plotted using downstream channel geometry, slope, and roughness. - **Companion curve:** **Jump Height Curve (JHC)** gives conjugate depth $y_2$ via **Bélanger equation** for a given $y_1$. - **Bélanger's equation:** $\dfrac{y_2}{y_1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$. - **Three cases of TWRC vs. JHC:** - $T > y_2$ → **Drowned/Submerged jump** at the toe. - $T = y_2$ → **Free jump** forms exactly at the toe. - $T < y_2$ → **Swept-out jump**, moving downstream. - **Tail water depth (wide channel):** $T = \left(\dfrac{Q n}{B S^{1/2}}\right)^{3/5}$. - **Energy loss in jump:** $E_L = \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$. - **Froude number:** $Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}}$ (must be $> 1$ for a jump to occur). - **Engineering role:** Governs design of **stilling basins, roller buckets, baffle piers, and apron elevation**. - **KTU Tip:** Always draw the **TWRC and JHC on the same $T$–$Q$ axes** with the **jump location** clearly indicated — examiners award **2–3 easy marks** for a correct sketch. - **Submergence ratio:** $S_r = T/y_2$; values between **1.05 and 1.30** indicate **mild to moderate submergence** — typical in Indian barrage design.

topics.title: Design Features of USBR Stilling Basins, Standing Wave Flume, Parshall Flume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET523 - OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design Features of USBR Stilling Basins, Standing Wave Flume, Parshall Flume # Module 3: Energy Dissipators and Flow Measurement Structures ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 USBR Stilling Basins — Formal Definition A **USBR Stilling Basin** is a specially designed reinforced concrete chamber (basin) constructed at the downstream toe of a hydraulic structure such as a spillway, weir, drop, or chute, whose geometric proportions and internal appurtenances (chute blocks, baffle piers, end sill) are standardized by the **United States Bureau of Reclamation (USBR)** to confine and stabilize the hydraulic jump, thereby dissipating the excess kinetic energy of supercritical flow before it can erode the downstream channel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The USBR classification of stilling basins into Types I, II, III, IV, V, and VI based on the incoming Froude Number (Fr₁) is a high-yield topic. Memorize the Fr-range for each basin type. #### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a fast-flowing mountain stream suddenly entering a flat, wide pool at the bottom of a waterfall. The wild, turbulent water (carrying enough energy to scour the riverbed for meters downstream) is forced to slow down abruptly. Now imagine engineers building a swimming pool with built-in **rocks (chute blocks) at the entrance**, **pillars (baffle blocks) in the middle**, and a **raised lip (end sill) at the exit**. These features act like a "turbulence cage" that breaks the water's energy, converting destructive kinetic energy into safer turbulence and heat. That engineered pool is essentially a **USBR Stilling Basin**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metric:** The USBR design charts correlate the Froude number (Fr₁), the incoming depth (y₁), and the conjugate depth (y₂) to determine basin length, baffle block height, and end sill height. --- ### 1.2 Standing Wave Flume — Formal Definition A **Standing Wave Flume** is a critical-depth flow-measuring device in which the channel constriction forces the flow to pass through critical depth, producing a stationary (standing) wave on the downstream side. Since critical depth is a unique function of discharge for a given channel shape, measurement of the upstream head gives a direct reading of discharge. #### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Picture a wide, slow river meeting a narrow rocky gorge. Just as the water enters the gorge, it speeds up dramatically and forms a single "wave" that seems frozen in place (the standing wave). Because the size of the wave depends only on how much water is coming, you can measure the upstream water level and instantly know the discharge. This is exactly how a **standing wave flume** works in engineered form. > [!NOTE] > The **Parshall Flume** is a refined, calibrated version of the standing wave flume concept with empirical discharge coefficients derived from extensive laboratory testing. --- ### 1.3 Parshall Flume — Formal Definition A **Parshall Flume** is a calibrated open-channel flow-measuring structure consisting of three sections — a **converging (or transition) section**, a **throat (or control section)**, and a **diverging section** — with a horizontal floor in the throat and a downward-sloping floor in the downstream diverging section. It was developed by **Ralph L. Parshall** of Colorado State University in the 1920s and is the most widely used primary device for irrigation and wastewater flow measurement in the world. #### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a Parshall flume as a "speedometer for a river." The flume narrows in the middle (throat), forcing the water to speed up and create a precisely known relationship between upstream depth and flow rate. By simply reading the upstream water level (Ha) on a gauge, the operator can read the discharge from a pre-calibrated table — no current meter, no complex computation, no obstruction of fish migration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Sizes (W = throat width):** Parshall flumes are manufactured in standard throat widths from **1 inch (25 mm) to 50 feet (15.24 m)**, each with its own calibrated rating table. --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Summary | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |----------|--------|----------------------| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** (or 32.2 ft/s²) | | Froude number range for Type II basin | $Fr_1$ | **2.5 ≤ Fr₁ ≤ 4.5** | | Baffle block height (Type II) | $h_b$ | **$y_1$** (≈ equal to incoming depth) | | Chute block height | — | **$y_1$** | | End sill height (Type II) | $h_s$ | **0.2 $y_2$** | | Parshall flume free-flow exponent | $n$ | **≈ 1.522** (US units) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 USBR Stilling Basin Classification Logic The USBR classified stilling basins into **six standard types** based on the **incoming Froude number ($Fr_1$)**, which is a dimensionless number representing the ratio of inertial forces to gravitational forces: $$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g \cdot y_1}}$$ The choice of basin type depends on the energy of the incoming jet, the desired energy dissipation efficiency, and economic considerations. ### 2.1.1 USBR Basin Types at a Glance | Basin Type | Froude Number Range | Key Appurtenances | Typical Application | |------------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Type I** | $1.7 \le Fr_1 < 2.5$ | None (plain basin) | Low-energy, small drops | | **Type II** | $2.5 \le Fr_1 \le 4.5$ | Chute blocks + baffle piers + end sill | Canal falls, small spillways (most common) | | **Type III** | $4.5 < Fr_1 \le 13$ | Larger chute blocks + baffle piers + end sill | Medium spillways | | **Type IV** | $Fr_1 > 13$ and $V_1 > 18$ m/s (60 ft/s) | Impact walls + large baffles + slotted end sill | High dam spillways | | **Type V** | $Fr_1 < 1.7$ | Sloped apron, low tailwater | Canal structures with low Froude numbers | | **Type VI** | High $Fr_1$ + high discharge | Baffle piers + stepped apron | Small diversion dams | > [!IMPORTANT] > The most-examined basin in KTU is **Type II** — students must know its dimensions and appurtenance geometry by heart. ### 2.1.2 USBR Type II Basin — Detailed Design Features The **Type II basin** is the workhorse of USBR design. Its key design features are: **A. Chute Blocks (at basin entrance):** - Height = $y_1$ (the incoming supercritical depth) - Width = $y_1$ at the top, sloped on the upstream face - Spacing = $0.75 \, y_1$ (clear space between blocks) - Number = approximately $W / (1.75 \, y_1)$, where $W$ = basin width - Function: Lift the incoming jet off the floor, creating a distributed jet pattern that spreads energy dissipation throughout the basin depth. **B. Baffle Piers (in middle of basin):** - Height = $y_1$ - Width and spacing each = $0.75 \, y_1$ - Located at a distance of $L_1$ from the basin entrance - Function: Generate intense turbulence to dissipate energy; act as impact surfaces for the high-velocity jet. **C. End Sill (at basin exit):** - Height = $0.2 \, y_2$ (where $y_2$ = conjugate depth of hydraulic jump) - Function: Reduces the residual velocity exiting the basin and controls the tailwater depth. **D. Basin Length ($L$):** - $L = 4.5 \, y_2$ (approximately) > [!NOTE] > **Hydraulic Jump Conjugate Depth Relation (Belanger equation):** > $$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8\,Fr_1^{2}} - 1\right)$$ --- ## 2.2 Standing Wave Flume — Theoretical Basis A standing wave flume operates on the principle of **critical flow**. By forcing flow through critical depth at a control section, the upstream head becomes a unique function of discharge. ### 2.2.1 Governing Equations **Specific Energy at the control section:** $$E_c = y_c + \frac{V_c^{2}}{2g}$$ **Critical depth for a rectangular channel:** $$y_c = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{Q^{2}}{g\,W^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$$ where $q = Q/W$ is the discharge per unit width and $W$ is the channel/control-section width. **Minimum specific energy at critical depth:** $$E_c = \frac{3}{2}\,y_c$$ **Discharge through a rectangular control section:** $$Q = W \cdot y_c \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot y_c} = W \cdot \sqrt{g} \cdot y_c^{3/2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does the standing wave form?** When the flume narrows, the kinetic energy increases. To conserve the specific energy (under ideal conditions), the depth drops. As the flow exits the constriction, the channel widens again, forcing the flow to convert back to subcritical — but this transition must pass through a hydraulic jump, which appears as a stationary (standing) wave on the downstream side. --- ## 2.3 Parshall Flume — Complete Theoretical Framework ### 2.3.1 Structural Components A standard Parshall flume consists of: 1. **Inlet section (converging):** Floor is level; walls converge from a width $W_a$ at the entrance to the throat width $W$. 2. **Throat (control section):** Floor is level (horizontal); walls are parallel at width $W$. 3. **Outlet section (diverging):** Floor slopes **downward** with a drop equal to: - **75 mm (3 inches)** for flumes of throat width $W \le 1.0$ m (3 ft) - **150 mm (6 inches)** for flumes of throat width $W > 1.0$ m - Walls diverge from $W$ to $W_b$ at the exit. > [!NOTE] > The downward-sloping floor of the diverging section is the **signature feature** of the Parshall flume — it is what distinguishes it from other standing-wave flumes and is the reason submerged-flow corrections are smaller and more predictable. ### 2.3.2 Free-Flow vs. Submerged Flow | Flow Regime | Condition | Equation | |-------------|-----------|----------| | **Free Flow** | $H_b / H_a \le 0.60$ (small flumes) or $H_b / H_a \le 0.70$ (large flumes) | $Q = C \cdot W \cdot H_a^{n}$ | | **Submerged Flow** | $H_b / H_a$ exceeds the above | $Q = Q_{free} \cdot \text{correction factor}$ | where: - $H_a$ = upstream depth (measured at the ha gauge, located 2/3 of the way up the converging section) - $H_b$ = downstream depth (measured at the hb gauge, located in the throat) - $C, n$ = empirically determined coefficients (depend on $W$ and unit system) - $W$ = throat width ### 2.3.3 Free-Flow Discharge Equation In **US customary units** (Q in cfs, W and Ha in feet): $$Q = C \cdot W \cdot H_a^{1.522}$$ In **SI units** (Q in m³/s, W and Ha in meters): $$Q = K \cdot W \cdot H_a^{1.55}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Note:** For KTU problems, you will typically be given the rating table or the values of $C$ and $n$ for a specific throat width. You do **not** need to memorize the rating tables, but you **must** know the form of the equation and how to apply the submergence correction. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This consolidated table is the **single most important reference** for your preparation.
